translation: [This is a declaration of] disassociation, from Allah and His Messenger, to those with whom you had made a treaty among the polytheists
commentary:
بَرَآءَةٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦٓ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ عَـٰهَدتُّم مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ

This ayah begins with a powerful and unprecedented declaration: بَرَاءَةٌ - a complete dissolution of all obligations. The opening of this surah is unique in the entire Quran. Allah ﷻ addresses the believers first, speaking about the disbelievers in the third person rather than addressing them directly. This itself is a form of distancing and withdrawal of honor.

The word بَرَاءَة carries profound meaning in Arabic. From the root ب-ر-أ, it means to be completely free from something, particularly from sickness or disease. The Arabs would say بَرِئْتُ مِنَ الْمَرَضِ - "I have been alleviated from a sickness." Allah ﷻ is declaring that the association with these treaty-breaking mushrikeen has become like a disease, and now He is cleansing the Muslim community from this affliction.

"A declaration of complete disassociation from Allah and His Messenger" - notice the gravity of this statement. It's not just the Prophet ﷺ declaring this; it's from Allah ﷻ Himself and His Messenger ﷺ together. This mirrors how Surah Al-Anfal began when there was internal dispute among Muslims about the spoils of war. Allah ﷻ simply stated: "The spoils belong to Allah and His Messenger" - no further explanation, no negotiation. The matter was closed. Similarly here, the declaration is final and absolute.

The ayah specifies "those with whom you made treaties from among the mushrikeen." These were the various Arab tribes around Makkah who had entered into different types of agreements with the Muslims. Some treaties were open-ended with no specific termination date, while others had fixed terms - six months, one year, two years. The open-ended treaties are now being given a definitive deadline.

translation: So travel freely, [O disbelievers], throughout the land [during] four months but know that you cannot cause failure to Allah and that Allah will disgrace the disbelievers
commentary:
فَسِيحُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَرْبَعَةَ أَشْهُرٍ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّكُمْ غَيْرُ مُعْجِزِى ٱللَّهِ ۙ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مُخْزِى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

The word فَسِيحُوا comes from سَاحَ, which describes water freely trickling in every direction when poured on the ground. It conveys the image of moving openly and without restriction. When someone is described as walking in a ساحة (courtyard), they're walking freely in an open space. Allah ﷻ is essentially saying: "Go take your final road trips. Travel freely in the land - but you have four months."

This is an extraordinary moment in prophetic history. To understand its significance, we must recognize what has never happened before. When Prophet Nuh (AS) called his people and the flood came, did it pause? Did Nuh (AS) say, "Wait, hold on - you have four more months to decide"? No. When Prophet Lut (AS) warned his people and the punishment rained from the sky, was there a pause button? When any previous prophet's warning materialized into actual punishment, there was no stopping it, no negotiation, no extension.

But here, the punishment has already arrived. The Sahabah themselves are the executors of Allah's punishment on the disbelievers. The difference is profound: in previous nations, disbelievers could dismiss warnings because they couldn't see the punishment. But now? The mushrikeen can see the Muslim army. The threat is tangible, real, standing right before them. The sword is at their necks - and then Allah ﷻ says, "Four more months." This unprecedented mercy has never been granted to any previous nation.

Think about this moment: the very people who used to throw animal remains on the Prophet ﷺ while he prostrated at the Kaaba, who beat him in those streets, who laughed at the early Muslims - these same people now stand powerless. The idols they worshipped at the Kaaba have been destroyed. People are walking over the broken pieces. And the announcement is being made by the same community they once mocked and tortured.

The ayah gives them three implicit options during these four months: First, accept Islam and save yourselves. Second, prepare your weapons because war is coming. Third, leave - migrate to Yemen, Persia, anywhere outside Muslim territory, because you cannot remain here.

وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّكُمْ غَيْرُ مُعْجِزِى ٱللَّهِ - "Know with certainty that you cannot escape Allah or render Him incapable." The term مُعْجِز means to overpower or incapacitate someone. You cannot overpower Allah's forces - meaning the Muslim army that Allah ﷻ has chosen and strengthened.

وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مُخْزِى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ - "And know that Allah will disgrace the disbelievers." The word مُخْزِي comes from خِزْي, meaning humiliation and disgrace. We've encountered another word for humiliation earlier in the Quran - صَغَار, which means to be made small, to be reduced from a position of greatness. Both are happening here. The Quraysh, who considered themselves the masters of that entire region, who controlled the sacred city and its economy, now stand completely powerless. Can you imagine a greater humiliation?

translation: And [it is] an announcement from Allah and His Messenger to the people on the day of the greater pilgrimage that Allah is disassociated from the disbelievers, and [so is] His Messenger. So if you repent, that is best for you; but if you turn away - then know that you will not cause failure to Allah. And give tidings to those who disbelieve of a painful punishment
commentary:
وَأَذَٰنٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦٓ إِلَى ٱلنَّاسِ يَوْمَ ٱلْحَجِّ ٱلْأَكْبَرِ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ بَرِىٓءٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ ۙ وَرَسُولُهُۥ ۚ فَإِن تُبْتُمْ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ وَإِن تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّكُمْ غَيْرُ مُعْجِزِى ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ

وَأَذَٰنٌ - "And a proclamation." This is a public announcement of the highest order, coming from Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ, directed إِلَى ٱلنَّاسِ (to all people) on يَوْمَ ٱلْحَجِّ ٱلْأَكْبَرِ (the Day of the Greater Hajj).

The term "Greater Hajj" requires clarification. In Arab tradition, there was a distinction between major and minor pilgrimages. Some scholars have suggested it refers to when Hajj falls on a Friday, but this isn't accurate. The "Greater Hajj" simply refers to the major pilgrimage season as opposed to the smaller 'Umrah.

An important historical note: the Arabs had four sacred months during which fighting was prohibited. However, when warfare became strategically necessary during these months, some tribes would manipulate the calendar, shifting the sacred months to different times. This practice was called النَّسِيء, which we'll encounter later in this surah. Over years of manipulation, the calendar had become completely misaligned from its original state. By Allah's decree, in the very year this announcement was made, the calendar had naturally realigned to its proper sequence - back to how it was when Allah ﷻ first created the heavens and the earth. The Prophet ﷺ himself declared this restoration.

أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ بَرِىٓءٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ وَرَسُولُهُۥ - "That Allah is free from obligation to the mushrikeen, and so is His Messenger." This statement stands in stark contrast to the entire Makkan period of revelation. For over a decade, the dominant message to the Prophet ﷺ was patience: فَٱصْبِرْ (be patient with what they say), speak to them kindly, remind them gently. When Allah ﷻ spoke to Prophet Musa (AS) about approaching Fir'awn, He said: فَقُولَا لَهُ قَوْلًا لَّيِّنًا (speak to him with gentle words). The entire Makkan Quran emphasized mercy, patience and gentle calling. Now, that chapter has closed. Allah ﷻ declares He has nothing to do with them, and neither does His Messenger ﷺ.

There's a famous incident related to this ayah about the critical importance of proper Quranic recitation. A non-Arab student learning Quran accidentally recited وَرَسُولُهُۥ incorrectly, changing the grammatical case in a way that made it sound like "and His Messenger is free from Allah" - an act of kufr. When Umar (RA) heard this, he immediately declared that no one should teach Quran except someone who properly knows the Arabic language. One misplaced vowel completely reversed the meaning and turned the statement into disbelief.

فَإِن تُبْتُمْ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ - "If you repent, it is better for you." Even after everything, even after all their crimes and treaty violations, the door of repentance remains open. This is the mercy of Allah ﷻ extending through His Messenger ﷺ.

وَإِن تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّكُمْ غَيْرُ مُعْجِزِى ٱللَّهِ - "But if you turn away, know that you cannot escape Allah." The warning is repeated for emphasis: you cannot overpower the forces Allah ﷻ has chosen.

وَبَشِّرِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ - "Give glad tidings to those who disbelieve of a painful punishment." This is profound sarcasm. The word بَشِّرْ means to give good news, glad tidings. What's the "good news" for those who persist in disbelief? Painful punishment awaits. The immediate four-month deadline and whatever consequences follow in this world are nothing compared to what awaits in the Hereafter.

translation: Excepted are those with whom you made a treaty among the polytheists and then they have not been deficient toward you in anything or supported anyone against you; so complete for them their treaty until their term [has ended]. Indeed, Allah loves the righteous [who fear Him]
commentary:
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ عَـٰهَدتُّم مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَنقُصُوكُمْ شَيْـًٔا وَلَمْ يُظَـٰهِرُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ أَحَدًا فَأَتِمُّوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِمْ عَهْدَهُمْ إِلَىٰ مُدَّتِهِمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ

Now comes the exception to the categorical declaration. إِلَّا (except) - Allah ﷻ distinguishes between different types of mushrikeen based on their conduct.

ٱلَّذِينَ عَـٰهَدتُّم مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ - "Those with whom you made treaties from among the mushrikeen" - but not just any treaties. These are specifically the treaties with fixed terms that haven't expired yet.

ثُمَّ لَمْ يَنقُصُوكُمْ شَيْـًٔا - "Then they have not been deficient toward you in anything." The word يَنقُصُوكُمْ means to fall short, to be deficient in fulfilling obligations. These tribes honored every aspect of their agreements. They didn't violate a single term.

وَلَمْ يُظَـٰهِرُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ أَحَدًا - "And they have not supported anyone against you." The word يُظَاهِرُوا comes from ظَهَرَ, meaning to back someone up, to support them. This addresses both direct and indirect betrayal. Not only did these tribes refrain from fighting the Muslims directly, but they also didn't engage in covert operations, espionage, or secretly backing other enemies against the believers. They maintained complete neutrality and honored their commitments.

فَأَتِمُّوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِمْ عَهْدَهُمْ إِلَىٰ مُدَّتِهِمْ - "So fulfill their treaty with them until their term." Allah ﷻ commands the Muslims: don't break their treaty just because you now have power. If they had a two-year agreement and six months remain, honor those six months. If they had a one-year contract and it hasn't expired, fulfill it completely. This is Islam's commitment to justice even in the midst of conflict.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ - "Indeed, Allah loves those who have taqwa." This statement appears in various contexts throughout the Quran, and each context reveals different dimensions of taqwa. In Surah Al-Anfal, during battle when Muslims were afraid of the enemy, Allah ﷻ said: have taqwa of Me, not them. Fear losing to Me more than fearing them. That was taqwa in a state of weakness and fear.

Here, it's the opposite situation. The Muslims are now in a position of overwhelming power. The enemy is afraid of them. In this context, إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ means: even when you have power, have taqwa of Allah. When you're strong and could easily crush your opponents, when rage could drive you to cross boundaries, when unchecked power could lead you to oppression - that's precisely when you need taqwa most. Without it, you will transgress the limits Allah ﷻ has set. Power without taqwa leads to injustice, but power with taqwa leads to justice and mercy.

translation: And when the sacred months have passed, then kill the polytheists wherever you find them and capture them and besiege them and sit in wait for them at every place of ambush. But if they should repent, establish prayer, and give zakah, let them [go] on their way. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
فَإِذَا ٱنسَلَخَ ٱلْأَشْهُرُ ٱلْحُرُمُ فَٱقْتُلُوا۟ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدتُّمُوهُمْ وَخُذُوهُمْ وَٱحْصُرُوهُمْ وَٱقْعُدُوا۟ لَهُمْ كُلَّ مَرْصَدٍ ۚ فَإِن تَابُوا۟ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَوُا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ فَخَلُّوا۟ سَبِيلَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

فَإِذَا ٱنسَلَخَ ٱلْأَشْهُرُ ٱلْحُرُمُ - "When the sacred months have passed" or more literally, "when they have slipped away." The word ٱنسَلَخَ suggests something peeling away or slipping off quickly, like skin shedding. The message: these four months will pass in no time. Don't think it's a long period - it will be over before you know it.

This announcement is being made at Hajj, in the most public gathering possible. Everyone can hear it: فَٱقْتُلُوا۟ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدتُّمُوهُمْ - "Then kill the mushrikeen wherever you find them." This is the command specifically for those treaty-breakers who violated their agreements, who supported enemies against the Muslims, who engaged in treachery.

وَخُذُوهُمْ - "Seize them" or "capture them." They're going to try to escape, to flee Makkah.

وَٱحْصُرُوهُمْ - "Besiege them," surround them so they cannot get away.

وَٱقْعُدُوا۟ لَهُمْ كُلَّ مَرْصَدٍ - "And sit in wait for them at every place of ambush." The word مَرْصَد comes from رَصَد, referring to a guard post or checkpoint. Picture security guards standing at checkpoints for long hours. Allah ﷻ is commanding: block every escape route from Makkah. Set up ambush points on every road, every path they might use to flee. Wait for them with patience and vigilance.

This is extremely tough language, but we must understand the context. This is divine punishment being executed on earth for treaty violation, for decades of persecution, for attempting to exterminate the Muslim community. This is justice being established after years of oppression.

فَإِن تَابُوا۟ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَوُا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ فَخَلُّوا۟ سَبِيلَهُمْ - "But if they repent, establish prayer and give zakah, then release them."

Notice what Allah ﷻ mentions: prayer and zakah. Why zakah specifically? Because Makkah's biggest problem with Islam wasn't just theological - it was economic. Makkah was a gold mine. Pilgrims from all over Arabia came year-round, spending money, enriching the Quraysh. The idols at the Kaaba were the foundation of this economy.

Now Islam has destroyed the idols. The entire economic system has collapsed. And whatever wealth the Makkans do manage to earn must be purified, and a portion must be given away as zakah. Allah ﷻ is striking directly at the economic motives that drove Quraysh opposition. One of their most devastating realizations: "We're not going to make money anymore. You've completely destroyed our city's economy."

Imagine if a law passed in Las Vegas banning all casinos and gambling - the absolute economic foundation of that city. The protests, the resistance, the desperation to preserve their livelihood. That's what the Quraysh faced. Their economic empire built on shirk was being dismantled.

فَخَلُّوا۟ سَبِيلَهُمْ - "Then leave their way clear." The word خَلُّوا comes from خَلَا, meaning to leave someone completely alone, to let them be, to go your separate ways. Even more literally, it can mean to be alone with someone in peaceful terms. So even if you were waiting at the checkpoint with your sword drawn, and they proclaim Islam and give zakah - let them go. Leave them in peace.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ - "Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful." After all that severity, after all those military commands, Allah ﷻ reminds us of His fundamental nature: He is always ready to forgive, always merciful to those who turn back to Him.

translation: And if any one of the polytheists seeks your protection, then grant him protection so that he may hear the words of Allah. Then deliver him to his place of safety. That is because they are a people who do not know
commentary:
وَإِنْ أَحَدٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ ٱسْتَجَارَكَ فَأَجِرْهُ حَتَّىٰ يَسْمَعَ كَلَـٰمَ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ أَبْلِغْهُ مَأْمَنَهُۥ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَعْلَمُونَ

وَإِنْ أَحَدٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ ٱسْتَجَارَكَ - "And if anyone from the mushrikeen seeks your protection." The word ٱسْتَجَارَكَ comes from جَار, which means neighbor, but also means refuge. Your neighbor is supposed to be your refuge, someone who protects you against outside threats. The verb اسْتَجَارَ means to ask for refuge, specifically from your neighbor against an external enemy.

There's something profound here. Normally, you seek your neighbor's help against someone else who threatens you. But in this situation, who is the threat? The Prophet ﷺ and the Muslims. And who was their neighbor? The Prophet ﷺ. So they're going to the very person they fear to ask for protection from him. This only makes sense because they know the character of Muhammad ﷺ. Even after everything - all the persecution, all the warfare, all the treaties they broke - if there's any hope for mercy, it will come from him.

Now, how could this scenario even happen? After 20 years of revelation, after countless battles, after the conquest of Makkah, after this public announcement at Hajj - how could there be someone who genuinely doesn't know what Islam is? It seems impossible. Yet Allah ﷻ acknowledges this possibility.

فَأَجِرْهُ - "Then grant him protection." Don't question his sincerity. Don't interrogate him. Grant him refuge.

حَتَّىٰ يَسْمَعَ كَلَـٰمَ ٱللَّهِ - "Until he hears the Word of Allah." Give him time to listen to the Quran. Let him hear the recitation. Allow him to sit in gatherings where the religion is being explained. Don't hover over him with a sword demanding immediate conversion. That wouldn't produce genuine faith.

ثُمَّ أَبْلِغْهُ مَأْمَنَهُۥ - "Then escort him to his place of safety." The word أَبْلِغْهُ means to make someone reach their destination, to ensure they get there. And مَأْمَنَهُۥ comes from أَمِنَ (to be safe), so مَأْمَن is a place of safety. 

But whose definition of safety? Allah ﷻ doesn't say "take him to where you think is safe." He says مَأْمَنَهُۥ - HIS place of safety. Where he feels secure, where he's comfortable. Take him there and leave him alone. Let him think about what he's heard without any pressure, without Muslims standing guard, without anyone pressuring him. He has four months. He's heard the message. Now let him process it in peace and make his own decision.

This is mercy beyond measure. This is an unprecedented level of compassion and respect for human agency even in the midst of conflict.

ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَعْلَمُونَ - "That is because they are a people who do not know." Allah ﷻ explains the wisdom: they genuinely have no idea what has been happening. They were completely oblivious to the message, to the struggles, to the transformation that occurred.

Normally, ignorance is not considered innocence, especially after 20 years of prophethood. But here, Allah ﷻ makes an exception. He acknowledges that some people truly may have been unaware, and they deserve the chance to learn and understand before any consequences befall them. This is the justice and mercy of Allah ﷻ perfectly balanced in His legislation.

translation: How can there be for the polytheists a treaty in the sight of Allah and with His Messenger, except for those with whom you made a treaty at al-Masjid al-Haram? So as long as they are upright toward you, be upright toward them. Indeed, Allah loves the righteous [who fear Him]
commentary:
كَيْفَ يَكُونُ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ عَهْدٌ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَعِندَ رَسُولِهِۦٓ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ عَـٰهَدتُّمْ عِندَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ۖ فَمَا ٱسْتَقَـٰمُوا۟ لَكُمْ فَٱسْتَقِيمُوا۟ لَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ

This ayah marks the beginning of two passages that prepare us for understanding the conquest of Makkah. Allah ﷻ asks a rhetorical question: How can there be any contract or agreement in favor of the people of shirk with Allah ﷻ and with His Messenger ﷺ? The question itself suggests the absurdity of maintaining treaties with those who have consistently violated them.

However, there is an exception: إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ عَـٰهَدتُّمْ عِندَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ—except for those with whom you made a treaty right at Al-Masjid Al-Haram, referring to the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah. The principle is then established: فَمَا ٱسْتَقَـٰمُوا۟ لَكُمْ فَٱسْتَقِيمُوا۟ لَهُمْ—so long as they uphold their end of the agreement for you, you must stand by it for them. This is not merely a political calculation but an act of taqwa, as Allah ﷻ concludes: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ—certainly Allah loves those who have taqwa. Standing by your treaties is itself an expression of consciousness of Allah ﷻ.

translation: How [can there be a treaty] while, if they gain dominance over you, they do not observe concerning you any pact of kinship or covenant of protection? They satisfy you with their mouths, but their hearts refuse [compliance], and most of them are defiantly disobedient
commentary:
كَيْفَ وَإِن يَظْهَرُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ لَا يَرْقُبُوا۟ فِيكُمْ إِلًّا وَلَا ذِمَّةً ۚ يُرْضُونَكُم بِأَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَتَأْبَىٰ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ

Allah ﷻ now provides the reasoning behind the questioning in the previous ayah: How can we respect these treaties when, if these people had the opportunity to gain the upper hand against you, they wouldn't show any regard for the agreement?

The word إِلًّا in this context carries a deep meaning. It literally refers to the back of the neck—the part a shepherd watches carefully to control his animal. Figuratively, it means to have courtesy, to watch over your agreements carefully, to respect mutual understandings. The word ذِمَّة refers to the promises made to protect someone, the obligation to honor commitments.

Allah ﷻ is saying these people would observe neither courtesy nor their protective obligations. يُرْضُونَكُم بِأَفْوَٰهِهِمْ—they please you with their mouths. This refers to when Abu Sufyan (RA) came to negotiate, trying to convince the Prophet ﷺ not to advance on Makkah. But وَتَأْبَىٰ قُلُوبُهُمْ—their hearts have refused, turned away. It hurt them deeply to have to come and negotiate. Allah ﷻ knows the true state of their hearts even when their words sound conciliatory.

translation: They have exchanged the signs of Allah for a small price and averted [people] from His way. Indeed, it was evil that they were doing
commentary:
ٱشْتَرَوْا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا فَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ سَآءَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

The crimes of the Quraysh leaders are enumerated. They sold off the signs of Allah ﷻ—both the miraculous revelations and the clear proofs—for a minuscule price. They traded away the truth to hold onto their petty political power, a transaction they had already lost. 

فَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦٓ—after stopping themselves from Allah's path, they actively prevented others from it. The phrase إِنَّهُمْ سَآءَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ emphasizes how terrible their actions truly were. For certain, how horrible is what they've been engaged in.

translation: They do not observe toward a believer any pact of kinship or covenant of protection. And it is they who are the transgressors
commentary:
لَا يَرْقُبُونَ فِى مُؤْمِنٍ إِلًّا وَلَا ذِمَّةً ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُعْتَدُونَ

This ayah generalizes the principle: it wouldn't matter which believer they dealt with, they would show no courtesy or regard. Whether concerning the natural expectations in close relationships (إِلًّا) or formal agreements to protect (ذِمَّةً), they would honor neither.

وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُعْتَدُونَ—and they are the ones who are truly crossing the line. They are the aggressors, the ones who violated the treaty. This designation is crucial because it establishes that any subsequent action taken against them is not aggression but a response to their transgressions.

translation: But if they repent, establish prayer, and give zakah, then they are your brothers in religion; and We detail the verses for a people who know
commentary:
فَإِن تَابُوا۟ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَوُا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ فَإِخْوَٰنُكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ ۗ وَنُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ

After highlighting all their crimes, Allah ﷻ still leaves the door open. Why should the believers give them another opportunity when they've been choking away every benefit until now? Why allow them to regroup? The answer lies in the mercy of this religion.

فَإِن تَابُوا۟—if they were to repent, وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَوُا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ—and establish the prayer and give the zakat, فَإِخْوَٰنُكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ—then they become your brothers in religion. All bygones become bygones. You can start fresh with them.

وَنُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ—We explain the miraculous signs for a nation that wants to know. If they haven't been paying attention to the Quran before, Allah ﷻ will reveal more lessons for them so they're ready to listen. The door to education and transformation remains open for those who genuinely seek knowledge.

translation: And if they break their oaths after their treaty and defame your religion, then fight the leaders of disbelief, for indeed, there are no oaths [sacred] to them; [fight them that] they might cease
commentary:
وَإِن نَّكَثُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَهُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ عَهْدِهِمْ وَطَعَنُوا۟ فِى دِينِكُمْ فَقَـٰتِلُوٓا۟ أَئِمَّةَ ٱلْكُفْرِ ۙ إِنَّهُمْ لَآ أَيْمَـٰنَ لَهُمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَنتَهُونَ

The Quran uses three different words for violating treaties: nakatha, nakaba, and naqada. Nakatha is the lightest of these violations—the minimal breach of a treaty. Even if they violate their oaths in the slightest way مِّنۢ بَعْدِ عَهْدِهِمْ—after they've made their promise, action becomes necessary.

The word طَعَنُوا۟ comes from tana, which means to take something sharp and stab or poke someone with it. Figuratively, it refers to saying hurtful things that really bite, words that wound deeply. They've been making hurtful statements about your religion all this time.

فَقَـٰتِلُوٓا۟ أَئِمَّةَ ٱلْكُفْرِ—then fight the imams of disbelief. Allah ﷻ doesn't say "fight the mushrikeen" but specifically "the imams of kufr." The mission of the Prophet ﷺ was to eliminate shirk not just from Makkah but from the Arabian lands, and the center of that disbelief, the leaders of it, were in Makkah. Now there was an opportunity to go after the head of the serpent.

Before completing the sentence, Allah ﷻ interjects: إِنَّهُمْ لَآ أَيْمَـٰنَ لَهُمْ—these people have no oaths whatsoever. Their word means nothing. It's completely empty. Then He completes the thought: لَعَلَّهُمْ يَنتَهُونَ—so that they will stop. The implication is clear: this is the only way they'll stop. Whatever they say with their mouths carries no weight.

translation: Would you not fight a people who broke their oaths and determined to expel the Messenger, and they had begun [the attack upon] you the first time? Do you fear them? But Allah has more right that you should fear Him, if you are [truly] believers
commentary:
أَلَا تُقَـٰتِلُونَ قَوْمًا نَّكَثُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَهُمْ وَهَمُّوا۟ بِإِخْرَاجِ ٱلرَّسُولِ وَهُم بَدَءُوكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ ۚ أَتَخْشَوْنَهُمْ ۚ فَٱللَّهُ أَحَقُّ أَن تَخْشَوْهُ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

Allah ﷻ now directly addresses the believers with a powerful rhetorical question: Aren't you going to fight a nation that violated their treaty? وَهَمُّوا۟ بِإِخْرَاجِ ٱلرَّسُولِ—they wanted so badly to expel the Messenger ﷺ. Have you forgotten what they did?

وَهُم بَدَءُوكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ—this phrase, "they started against you the first time," is significant. It will appear again in this surah, establishing a pattern of Quraysh aggression. They initiated hostilities; the believers were always responding.

أَتَخْشَوْنَهُمْ—are you afraid of them? This question cuts to the heart of iman. فَٱللَّهُ أَحَقُّ أَن تَخْشَوْهُ—Allah is more deserving that you should fear Him. إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ—if you are indeed people of faith. The connection between true faith and proper fear is made explicit. If your fear of the enemy outweighs your consciousness of Allah ﷻ, then you need to examine your iman.

translation: Fight them; Allah will punish them by your hands and will disgrace them and give you victory over them and satisfy the breasts of a believing people
commentary:
قَـٰتِلُوهُمْ يُعَذِّبْهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِأَيْدِيكُمْ وَيُخْزِهِمْ وَيَنصُرْكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَيَشْفِ صُدُورَ قَوْمٍ مُّؤْمِنِينَ

This ayah deserves special attention. قَـٰتِلُوهُمْ يُعَذِّبْهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِأَيْدِيكُمْ—fight them, and Allah will punish them by your hands. You, the Sahabah (RA), will be divine punishment. You are not like the flood of Nuh (AS) or punishment from the sky; you are the earthly instrument of Allah's justice.

This is why these ayahs are so particular to this historical moment, why it's so difficult to generalize them to our foreign policy or to other nations. Allah ﷻ specifically made a list of crimes committed by the Quraysh: they wanted to expel the Messenger ﷺ, they violated the treaty made with him, they mocked your religion before the Messenger ﷺ. Having committed all these specific crimes, they became worthy of this particular punishment.

وَيُخْزِهِمْ—He will humiliate them. وَيَنصُرْكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ—He will grant you victory over them. وَيَشْفِ صُدُورَ قَوْمٍ مُّؤْمِنِينَ—and He will heal the chests of a believing people. The word صُدُورَ (chests) is used figuratively for feelings and sentiments. Allah ﷻ will cure the emotional wounds of the believers.

Which believers specifically? Those who were weakened in the earth, who couldn't make hijrah, who were stuck in Makkah—some Sahabah (RA) who stayed behind, elderly people, weak individuals, children. They remained trapped in Makkah, taking abuse. When the Quraysh returned from battles against the Muslims, do you think they became kinder to the Muslims living among them? These believers endured torture for years. Now, finally, when they would see justice served, their hearts would find relief. Justice wasn't merely political; it had a healing dimension for those who had suffered.

translation: And remove the fury in the believers' hearts. And Allah turns in forgiveness to whom He wills; and Allah is Knowing and Wise
commentary:
وَيُذْهِبْ غَيْظَ قُلُوبِهِمْ ۗ وَيَتُوبُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ

وَيُذْهِبْ غَيْظَ قُلُوبِهِمْ—Allah ﷻ will remove the deeply rooted frustration from their hearts. The word غَيْظَ refers to a frustration that has settled deep within, anger that has been suppressed for a long time. This conquest would finally release that pent-up pain.

وَيَتُوبُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ—and additionally, Allah will accept the repentance of whomever He wills. Even now, the door remains open. Consider Abu Sufyan (RA)—what was his eventual fate? He became Muslim. The possibility of transformation was never completely closed.

وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ—Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise. He knows who is sincere in their potential repentance, and His decisions are based on perfect wisdom.

translation: Do you think that you will be left [as you are] while Allah has not yet made evident those among you who strive [for His cause] and do not take other than Allah, His Messenger and the believers as intimates? And Allah is Acquainted with what you do
commentary:
أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَن تُتْرَكُوا۟ وَلَمَّا يَعْلَمِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ جَـٰهَدُوا۟ مِنكُمْ وَلَمْ يَتَّخِذُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا رَسُولِهِۦ وَلَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلِيجَةً ۚ وَٱللَّهُ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ

This type of ayah appears multiple times in the Quran, each addressing a different test. In Surah Al-Baqarah, before the Battle of Badr, Allah ﷻ asks if people think they'll enter Paradise without facing what previous believers faced. In Surah Al-Ankabut, He questions whether people assume they'll just say "we believe" without being tested.

Here is the third instance: أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَن تُتْرَكُوا۟—do you assume you're going to be left alone? The first test was in Makkah—enduring persecution without retaliation. The second was at Badr—facing an impossible military challenge. Now comes a third, different kind of test.

This time, you have a powerful-looking army entering Makkah without much resistance. But this isn't a test of military might or patience under oppression. This is a test of loyalty and allegiance. وَلَمْ يَتَّخِذُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا رَسُولِهِۦ وَلَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلِيجَةً—and those who haven't taken anyone other than Allah, His Messenger ﷺ, and the true believers as their deeply intimate confidants.

The word وَلِيجَةً comes from ولج, meaning to enter. A walijah is a friend so close they've entered into every one of your personal matters, someone who has entered deep inside your heart. You're all mixed up in their life and they're all mixed up in yours—that level of intimate friendship.

Allah ﷻ is testing whether the believers have purified their hearts of such deep loyalties to those on the other side. Some of the people in Makkah were family members, childhood friends. It's hard to go and fight when civilians you care about might be affected. Allah ﷻ is saying: I'm telling you to trust Me. Let Me see who will put aside all their past judgments, family loyalties, and personal attachments to walk purely in My path.

translation: It is not for the polytheists to maintain the mosques of Allah [while] witnessing against themselves with disbelief. [For] those, their deeds have become worthless, and in the Fire they will abide eternally
commentary:
مَا كَانَ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ أَن يَعْمُرُوا۟ مَسَـٰجِدَ ٱللَّهِ شَـٰهِدِينَ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِم بِٱلْكُفْرِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ وَفِى ٱلنَّارِ هُمْ خَـٰلِدُونَ

مَا كَانَ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ أَن يَعْمُرُوا۟ مَسَـٰجِدَ ٱللَّهِ—it is not appropriate for the people of shirk to be building, constructing, or maintaining the houses of Allah. شَـٰهِدِينَ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِم بِٱلْكُفْرِ—while they openly testify against themselves with disbelief, calling upon Lat and Manat as they prepare the Kaaba.

Perhaps you think you're showing them mercy by not fighting them. But أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ—all their deeds have been nullified, seized. They thought they did something praiseworthy by maintaining the House of Allah ﷻ, but it counted for nothing. وَفِى ٱلنَّارِ هُمْ خَـٰلِدُونَ—they will be eternally in the Fire. What you might do to them in this world is nothing compared to what awaits them. Don't assume you're doing them harm by following Allah's command; you cannot save them from their ultimate fate.

translation: The mosques of Allah are only to be maintained by those who believe in Allah and the Last Day and establish prayer and give zakah and do not fear except Allah, for it is expected that those will be of the [rightly] guided
commentary:
إِنَّمَا يَعْمُرُ مَسَـٰجِدَ ٱللَّهِ مَنْ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَأَقَامَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَى ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَلَمْ يَخْشَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ ۖ فَعَسَىٰٓ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَن يَكُونُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ

إِنَّمَا يَعْمُرُ مَسَـٰجِدَ ٱللَّهِ مَنْ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ—the only people who should rightfully be building and maintaining the houses devoted to Allah ﷻ are those who believe in Allah and the Last Day. The structure of this ayah follows the logical order of a masjid's functions.

You build a masjid, and the first thing it reinforces is iman. The two aspects of iman most emphasized are belief in Allah ﷻ and the Last Day—the beginning and the end, this life and the next. The masjid becomes a place of spiritual reminder.

وَأَقَامَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ—then it becomes a place for the establishment of prayer. وَءَاتَى ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ—and it becomes the community center where you get to know people, understand who deserves zakat, and build communal strength. Everything happens at the masjid when it functions properly.

وَلَمْ يَخْشَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ—and they don't fear anyone except Allah. This quality ensures the masjid remains a place of truth, not compromised by political or social pressures.

This ayah teaches us the spirit behind building masajid. Alhamdulillah, in many places, especially in the West, there's a beautiful movement to construct masajid everywhere. But the intention behind the masjid must be clear from the beginning. This will be a place to remember Allah ﷻ. This will be a place to heal people spiritually. This will be a place where we feel a sense of community and help those in need around us.

Unfortunately, building masajid has sometimes become a fashionable competition with other masajid. You can feel the intention when you walk into a masjid—some fill you with peace the moment you enter, while others feel no different from walking into a corporate office. There's no soul in them. The intentions behind their construction have real consequences that manifest in the very atmosphere of the place.

translation: Have you made the providing of water for the pilgrim and the maintenance of al-Masjid al-Haram equal to [the deeds of] one who believes in Allah and the Last Day and strives in the cause of Allah? They are not equal in the sight of Allah. And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people
commentary:
۞ أَجَعَلْتُمْ سِقَايَةَ ٱلْحَآجِّ وَعِمَارَةَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ كَمَنْ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَجَـٰهَدَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ لَا يَسْتَوُۥنَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

سِقَايَةَ ٱلْحَآجِّ—giving drink to the pilgrims who make hajj. This was truly a beautiful practice. People didn't arrive by airplane; they came on camels, on foot, traveling great distances to reach Makkah. They arrived exhausted and thirsty. The people of Makkah were known for their hospitality to the pilgrims, providing them with water and refreshment. What a humanitarian gesture.

وَعِمَارَةَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ—and the maintenance of the Sacred Masjid. These were genuinely good deeds by any human standard.

But Allah ﷻ asks: Do you equate these actions كَمَنْ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ—with the one who believes in Allah and the Last Day وَجَـٰهَدَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ—and struggled in Allah's path? 

This is a critical lesson about how we understand good deeds versus how Allah ﷻ judges them. We might say about non-Muslim acquaintances, "That's such a nice person. They do so many good things." And while humanitarian acts have value, if someone is an enemy to Islam—as these leaders of Quraysh had become—their charitable works don't balance their opposition to truth.

This parallels the situation with Firaun. When Musa (AS) confronted him, Firaun responded, "Didn't I keep you in my house? Didn't I raise you as a baby?" And Musa (AS) had to say, "Do you remind me of your favor when you've enslaved an entire nation?" The context matters. The greater crime cannot be offset by personal kindness.

لَا يَسْتَوُۥنَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ—they are not equal in Allah's sight. وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ—Allah does not guide the wrongdoing nation. Even though He acknowledged what they do in terms of service, the ultimate label placed upon them is that of wrongdoers who are not deserving of guidance.

translation: The ones who have believed, emigrated and striven in the cause of Allah with their wealth and their lives are greater in rank in the sight of Allah. And it is those who are the attainers [of success]
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَهَاجَرُوا۟ وَجَـٰهَدُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ أَعْظَمُ دَرَجَةً عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَآئِزُونَ

ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟—those who believed. This is the foundation. وَهَاجَرُوا۟—and those who migrated. There was the great hijrah from Makkah to Madinah, but now, for the conquest of Makkah, there's a hijrah from Madinah back to Makkah. The Ansar, who hosted the Muhajirun, now have their own opportunity to be considered muhajirun. They're leaving their homes, their comfort, to march toward Makkah.

وَجَـٰهَدُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ—and they struggled in Allah's path with their wealth and their very selves. The struggle involves both material sacrifice and personal risk. 

أَعْظَمُ دَرَجَةً عِندَ ٱللَّهِ—they are higher in rank with Allah ﷻ. This isn't just about worldly recognition but about standing in the sight of Allah ﷻ. وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَآئِزُونَ—and these are the ones who are ultimately successful, the true winners. Not those who maintained political power or provided water to pilgrims while opposing the truth, but those who sacrificed everything for the sake of Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ.

translation: Their Lord gives them good tidings of mercy from Him and approval and of gardens for them wherein is enduring pleasure
commentary:
يُبَشِّرُهُمْ رَبُّهُم بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنْهُ وَرِضْوَٰنٍ وَجَنَّـٰتٍ لَّهُمْ فِيهَا نَعِيمٌ مُّقِيمٌ

Their Master gives them glad tidings with a special mercy that comes from Him, with contentment that comes only from Him, and gardens in which they will remain constantly blessed.

The word نَعِيم carries a beautiful dual meaning in Arabic. It functions as both an ism (noun) and a sifah (adjective) simultaneously. Naim describes someone who is constantly blessed while also describing the blessings around them that never cease. These are permanent, stable blessings—not temporary luxuries that fade away. There's no uncertainty, no wondering if they'll have to move next year or if their situation will change. They're settled, established in that state permanently.

This point about stability holds particular significance for the Companions who lived uncertain lives, never knowing where they would end up. Allah ﷻ had promised them earlier, "He will surely give you land to settle in." Now He confirms they will remain in that land permanently in Paradise.

translation: [They will be] abiding therein forever. Indeed, Allah has with Him a great reward
commentary:
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عِندَهُۥٓ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ

They will remain there permanently, forever. Certainly Allah ﷻ—He alone possesses the ultimate compensation, the great reward.

Notice the transition in motivation that Allah ﷻ presents to the Muslims regarding the advance against Makkah. Initially, the focus was on what the Makkans had done—their past aggression and betrayals. The message was clear: they deserve this response. Then the discourse shifts to emphasize that the disbelievers deserve even worse consequences in the Hereafter. Finally, Allah ﷻ redirects the believers' focus entirely: keep your eyes on the prize. The real goal is Paradise. That's why you're doing this—because I will be pleased with you.

This progression teaches us something profound about how Allah ﷻ motivates the believers. He acknowledges the justice of their cause, but ultimately He wants their hearts fixed on pleasing Him and attaining His reward, not merely on worldly justice.

translation: O you who have believed, do not take your fathers or your brothers as allies if they have preferred disbelief over belief. And whoever does so among you - then it is those who are the wrongdoers
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّخِذُوٓا۟ ءَابَآءَكُمْ وَإِخْوَٰنَكُمْ أَوْلِيَآءَ إِنِ ٱسْتَحَبُّوا۟ ٱلْكُفْرَ عَلَى ٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ ۚ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّهُم مِّنكُمْ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ

O you who believe, do not take your fathers and your brothers as close allies if they have preferred disbelief over faith. And whoever among you takes them as allies—then those are the wrongdoers.

These two ayahs regarding family ties represent a final verdict, addressing the concerns of some Companions who worried about their family members being killed in Makkah. Allah ﷻ tells the believers not to take their ancestors, fathers, brothers, or extended family as protective, closely-held friends if these relatives have chosen to love disbelief over faith.

The timing is critical. It had been 23 years since the Prophet's ﷺ mission began. Time was up. These people had made their choices. Where there had previously been room for calling them to Islam, that opportunity had closed. The loyalty the believers felt toward these family members now had to be set aside. Whoever continues to befriend them becomes among the wrongdoers.

We must understand the context carefully. When someone converts to Islam today and their family remains non-Muslim, we cannot simply hand them this ayah and say, "Don't befriend your father or brother." Context matters immensely. Which people were the Companions supposed to have no loyalty toward? These specific Makkans about whom Allah ﷻ had passed a particular verdict.

Consider Prophet Nuh (AS), who dealt with his disbelieving son for over 900 years. Did Allah ﷻ tell him from the beginning, "Don't worry about him"? No. That verdict came only at the very end, when Allah ﷻ finally said, "He is not from your family." Throughout those centuries, Nuh (AS) continued trying.

Look at Prophet Ibrahim (AS) and his father. Allah ﷻ didn't immediately command Ibrahim (AS) to cut ties. Even after his father died, Ibrahim (AS) continued making supplication for him, until Allah ﷻ specifically revealed that he should stop. Until that divine verdict came explicitly, Ibrahim (AS) wouldn't give up on his father.

Imagine someone comes to you saying, "My son goes to parties and clubs. My other son is a hafiz of the Quran. So the first one isn't my son anymore." Would we accept this? Prophet Nuh (AS) dealt with his son for 950 years before receiving explicit instruction from Allah ﷻ. We don't get to decide to cut off family relationships—those verdicts come from Allah ﷻ alone.

Allah ﷻ had already told the believers earlier that He had not yet exposed who among them would truly struggle in His path. Their loyalties had to be tested. This became the ultimate test of loyalty for the Companions—they had to consider these family relations cut. These were the same family ties they had maintained throughout the entire Seerah. They weren't supposed to cut them before. But this represented a special punishment.

Maintaining family ties is a form of mercy, isn't it? But this ayah came without any mercy for these particular disbelievers. So even that which the religion upholds in every other situation was suspended here. For these specific people, the ruling was: you cannot be friends anymore. It's over. The believers had to find it in their hearts to accept this.

Umar (RA) said that before this surah, there were three types of people: believer, disbeliever, and hypocrite. After this surah came, there were only believers and disbelievers. It became crystal clear where everyone stood. He said this surah came like a sword drawn from its sheath.

translation: Say, [O Muhammad], "If your fathers, your sons, your brothers, your wives, your relatives, wealth which you have obtained, commerce wherein you fear decline, and dwellings with which you are pleased are more beloved to you than Allah and His Messenger and jihad in His cause, then wait until Allah executes His command. And Allah does not guide the defiantly disobedient people
commentary:
قُلْ إِن كَانَ ءَابَآؤُكُمْ وَأَبْنَآؤُكُمْ وَإِخْوَٰنُكُمْ وَأَزْوَٰجُكُمْ وَعَشِيرَتُكُمْ وَأَمْوَٰلٌ ٱقْتَرَفْتُمُوهَا وَتِجَـٰرَةٌ تَخْشَوْنَ كَسَادَهَا وَمَسَـٰكِنُ تَرْضَوْنَهَآ أَحَبَّ إِلَيْكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَجِهَادٍ فِى سَبِيلِهِۦ فَتَرَبَّصُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِىَ ٱللَّهُ بِأَمْرِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ

Say: If your fathers, your sons, your brothers, your spouses, your extended family, wealth that you have accumulated, commerce that you fear will decline, and homes that please you—if these are more beloved to you than Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ and struggling in His path, then wait until Allah ﷻ brings His decision. And Allah ﷻ does not guide the corrupt people.

This stands as one of the most powerful ayahs in the Quran on this subject. Allah ﷻ commands the Prophet ﷺ to tell them: if your ancestors, your parents, your elders, your children, your brothers, your spouses, and your extended family—your dozens of relatives all around you—if these matter more to you, then consider what else competes for your heart.

The wealth that you've gathered from here and there, piled together carefully. The businesses you constantly worry about—checking balances, monitoring inventory, reviewing sales figures, always fearing they might go down. The homes you've worked so hard to perfect—making sure everything looks exactly the way you want it, obsessing over furniture arrangement, color combinations, the backyard landscaping, every detail of the interior design.

The ayah begins with family, then transitions to possessions. These eight categories represent what consumes our minds: family relationships first, then material concerns. On one hand, there are the people. On the other, there's the stuff—the money, the business anxieties, the house improvements. Whether you're checking your account balance online multiple times a day, or lying awake at night worried about contracts not coming through or shipments being delayed, or endlessly rearranging furniture and planning renovations—these are the things that take over our consciousness.

Then Allah ﷻ places all eight of these on a scale. If all of this combined—these eight things together—are more beloved to you than Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ, it would have been easier if the ayah had ended there. We could say, "Of course we love Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ more." But Allah ﷻ doesn't stop there. He adds a third element that makes it tangible: وَجِهَادٍ فِى سَبِيلِهِ—and struggling in His path.

The love of Allah ﷻ and love of the Messenger ﷺ are intangible, internal feelings. Anyone can claim them. You could be living any kind of life and say, "Of course I love Allah ﷻ. I love the Messenger ﷺ so much." But Allah ﷻ says: put your life where your mouth is. The proof of the first two is in the third—jihad in His path. This struggle is tangible. It involves time, effort, sacrifice, financial commitment, sacrificing your youth, your talents, your energy.

If there's no visible effort, no tangible sacrifice, then what should you do? فَتَرَبَّصُوا—then just sit and wait. The word تَرَبُّص in Arabic carries a powerful meaning. It describes waiting as a form of procrastination. "I have time—tomorrow, tomorrow. I'll take my deen seriously next year, insha'Allah. Let me just take care of this first. I really need to work on this. I have these ideas for projects to serve the community. Why don't I do them? Well, I'm in college right now. As soon as I graduate... As soon as I get a job... As soon as I get married, insha'Allah... As soon as the baby comes... As soon as the kids are in school... As soon as we buy the house... As soon as we pay off the kids' college... As soon as they get married... As soon as the grandkids arrive..."

The procrastination never ends. There's always going to be something—tax season if you're an accountant, vacation time because the family needs a break. The excuses keep shifting: college, then marriage, then children, then savings, then the house, then retirement. You find people who partied their entire youth filling the masjid in their seventies and eighties. All the young people look at them and think, "I have time. I have time."

The Messenger of Allah ﷺ warned us about this: take advantage of five things before five things come. One of the primary teachings? Youth before old age. Take advantage of your youth before old age arrives. Shaitan doesn't want to hear about what you'll do when you're old. By then you won't have the energy. You can't get out of your chair, let alone go to the places of temptation. Shaitan comes to you when you're young—when you have the energy, the ability, the opportunity. That's when he attacks with full force.

Allah ﷻ says to those who prioritize these eight things over Him, His Messenger ﷺ, and struggle in His path: then sit back, relax, and wait until Allah ﷻ brings His decision. It's like when parents tell a misbehaving child, "Just wait until your father gets home"—you don't know exactly what it means, but you're terrified.

وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ—Allah ﷻ does not guide the corrupt people. This ayah defines what corruption (fisq) means to Allah ﷻ. All these relationships, all these material concerns—they become false idols when they take precedence over Allah ﷻ. The only thing that should remain standing is لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا ٱللَّٰهُ.

A personal observation: there seems to be more energy and activism among college-age Muslims today than in previous generations, with MSAs and Islamic programs thriving. But what often happens when these activists graduate? They disappear. Not to study Islam more deeply at home or attend the masjid more regularly—they simply vanish. These same people who used to juggle college, work, and multiple Islamic programs—organizing conferences, setting up booths, establishing mentorship programs, even sponsoring orphans—become consumed by careers, mortgages, and material concerns.

I remember MSAs that would sponsor an orphan every year, finding them adoptive parents and setting them up for success. I witnessed Columbia University's MSA coordinate a conference of 500 student groups from 30 colleges across New York City, addressing human rights violations—and they strategically involved the ping pong club, the chess club, the Hindu Student Society, every kind of campus organization. A thousand people showed up, and no one could say it was just the Muslims organizing it because everyone was involved. It was brilliant activism. But then that passion often dies after graduation, buried under 80-hour work weeks, savings plans, house purchases, and family obligations.

But it doesn't have to be that way. We can still make time for the deen. We must make time for learning, for breaking away from those consuming concerns to push ourselves further in our religion. Make time for the masjid, for studying, for reciting Quran, for keeping up with Islamic education—this is an essential part of life. This is why we're on this Earth. We're not here merely to work, save money, or build houses. That's not our purpose. These houses will all be gone.

Sometimes it helps to step outside, look at our homes and cities, and recite the ayah: "We will turn this place into a barren desert, flat land with nothing on it." Walk through any major city, see the skyscrapers, and reflect: what would it take to turn this into a flat desert? It's Allah's promise. Human beings need constant reality checks. Even the most knowledgeable Muslims require this reinforcement repeatedly.

translation: Allah has already given you victory in many regions and [even] on the day of Hunayn, when your great number pleased you, but it did not avail you at all, and the earth was confining for you with its vastness; then you turned back, fleeing
commentary:
لَقَدْ نَصَرَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ فِى مَوَاطِنَ كَثِيرَةٍ ۙ وَيَوْمَ حُنَيْنٍ ۙ إِذْ أَعْجَبَتْكُمْ كَثْرَتُكُمْ فَلَمْ تُغْنِ عَنكُمْ شَيْـًٔا وَضَاقَتْ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضُ بِمَا رَحُبَتْ ثُمَّ وَلَّيْتُم مُّدْبِرِينَ

Allah ﷻ has already aided you on so many occasions, and on the Day of Hunayn, when your great numbers impressed you—but they availed you nothing. The earth, despite its vastness, became constricted for you, and then you turned back, fleeing.

Allah ﷻ reminds the believers: you've relied on Me for so many victories. Why not rely on Me this time? Most recently, consider what happened at Hunayn.

When the Muslim army advanced against the enemy at Hunayn, they had to pass through a narrow valley with tall mountains on either side. A huge force of Muslims—some reports mention up to 10,000—moved through this passage. The enemy had positioned expert archers on top of the surrounding heights. These archers functioned like snipers, raining arrows down from above. Combined with ground combat, the situation created chaos. The Muslim forces scattered in different directions.

At one critical point, the Prophet ﷺ found himself nearly alone. Thousands had come, yet he stood almost by himself. He grabbed the flag—and understand, when archers are shooting, who's the main target? The man holding the flag, because when the flag falls, the army scatters. The Messenger of Allah ﷺ grabbed that flag and called out: أَنَا النَّبِيُّ لَا كَذِبْ، أَنَا ابْنُ عَبْدِ الْمُطَّلِّبِ—"I am the Prophet, no lie! I am the son of Abdul Muttalib!" Then he called: يَا أَصْحَابَ الشَّجَرَةِ—"O people of the tree!"—meaning those who had taken the pledge at Hudaybiyah. "Come back to me! Let's go!"

He managed to regroup them. From thousands, only about 200 to 300 gathered back to him. And were they in a militarily advantageous position? No—they were at the bottom of a valley.

This is what Allah ﷻ references: "I helped you on many occasions, but have you forgotten what happened at Hunayn?" إِذْ أَعْجَبَتْكُمْ كَثْرَتُكُمْ—when your huge numbers really impressed you. You thought, "We managed victory at Badr with 313 against 1,000. This time with our numbers, it will be easy." فَلَمْ تُغْنِ عَنكُمْ شَيْـًٔا—but your numbers didn't help you at all.

وَضَاقَتْ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضُ بِمَا رَحُبَتْ—the earth became constricted against you despite its vast expanse. Though the land was wide and open, it felt like it shrank around you, closing in from all sides. ثُمَّ وَلَّيْتُم مُّدْبِرِينَ—then you turned away, showing your backs in retreat.

This vivid reminder serves to humble the believers: victory comes from Allah ﷻ alone, not from numbers, preparation, or military advantage. No matter how strong you appear, without His help, even the earth itself feels like it's collapsing around you.

translation: Then Allah sent down His tranquillity upon His Messenger and upon the believers and sent down soldiers angels whom you did not see and punished those who disbelieved. And that is the recompense of the disbelievers
commentary:
ثُمَّ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ سَكِينَتَهُۥ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ وَعَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَأَنزَلَ جُنُودًا لَّمْ تَرَوْهَا وَعَذَّبَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ جَزَآءُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

This ayah speaks of divine intervention following a difficult moment. Allah ﷻ sent down His سَكِينَة - His tranquility and peace - upon His Messenger ﷺ and upon the believers. This came at a time when they needed it most, when hearts were shaken and composure was necessary.

Beyond the visible support, Allah ﷻ sent armies that remained unseen to human eyes. These were forces beyond our perception, yet their effects were manifest in the punishment that befell those who disbelieved. The ayah concludes with a powerful statement: this is the just recompense of the disbelievers. Their punishment wasn't arbitrary but rather the natural consequence of their rejection of truth.

translation: Then Allah will accept repentance after that for whom He wills; and Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
ثُمَّ يَتُوبُ ٱللَّهُ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

Despite the severity of what came before, Allah ﷻ immediately opens the door of mercy. Even after such failings - including those who fled from the battlefield - there remains room for repentance. The message is clear: regardless of what weakness overtook you in that moment, regardless of how you were carried away by fear, the door to Allah's ﷻ forgiveness remains open.

This is the nature of our Lord - غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ. He is exceedingly forgiving and perpetually merciful. His mercy encompasses even those who stumbled, provided they turn back to Him with sincere repentance. The phrase "whoever He wills" doesn't restrict His mercy but rather emphasizes that it's His divine prerogative to accept genuine repentance.

translation: O you who have believed, indeed the polytheists are unclean, so let them not approach al-Masjid al-Haram after this, their [final] year. And if you fear privation, Allah will enrich you from His bounty if He wills. Indeed, Allah is Knowing and Wise
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَا ٱلْمُشْرِكُونَ نَجَسٌ فَلَا يَقْرَبُوا۟ ٱلْمَسْجِدَ ٱلْحَرَامَ بَعْدَ عَامِهِمْ هَـٰذَا ۚ وَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ عَيْلَةً فَسَوْفَ يُغْنِيكُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦٓ إِن شَآءَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ

The conversation now shifts back to domestic policy following the conquest of Makkah. Allah ﷻ addresses the believers directly: the mushrikun - those who associate partners with Allah ﷻ - are نَجَسٌ, spiritually impure. This isn't about physical cleanliness but about the spiritual corruption of shirk itself. Therefore, after this year's Hajj, they should not even approach the Masjid al-Haram.

This decree raised an immediate concern among the believers. Makkah's economy had always depended on the pilgrims who came to worship at the Ka'bah, even when they came to pay tribute to idols. If this entire system of shirk was eliminated, who would come? How would the economy survive? The believers feared عَيْلَة - bankruptcy, economic hardship.

Allah ﷻ addresses this concern directly with a promise: He will enrich you from His bounty if He wills. Within just a few years of this revelation, treasures from the Persian and Roman empires would flow into the Muslim lands. The believers would have so much wealth they wouldn't know where to store it. And centuries later, even oil would be discovered in these lands, bringing unimaginable wealth.

The ayah concludes by reminding us that Allah ﷻ is عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ- All-Knowing and All-Wise. He knows what concerns you, and His wisdom encompasses solutions beyond your imagination.

translation: Fight those who do not believe in Allah or in the Last Day and who do not consider unlawful what Allah and His Messenger have made unlawful and who do not adopt the religion of truth from those who were given the Scripture - [fight] until they give the jizyah willingly while they are humbled
commentary:
قَـٰتِلُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَلَا بِٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَلَا يُحَرِّمُونَ مَا حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ وَلَا يَدِينُونَ دِينَ ٱلْحَقِّ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ حَتَّىٰ يُعْطُوا۟ ٱلْجِزْيَةَ عَن يَدٍ وَهُمْ صَـٰغِرُونَ

This ayah outlines the policy toward those who don't believe in Allah ﷻ and the Last Day, who don't hold sacred what Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ made sacred, and who don't practice the religion of truth. Significantly, this includes من الذين أوتوا الكتاب- those from among the People of the Book.

Some might have assumed that the mushrikun needed to be dealt with, but perhaps the People of the Book living in Arabia or Madinah could maintain the status quo. This ayah clarifies that even they fall under this policy until they give the jizyah - a form of tax - while they are in a state of acknowledgment of Islamic authority.

The concept of jizyah requires proper understanding in its historical context. In the Islamic state, citizenship was defined by one's acceptance of Islam as the supreme law. Just as modern nations have immigration policies and different tax brackets for citizens versus non-citizens, the Islamic state had a constitutional framework. 

Muslims paid zakat as their religious obligation and tax. Non-Muslims, who hadn't taken an oath to uphold the Islamic constitution, paid jizyah instead. This wasn't about humiliation but about a clear legal framework where everyone contributed to the state according to their status. The phrase وَهُمْ صَـٰغِرُونَ means they would not be above Islamic law - they would live under its protection while acknowledging its supremacy as the law of the land.

This system also protected the lives and property of non-Muslim citizens. They could maintain their own religious courts and domestic laws within the larger umbrella of Islamic law, similar to how certain communities maintain internal arbitration systems within modern legal frameworks.

translation: The Jews say, "Ezra is the son of Allah "; and the Christians say, "The Messiah is the son of Allah." That is their statement from their mouths; they imitate the saying of those who disbelieved [before them]. May Allah destroy them; how are they deluded
commentary:
وَقَالَتِ ٱلْيَهُودُ عُزَيْرٌ ٱبْنُ ٱللَّهِ وَقَالَتِ ٱلنَّصَـٰرَى ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ ٱللَّهِ ۖ ذَٰلِكَ قَوْلُهُم بِأَفْوَٰهِهِمْ ۖ يُضَـٰهِـُٔونَ قَوْلَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ ۚ قَـٰتَلَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ

Having just discussed the mushrikun and the People of the Book, Allah ﷻ now addresses a critical question: aren't the People of the Book closer to us in belief than the mushrikun? This ayah reveals why that assumption is problematic.

The Jews said Uzayr is the son of Allah ﷻ, and the Christians said the Messiah is the son of Allah ﷻ. It's important to note that this refers to specific denominations that existed in Arabia at the time. Many Jewish communities never held this belief about Uzayr, but there was a faction that did incorporate this into their theology. Similarly, various Christian denominations have different formulations of the Trinity, but the core claim about Christ being the son of God remains.

Allah ﷻ then makes a striking statement: ذَٰلِكَ قَوْلُهُم بِأَفْوَٰهِهِمْ - these are merely words coming from their mouths. They have no real basis in revelation or sound reasoning. More significantly, يُضَاهِئُونَ قَوْلَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ - they are plagiarizing, adopting, mimicking the statements of disbelievers from long before them.

Some historians trace the Christian formulation of the Trinity to when Paul expanded Christianity beyond the Israelites to all humanity. One of the earliest audiences was the Egyptians, who already had a mythology featuring Osiris (the father god), Isis (the mother goddess), and Horus (the son of god). The earliest Christian Trinity bore striking similarities: God the Father, Mary (Mother of God), and Jesus (Son of God). This historical connection illuminates what Allah ﷻ means by "plagiarizing the words of those who disbelieved before."

The ayah ends with قَاتَلَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ - may Allah ﷻ destroy them, and أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ- how are they being turned away from truth? This isn't merely condemnation but an expression of bewilderment at how people can adopt such beliefs when they have access to revealed scripture.

translation: They have taken their scholars and monks as lords besides Allah, and [also] the Messiah, the son of Mary. And they were not commanded except to worship one God; there is no deity except Him. Exalted is He above whatever they associate with Him
commentary:
ٱتَّخَذُوٓا۟ أَحْبَارَهُمْ وَرُهْبَـٰنَهُمْ أَرْبَابًا مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْمَسِيحَ ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَمَآ أُمِرُوٓا۟ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَـٰهًا وَٰحِدًا ۖ لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ

They took their أَحْبَار- their scribes, scholars of great authorship who wrote so prolifically they were called "people of ink" - and their رُهْبَان - their monks, the aesthetically spiritual who isolated themselves in monasteries, wore uncomfortable clothes, and dedicated themselves entirely to worship while shunning worldly matters - as أَرْبَابًا, lords and masters besides Allah ﷻ. They also took the Messiah, son of Maryam (AS), in this way.

A companion who had previously been Christian, Adi ibn Hatim (RA) - the son of the legendary Hatim al-Ta'i known for his generosity - heard this ayah and was confused. He said to the Prophet ﷺ, "We didn't take them as gods. What does the ayah mean?" The Prophet ﷺ asked him, "Didn't they make halal what was haram, and didn't they make haram what was halal, and you followed them?" Adi (RA) confirmed this was true. The Prophet ﷺ explained, "That is what it means to take them as lords."

One core meaning of the word رَبّ is the one who legislates right and wrong for you, who decides what is permissible and what is forbidden. When religious authorities make these determinations and people follow them blindly without referring back to divine revelation, those authorities have effectively replaced Allah ﷻ as the source of moral legislation.

Muslims today must reflect on this warning. We sometimes fall into a similar trap, though in a less severe form. We see a knowledgeable person doing something questionable and think, "Well, if they're doing it, it must be okay." Or we justify our actions by saying, "So-and-so said it," without examining the evidence ourselves. We transfer the responsibility off our shoulders, making their word the final authority rather than the teachings of the Quran and Sunnah.

This is why Islamic scholarship has always maintained transparency. Classical scholars would never just declare something halal or haram without explaining their reasoning and documenting their sources. This allowed other scholars and even students to examine their conclusions and, if necessary, respectfully disagree. Imam Ibn Kathir, for example, wrote a comprehensive Sirah, and within it, Imam Dhahabi corrected him on certain points. This process of scholarly discourse and correction is essential to prevent any human being from becoming an absolute, unquestionable authority.

The ayah concludes by reminding us that they were commanded only لِيَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَـٰهًا وَٰحِدًا - to worship One God alone. لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ - there is none worthy of worship except Him. سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ - He is far above, exalted beyond the shirk they attribute to Him.

translation: They want to extinguish the light of Allah with their mouths, but Allah refuses except to perfect His light, although the disbelievers dislike it
commentary:
يُرِيدُونَ أَن يُطْفِـُٔوا۟ نُورَ ٱللَّهِ بِأَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَيَأْبَى ٱللَّهُ إِلَّآ أَن يُتِمَّ نُورَهُۥ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ

This powerful ayah captures the ultimate futility of opposing divine truth. The disbelievers want يُطْفِئُوا - to extinguish - نُورَ ٱللَّهِ, the light of Allah, بِأَفْوَٰهِهِمْ, with their mouths alone. Just words, just speech, just rhetoric trying to put out divine illumination.

But Allah ﷻ refuses: وَيَأْبَى ٱللَّهُ إِلَّآ أَن يُتِمَّ نُورَهُۥ. The verb أَبَى (aba) when used with إِلَّا  expresses absolute refusal to accept any alternative. Allah ﷻ is determined, will not take no for an answer, refuses to accept anything except the completion of His light. No matter what opposition arises, the nur of Allah ﷻ will be completed.

The contrast between kafir and nur is linguistically profound. The word كَافِر comes from a root meaning to bury something in the ground where it's dark, to cover and conceal. نُور is light - that which emerges from darkness, that which illuminates and reveals. Just as morning light has degrees - a little light, then more light, gradually increasing until full brightness - the nur of Allah ﷻ manifests progressively. It cannot be stopped, just as the sun cannot be prevented from rising no matter how much anyone tries to keep it covered.

The ayah ends with وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ - even though the disbelievers despise it, even though they hate it with intense aversion. Their hatred cannot stop the inevitable completion of Allah's ﷻ light. This is both a warning to those who oppose truth and a source of comfort for believers: the ultimate victory of divine guidance is assured, regardless of how much resistance it faces.

translation: It is He who has sent His Messenger with guidance and the religion of truth to manifest it over all religion, although they who associate others with Allah dislike it
commentary:
هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُۥ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ وَدِينِ ٱلْحَقِّ لِيُظْهِرَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلدِّينِ كُلِّهِۦ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْمُشْرِكُونَ

*He is the One Who has sent His Messenger with guidance and the religion of truth, so He may make it prevail over all religions, even though the polytheists hate it.*

### The Deeper Form of Shirk

While the previous ayah spoke of the kafirun and their hatred of Islam's very presence, this ayah addresses the mushrikun and their hatred of Islam's authority. This distinction reveals a profound psychological reality.

Consider the example of building a masjid. When fundraising for masjid construction, organizers typically go throughout the community seeking donations from Muslims. An interesting phenomenon emerges: even Muslims who are not particularly observant of the religion often want to contribute. You might approach a Muslim business owner whose earnings come from questionable sources—perhaps he owns a convenience store that sells alcohol and lottery tickets. When you ask him to donate toward the masjid, what does he say? "Absolutely! In fact, can I make my donation on the 27th night of Ramadan? Isn't that better for getting more reward?"

His motivation might be a guilty conscience, or perhaps just a feeling that since he doesn't do much else for Islam, this is the least he can contribute. But the point is clear: even Muslims engaged in haram practices generally have no problem with the light of Islam spreading in some form. They're happy to see a masjid built, pleased when Islam has some visible presence in society.

But now imagine a different campaign. The masjid is built, alhamdulillah. Now we launch a new initiative—not a fundraising drive, but a pledge drive. We're asking Muslims to sign a written commitment that they will not earn money through haram means anymore. The same person who went around collecting donations from questionable businesses now approaches that convenience store owner with this new request: "Would you be willing to sign this pledge that you won't earn haram income anymore?"

Who's going to sign up for that? There's a massive difference now, isn't there? That same person who happily contributed money is suddenly uncomfortable. Why? Because he's fine with Islam existing as long as it doesn't interfere with his life choices. He likes to see some manifestation of Islamic light, but he doesn't want Islam to be the dominant, authoritative force in his personal decisions.

### Shirk Beyond Idols

This reveals that shirk takes forms beyond just worshiping physical idols. The most recognizable form of shirk is bowing before statues, making prayers to created objects—that's easily identifiable. But fundamentally, what does shirk mean? It means placing something in a position that only Allah ﷻ deserves to occupy. That "something" could be an object of worship, but it could also be ourselves—our own desires, preferences, and autonomy.

True tawhid means recognizing that the final authority in every aspect of life belongs to Allah ﷻ. But if I decide that another authority has precedence—whether that's my own personal aspirations, my preferences for how I should earn income, my ideas about how I want to structure my family life or distribute my inheritance—then I have, in essence, set up a partner to Allah ﷻ. I'm willing to give Allah ﷻ authority over some things (like which direction to face in prayer), but not willing to submit to Him in other matters. I want Him to stay out of certain areas of my life.

This mindset has become especially pervasive in societies that celebrate "individual freedom" as the highest human achievement. The narrative suggests that human beings throughout history have been striving for this one goal, and finally we've reached it—the freedom to be whoever we want, to make our own choices without anyone imposing anything on us.

But Islam begins with exactly the opposite premise: individual slavery to Allah ﷻ. The entire religion is built on the foundation of Allah ﷻ imposing His will upon the slave. This is where the tension arises.

### The Mission of the Messenger

Allah ﷻ states: هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُۥ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ وَدِينِ ٱلْحَقِّ - "He is the One Who sent His messenger with guidance and the religion of truth."

The word هُدَىٰ means guidance—direction toward what is right and beneficial. But notice the second phrase: دِينِ ٱلْحَقِّ - "the religion of truth" or more precisely, "the way of dealing with everything according to ultimate truth." This isn't just spiritual guidance for personal enlightenment. This is a comprehensive system for how to conduct every aspect of life according to what is objectively, ultimately true.

Then comes the purpose clause: لِيُظْهِرَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلدِّينِ كُلِّهِۦ - "so that He may make it prevail over all other ways of life." The verb يُظْهِرَ means to make something dominant, to give it authority and supremacy over other things. Allah's ﷻ purpose in sending His Messenger ﷺ wasn't just to offer Islam as one option among many lifestyles. It was to establish Islam as the authoritative way above all others.

### Two Parties Disturbed by Islam's Authority

This reality—that Islam has come to be the dominant authority—deeply disturbs two groups. Both groups are mentioned across these two ayahs: the kafirun and the mushrikun.

By the time of Surah At-Tawbah's revelation, Makkah had been conquered. Muslims were in a position of authority, and Allah's law was not merely theoretical—it was being implemented in practice. Two parties found this development unbearable:

First, the mushrikun—the idol worshipers whose physical objects of worship had been destroyed. But the real object of their worship was never those stone idols. The real deity they worshiped was themselves, their own autonomy, their own authority over their lives. You don't need a physical idol for that kind of shirk.

Second, the munafiqun—the hypocrites. One of the major themes in the latter portion of Surah At-Tawbah is the exposure of hypocrisy. Nifaq (hypocrisy) is essentially hidden shirk. It's internal shirk, psychological shirk, character shirk that resides inside a person. On the outside, it appears to be tawhid and submission, but on the inside, there is nothing but shirk—partnership and resistance to Allah's complete authority.

Both groups—the outward mushrikun and the internal mushrikun (the munafiqun)—are called out in these ayahs. As Surah At-Tawbah continues, we will see the hypocrites exposed in even greater detail, their characteristics laid bare, their internal reality brought into the light they so desperately try to avoid.

The ayah concludes: وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْمُشْرِكُونَ - "even though the polytheists hate it." Just as the kafirun hate seeing any trace of Islam's light, the mushrikun hate seeing Islam in a position of authority. But just as the hatred of the kafirun cannot prevent Allah ﷻ from perfecting His light, the hatred of the mushrikun cannot prevent Islam from prevailing over all other ways of life. Their feelings about it are utterly irrelevant to the inevitable outcome that Allah ﷻ has decreed.

translation: O you who have believed, indeed many of the scholars and the monks devour the wealth of people unjustly and avert [them] from the way of Allah. And those who hoard gold and silver and spend it not in the way of Allah - give them tidings of a painful punishment
commentary:
