۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلْأَحْبَارِ وَٱلرُّهْبَانِ لَيَأْكُلُونَ أَمْوَٰلَ ٱلنَّاسِ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ وَيَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱلَّذِينَ يَكْنِزُونَ ٱلذَّهَبَ وَٱلْفِضَّةَ وَلَا يُنفِقُونَهَا فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَبَشِّرْهُم بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ

Allah ﷻ begins this ayah with a direct address to the believers, warning them about a serious corruption that exists among religious authorities. The word الْأَحْبَارِ refers to scholars and research scholars—those who possess deep knowledge and are serious in their pursuit of learning. The word comes from حِبْر, denoting someone devoted to scholarship and research.

The second group mentioned, الرُّهْبَانِ, refers to spiritual leaders—people known for their intense devotion and fear of Allah ﷻ. The word رَاهِب is from the same root as رَهْبَة, meaning to be terrified. These are individuals who present themselves as people deeply afraid of disobeying or disappointing Allah ﷻ. In society, they are viewed as godly people, righteous individuals whom others turn to for prayers and spiritual guidance.

These two categories represent the types of religious figures that general masses of Muslims naturally look up to: people of scholarship and people of worship. You see this dynamic everywhere—the hafiz who memorizes the Quran, the elder always engaged in dhikr at the masjid. When people face difficulties, they seek out these individuals, asking them to make dua, believing they possess a special level of spirituality.

The problem arises when people are placed on such pedestals of religious authority. Those in these positions gain significant influence, and unless their scholarship has truly penetrated the depths of their hearts, and unless their spirituality is genuine, they will inevitably take advantage of this position. Religion, tragically, can become an industry.

This is what Allah ﷻ warns about when He says: لَيَأْكُلُونَ أَمْوَٰلَ ٱلنَّاسِ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ—"they consume the monies of the people using falsehood." The phrase بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ is critical here. It indicates that these religious figures exploit their positions to extract wealth through false means, manipulating people's trust and sincere devotion.

This is one of the most challenging conversations in the Quran to have within Muslim communities. Our scholars, our religious leaders, those we learn from and look up to—people in positions of religious authority—wield tremendous influence. Even though Muslims today may not live under Islamic political power in many places, religious credentials still carry weight. The sheikh, the mufti, the imam—these titles command respect and create positions of leadership.

We cannot pretend that money doesn't play a role in society, and we must examine where funding for Islamic scholarship comes from. If the funding source is not clean, it will inevitably influence the work these scholars do. Does funding influence medical research? Yes. Does it influence political science? Certainly. The same applies to religious scholarship.

Consider a simple example: a masjid made up of very wealthy individuals needs an imam. But they want someone who won't challenge their lifestyle. During the interview process, they ask whether he's "extreme" in his views—by which they mean, will he call haram things haram? If a candidate passes the interview but later says something they disagree with, they'll label him as "too extreme" or "not understanding the American context." His job becomes vulnerable.

Even more subtle pressures emerge. Questionable events might be scheduled at the masjid, and the imam is told ahead of time to stay quiet, not to cause fitna. If he speaks up, he risks losing his position. If he stays silent, why? Because he has a family to provide for, bills to pay, children to put through school. These are real pressures that every working person faces.

The result is that corruption becomes further entrenched. We need to free our scholars from these pressures so they can speak openly and clearly, without fear of being fired or boycotted. Only then can honest conversations take place.

The problem manifests differently on the رُهْبَان side—the spiritual leadership side. Many people are emotionally vulnerable, not encouraged to be critical thinkers, especially in matters of religion. In some societies, people are taught simply to follow, never to question. When these individuals face spiritual problems and need religious counsel, they become targets for exploitation.

An entire industry develops around taking advantage of religiously uneducated people. In places like Pakistan, you'll find shrines where individuals who appear to have not bathed in months dance around graves. People come to them, give donations, receive a talisman to wear on their arm, and are told they'll never have a motorcycle accident again. This is a massive, exploitative industry.

These so-called spiritual guides claim lineage from the family of the Prophet ﷺ, promising to solve all your problems. They'll tell you how to make your business succeed, how to have more sons instead of daughters, whatever problems people bring to them. They make hundreds of thousands of dollars in a matter of weeks because desperate people throw money at them.

This phenomenon exists in America too, and it's growing. Certain individuals prey on the emotional immaturity of younger people, pulling them into their circle through calculated tactics. First, they break people down psychologically, making them feel worthless, telling them they're horrible, destined for Hell, that they don't remember Allah ﷻ properly or pray correctly. After weeks of this conditioning, when the person is completely broken down, they've established themselves as everything while the victim feels like nothing.

At that point, they begin giving spiritual advice that the person follows without question. Then they progress to more controlling directives: your parents don't understand you, your spouse doesn't understand, you need to divorce, you need to move out. Eventually, they begin extracting money, often indirectly at first. They'll mention a problem like unpaid rent, and the conditioned follower will offer to help. It's all part of a deliberate plan.

These individuals are trained in this manipulation. They hold halaqas in homes rather than masajid, telling people not to go to masajid because "they're built with haram money." Why do they want to keep people away from masajid? Because in a masjid, others will recognize who this person really is. They do their damage, get exposed, pack their bags, move to another town, and repeat the cycle.

The ayah continues: وَيَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ—"and they stop themselves and others from the path of Allah ﷻ." Not only do they exploit people financially, but they actively prevent people from true guidance.

Allah ﷻ then addresses a different but related issue: وَٱلَّذِينَ يَكْنِزُونَ ٱلذَّهَبَ وَٱلْفِضَّةَ—"And those who continue to hoard gold and silver." The verb يَكْنِزُونَ indicates an ongoing action of hoarding wealth. These are people who accumulate gold and silver وَلَا يُنفِقُونَهَا فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ—"and they don't spend it in the path of Allah ﷻ."

The phrase فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ refers specifically to spending in Allah's path, which is distinct from general sadaqah. When Allah ﷻ designates spending "in His path," it carries particular weight and obligation.

Abu Dharr al-Ghifari (RA) was one of the most spiritual companions. Hasan al-Basri said that whoever wants to see the spiritual devotion of Isa (AS) should look at Abu Dharr (RA). When this ayah was revealed, Abu Dharr (RA) was deeply concerned. As the Muslim community grew wealthier through various conquests, he approached Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA) and mentioned this ayah about hoarding gold and silver, worried about its implications.

Umar (RA) responded that while the ayah speaks about the People of the Book, it serves as a warning for Muslims as well. This reflects how the companions viewed Quranic stories—not merely as history, but as mirrors held up to their own behavior. The camera is on us; the warning applies to us. We must view these passages the same way.

The ayah concludes with a stark warning: فَبَشِّرْهُم بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ—"then congratulate them with a painful punishment." The use of بَشِّرْ (congratulate) is deeply ironic, as this word typically brings good news, but here announces torment.

translation: The Day when it will be heated in the fire of Hell and seared therewith will be their foreheads, their flanks, and their backs, [it will be said], "This is what you hoarded for yourselves, so taste what you used to hoard
commentary:
يَوْمَ يُحْمَىٰ عَلَيْهَا فِى نَارِ جَهَنَّمَ فَتُكْوَىٰ بِهَا جِبَاهُهُمْ وَجُنُوبُهُمْ وَظُهُورُهُمْ ۖ هَـٰذَا مَا كَنَزْتُمْ لِأَنفُسِكُمْ فَذُوقُوا۟ مَا كُنتُمْ تَكْنِزُونَ

Allah ﷻ describes the Day of Judgment when this hoarded wealth becomes an instrument of punishment: يَوْمَ يُحْمَىٰ عَلَيْهَا فِى نَارِ جَهَنَّمَ—"The day on which it is heated in the fire of Jahannam."

Already these people are in the Fire, but something additional is being done to them. Their gold and silver—the very wealth they clung to—is melted down and poured upon them. How do you pour gold and silver? It must first be melted, turned into molten metal, then poured onto their bodies.

فَتُكْوَىٰ بِهَا describes the branding that follows. The word كَيّ in classical Arabic refers to taking metal, heating it until it turns red-hot, then pressing it against skin to leave a burning brand, a permanent scar. This is medical cauterization taken to an extreme level of torture.

The ayah specifies three parts of the body that are branded: جِبَاهُهُمْ—their wide foreheads, جُنُوبُهُمْ—their sides, and ظُهُورُهُمْ—their backs. Each part represents a different dimension of their existence branded by the consequences of their greed.

Then comes the statement that will echo in their consciousness for eternity: هَـٰذَا مَا كَنَزْتُمْ لِأَنفُسِكُمْ—"This is what you hoarded for yourselves." The wealth they thought they were securing, the gold and silver they believed would bring them comfort and security, has become the very source of their torment. What they treasured has turned into their punishment.

The ayah concludes: فَذُوقُوا۟ مَا كُنتُمْ تَكْنِزُونَ—"So taste what you've been hoarding." Taste the very thing you couldn't let go of. Taste what you prioritized over obedience to Allah ﷻ. Taste what you valued more than spending in His path.

The context of these ayahs is also significant. This is Surah At-Tawbah, revealed during the expedition to Tabuk, the ultimate emergency situation in the sirah of the Prophet ﷺ. When funds were desperately needed, it wasn't a casual request—it was an absolute emergency. For people to hold back wealth even in such dire circumstances meant the sternest warnings needed to be revealed.

translation: Indeed, the number of months with Allah is twelve [lunar] months in the register of Allah [from] the day He created the heavens and the earth; of these, four are sacred. That is the correct religion, so do not wrong yourselves during them. And fight against the disbelievers collectively as they fight against you collectively. And know that Allah is with the righteous [who fear Him]
commentary:
إِنَّ عِدَّةَ ٱلشُّهُورِ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ٱثْنَا عَشَرَ شَهْرًا فِى كِتَـٰبِ ٱللَّهِ يَوْمَ خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ مِنْهَآ أَرْبَعَةٌ حُرُمٌ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلدِّينُ ٱلْقَيِّمُ ۚ فَلَا تَظْلِمُوا۟ فِيهِنَّ أَنفُسَكُمْ ۚ وَقَـٰتِلُوا۟ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ كَآفَّةً كَمَا يُقَـٰتِلُونَكُمْ كَآفَّةً ۚ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ

Allah ﷻ establishes a fundamental principle about time itself: إِنَّ عِدَّةَ ٱلشُّهُورِ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ٱثْنَا عَشَرَ شَهْرًا—"No doubt, the legitimate count of months with Allah is twelve months." This isn't merely stating a fact; it's declaring that the twelve-month calendar is a divine revelation, not a human invention.

The ayah continues: فِى كِتَـٰبِ ٱللَّهِ يَوْمَ خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ—"in Allah's book, the day He created the heavens and the earth." This means the concept of a twelve-month calendar was established by Allah ﷻ at the very moment of creation. The lunar phases, the way the moon changes over the course of a month, the positioning that creates these cycles—all of this is Allah's design.

The calendar we follow isn't arbitrary. The relationship between the sun and moon, the way these celestial bodies move and create the passage of time—this is Allah's scheduling, His divine order built into the fabric of creation.

مِنْهَآ أَرْبَعَةٌ حُرُمٌ—"Of them, four months are sacred." These four sacred months (Dhul-Qi'dah, Dhul-Hijjah, Muharram, and Rajab) are designated as حُرُم—forbidden. What's forbidden in them is fighting and warfare. These months were meant to be times of peace, where conflicts would cease.

ذَٰلِكَ ٱلدِّينُ ٱلْقَيِّمُ—"That is the established religion." This phrase indicates that this is the official position, the unchangeable divine law. This declaration is especially important because of what's about to be mentioned in the next ayah regarding an-nasi', the practice that violated this sacred calendar.

The Quraysh, who were in charge of the Kaaba, considered themselves custodians of Arabian religion, and therefore they also controlled the calendar. When they found it convenient, they would manipulate the sacred months. If they wanted to wage war during a sacred month, they would simply move that sacred month to a different time and say, "We'll make this month sacred two months from now instead." This practice of postponing and rearranging the sacred months was called an-nasi'.

Allah ﷻ then commands: فَلَا تَظْلِمُوا۟ فِيهِنَّ أَنفُسَكُمْ—"So don't wrong yourselves in the matter of these months." The pronoun فِيهِنَّ uses the heavier feminine plural form rather than a lighter pronoun. Imam al-Qurtubi explains this grammatical choice indicates the gravity and weightiness of this matter. These months are no small thing; they carry significant spiritual weight.

The ayah then addresses the immediate situation: وَقَـٰتِلُوا۟ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ كَآفَّةً—"And fight the mushrikin all together without compromise." These are the very people who violated the sanctity of these months to begin with. The command uses كَآفَّةً, meaning totally, entirely, without exception.

كَمَا يُقَـٰتِلُونَكُمْ كَآفَّةً—"Just as they have been fighting you and will continue to fight you all together." The mushrikin had demonstrated their commitment to fighting the believers without compromise. They would never accept a peace treaty in good faith; they would never genuinely give in. Therefore, the believers must respond with equal resolve and never give in either.

The ayah concludes with a powerful assurance: وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ—"And you had better know that Allah is on the side of those who continue to protect themselves from disappointing Allah or dissipating Allah's commands." The word ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ refers to those who maintain taqwa—consciousness of Allah ﷻ, protecting themselves from His displeasure.

During Hajjat al-Wida (the Farewell Pilgrimage), the Prophet ﷺ made a significant statement: "The ages have come around full circle to be aligned with the way they were the day on which Allah created the heavens and the earth." This declaration was momentous. After years of the Quraysh manipulating and corrupting the calendar, throwing everything off balance, the calendar had finally returned to its proper alignment on the day of the Prophet's ﷺ final pilgrimage. From that moment, the calendar was properly reset, never to be tampered with again.

translation: Indeed, the postponing [of restriction within sacred months] is an increase in disbelief by which those who have disbelieved are led [further] astray. They make it lawful one year and unlawful another year to correspond to the number made unlawful by Allah and [thus] make lawful what Allah has made unlawful. Made pleasing to them is the evil of their deeds; and Allah does not guide the disbelieving people
commentary:
إِنَّمَا ٱلنَّسِىٓءُ زِيَادَةٌ فِى ٱلْكُفْرِ ۖ يُضَلُّ بِهِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يُحِلُّونَهُۥ عَامًا وَيُحَرِّمُونَهُۥ عَامًا لِّيُوَاطِـُٔوا۟ عِدَّةَ مَا حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ فَيُحِلُّوا۟ مَا حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ زُيِّنَ لَهُمْ سُوٓءُ أَعْمَـٰلِهِمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

Allah ﷻ now addresses directly the practice of an-nasi': إِنَّمَا ٱلنَّسِىٓءُ زِيَادَةٌ فِى ٱلْكُفْرِ—"Indeed, an-nasi' is an excess in disbelief." The word زِيَادَةٌ means an addition, an excess. The disbelievers were already far from Allah ﷻ—they didn't believe in Him, they had no regard for His House, but then they added this violation on top of everything else.

The sacred months that Allah ﷻ established weren't even something new that Islam introduced. The mushrikin already knew about these sacred months from the legacy of Ibrahim (AS). These months had been honored in Arabian society for generations. Yet they violated even this ancient, known sanctity. That's an additional act of disbelief, another layer of rejection of divine authority.

يُضَلُّ بِهِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟—"The disbelievers are misled as a result of it." They led themselves astray through this practice, creating their own misguidance.

يُحِلُّونَهُۥ عَامًا وَيُحَرِّمُونَهُۥ عَامًا—"They make it halal one year and haram another year." Their manipulation wasn't even consistent. Some years they would respect the sacred months; other years they would move them around according to their convenience and desires.

The word لِّيُوَاطِـُٔوا۟ is particularly significant. It comes from the root و ط أ, which literally means to tread upon the earth, to crush something under your feet. From this root comes the word مُوَاطَأَة, which means to deal with something however you want, without any code of conduct or principles guiding your behavior.

Imagine someone eating food. There are basic manners: eat with your right hand, take small bites that fit in your mouth. But someone doing muwata'ah with food would eat however he feels like it—stick his whole face in the plate, use his left hand, stuff enormous amounts in his mouth like a pie-eating contest. He has no code; he just does whatever comes into his head.

This is exactly what the mushrikin were doing with the calendar and with what Allah ﷻ made sacred: لِّيُوَاطِـُٔوا۟ عِدَّةَ مَا حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ—"so that they do whatever they want with the count of what Allah made forbidden."

فَيُحِلُّوا۟ مَا حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ—"So then they make halal what Allah made haram." If they're that self-indulged, able to manipulate sacred time itself to fit their desires, what's the big deal to them about violating any other divine command?

زُيِّنَ لَهُمْ سُوٓءُ أَعْمَـٰلِهِمْ—"The worst of their deeds have been beautified to them." This is the age we live in, where the ultimate authority for many people is themselves. When "I can do whatever I want" becomes the most glorified idea, people take pride in the worst things they do. You see this attitude everywhere—people bragging about speeding dangerously, nearly causing accidents, and thinking it's awesome. The worse the action, the more pride they take in it.

The ayah concludes: وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ—"And Allah does not guide the disbelieving nation." When a people establish themselves as their own ultimate authority, rejecting divine guidance at every level, Allah ﷻ does not guide them. They've chosen their path of misguidance.

These ayahs bring to a close the internal matters being addressed in the early part of Surah At-Tawbah. The passages we've covered expose the corruption within religious leadership, the hoarding of wealth, and the manipulation of sacred law to serve human desires. Now the surah transitions to its second major section—the international phase of the Prophet's ﷺ campaign, the outward mission that began in his lifetime and continued after through his companions (RA).

translation: O you who have believed, what is [the matter] with you that, when you are told to go forth in the cause of Allah, you adhere heavily to the earth? Are you satisfied with the life of this world rather than the Hereafter? But what is the enjoyment of worldly life compared to the Hereafter except a [very] little
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَا لَكُمْ إِذَا قِيلَ لَكُمُ ٱنفِرُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ٱثَّاقَلْتُمْ إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ أَرَضِيتُم بِٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا مِنَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ فَمَا مَتَـٰعُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ

Allah ﷻ begins with a searching question directed at the believers: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَا لَكُمْ – "O you who believe, what is wrong with you?" This rhetorical device مَا لَكُمْ carries the weight of divine disappointment. It's not the gentle call we sometimes hear in the Quran. This is Allah ﷻ calling His servants to account.

The phrase يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا typically introduces commands or guidance with mercy and kindness. But here it introduces a sharp rebuke. In certain places, this address comes with intensity: "Why do you say what you don't do?" "Why don't you actually believe?" This is one of those moments, and it teaches us something profound – when Allah ﷻ uses this phrase, He includes everyone who claims faith, even the hypocrites hidden among the believers.

إِذَا قِيلَ لَكُمُ ٱنفِرُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ – "When it is said to you, 'March forth in the path of Allah.'" The word انفِرُوا comes from نَفَر, which refers specifically to leaving for a military expedition. A نَفِير is a small army unit, and from this root we get the call to mobilize for the sake of Allah ﷻ.

ٱثَّاقَلْتُمْ إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ – "You become heavy toward the earth." This phrase carries layers of meaning. The root ثَقُلَ means to be heavy or weighed down, but the form اثَّاقَلْتُمْ implies pretending to be heavy, making yourself seem too burdened to move. Your feet become as if planted in the ground, too heavy to lift. There's an element of theatrical reluctance here – not actual inability, but performed helplessness.

Then comes the piercing diagnosis: أَرَضِيتُم بِٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا مِنَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ – "Have you become content with the worldly life instead of the Hereafter?"

This question lands with particular force because of who it's addressing. These aren't the hypocrites of Makkah who have no expectations of the afterlife. These are the believers who fought at Badr, who stood at Uhud, who witnessed the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, who entered Makkah in victory. Allah ﷻ is asking them: after everything you've been through, after all you've seen, you prefer this world?

The ayah concludes with devastating perspective: فَمَا مَتَـٰعُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ – "The enjoyment of worldly life compared to the Hereafter is nothing but insignificant." Whatever utilities, benefits and pleasures you think you're preserving by staying behind – what are they really worth measured against eternal life? The word مَتَاع refers to temporary provisions, utilities that serve a purpose but don't last. Against the permanence of the Hereafter, all of it becomes قَلِيل – minuscule, negligible, not worth consideration.

translation: If you do not go forth, He will punish you with a painful punishment and will replace you with another people, and you will not harm Him at all. And Allah is over all things competent
commentary:
إِلَّا تَنفِرُوا۟ يُعَذِّبْكُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا وَيَسْتَبْدِلْ قَوْمًا غَيْرَكُمْ وَلَا تَضُرُّوهُ شَيْـًٔا ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

Now comes the warning: إِلَّا تَنفِرُوا۟ يُعَذِّبْكُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا – "If you do not march forth, He will punish you with a painful punishment."

You're worried about what the Romans might do to you? That's not the punishment you should fear. The real consequence, the real pain, will come from Allah ﷻ if you refuse this call. But the ayah doesn't stop there – that's not even the most devastating part of the warning.

وَيَسْتَبْدِلْ قَوْمًا غَيْرَكُمْ – "And He will replace you with a people other than you."

Let this sink in. These are the Companions of the Prophet ﷺ being told this. These are the people who sacrificed everything for Islam, who were tortured in Makkah, who migrated with nothing, who stood on the battlefield multiple times. They had just witnessed the conquest of Makkah. They could have thought: we are the chosen generation, the nation of Muhammad ﷺ, the ones Allah ﷻ gave victory. We are secure in our position.

Allah ﷻ shatters that illusion completely. No one is irreplaceable in His plan. If this blessed generation can be threatened with replacement, what does that mean for us? If we don't step up and fulfill our responsibilities as an Ummah, this warning applies to us even more severely.

This isn't theoretical. History has already shown us this principle in action. When the Tatars swept through Iraq and the Muslim lands, they committed unspeakable atrocities. The descriptions in history books are so graphic they're difficult to read – piles of scholars' heads, libraries burned, entire populations devastated. These invaders hated Islam with a fierce hatred. Yet by the end of it all, they became Muslim. Their descendants became Muslim rulers – the Mughals trace their lineage back to these very Tatars. Allah ﷻ replaced one group of Muslims with another entirely, bringing the faith through the very people who had tried to destroy it.

And this wasn't the first time Allah ﷻ had demonstrated this principle. Before the Sahabah, there was Bani Israel. They were chosen, given prophet after prophet, miracle after miracle. When they failed to fulfill their covenant, Allah ﷻ raised the Arabs – who had been idol worshippers in the desert – to carry the message of Tawhid to the world.

وَلَا تَضُرُّوهُ شَيْـًٔا – "And you will not harm Him at all."

The pronoun "Him" here doesn't refer to Allah ﷻ – it refers to the Prophet ﷺ. Allah ﷻ is addressing a potential misunderstanding. When someone in authority threatens punishment, it often reveals their own weakness or desperation. A parent who says, "If you don't do this, I'll punish you!" might be revealing that they need you to comply because they have no other options.

Allah ﷻ preempts this thought immediately. Don't think for a moment that this mission depends on you. Don't think the Prophet ﷺ will fail if you don't show up. Your absence won't harm him in the slightest.

وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ – "And Allah has complete power over everything." The mission will succeed. The only question is whether you'll be part of that success or replaced by those who will be.

translation: If you do not aid the Prophet - Allah has already aided him when those who disbelieved had driven him out [of Makkah] as one of two, when they were in the cave and he said to his companion, "Do not grieve; indeed Allah is with us." And Allah sent down his tranquillity upon him and supported him with angels you did not see and made the word of those who disbelieved the lowest, while the word of Allah - that is the highest. And Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise
commentary:
إِلَّا تَنصُرُوهُ فَقَدْ نَصَرَهُ ٱللَّهُ إِذْ أَخْرَجَهُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ثَانِىَ ٱثْنَيْنِ إِذْ هُمَا فِى ٱلْغَارِ إِذْ يَقُولُ لِصَـٰحِبِهِۦ لَا تَحْزَنْ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَنَا ۖ فَأَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ سَكِينَتَهُۥ عَلَيْهِ وَأَيَّدَهُۥ بِجُنُودٍ لَّمْ تَرَوْهَا وَجَعَلَ كَلِمَةَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱلسُّفْلَىٰ ۗ وَكَلِمَةُ ٱللَّهِ هِىَ ٱلْعُلْيَا ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ

إِلَّا تَنصُرُوهُ فَقَدْ نَصَرَهُ ٱللَّهُ – "If you do not help him, then indeed Allah has already helped him."

To drive the point home, Allah ﷻ reminds them of a moment when the Prophet ﷺ had no army, no supporters, no resources whatsoever. This isn't the first time he's been alone.

إِذْ أَخْرَجَهُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ثَانِىَ ٱثْنَيْنِ – "When those who disbelieved expelled him, the second of two."

This is the Hijrah, the migration from Makkah to Madinah. But look at how Allah ﷻ describes it – not as a strategic retreat or a planned migration, but as an expulsion. The disbelievers forced him out. And in that moment of apparent defeat, he had only one companion with him.

ثَانِىَ ٱثْنَيْنِ – "The second of two" – this is one of the beautiful titles given to Abu Bakr as-Siddiq (RA) in the Quran. He was the one person with the Prophet ﷺ in the cave.

إِذْ هُمَا فِى ٱلْغَارِ – "When they were in the cave." The Cave of Thawr, where they hid from the Makkan search parties. Imagine that scene: the two of them concealed in a cave, while their enemies scour the mountain looking for them. The search party comes so close that Abu Bakr (RA) can see the glint of their swords. If just one of them looked down, it would be over.

إِذْ يَقُولُ لِصَـٰحِبِهِۦ لَا تَحْزَنْ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَنَا – "When he said to his companion, 'Do not grieve; indeed Allah is with us.'"

In that moment of maximum vulnerability, with death literally standing above them, the Prophet ﷺ tells Abu Bakr (RA) to relax. لَا تَحْزَنْ – don't be anxious, don't worry. And then he states with absolute conviction: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَنَا – "Indeed, Allah is with us."

Not "might be" or "hopefully" or "I think" – إِنَّ, the emphatic particle of certainty. Allah is with us. No doubt about it.

فَأَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ سَكِينَتَهُۥ عَلَيْهِ – "Then Allah sent down His tranquility upon him."

The word سَكِينَة means tranquility or peace, but notice that Allah ﷻ makes it possessive: سَكِينَتَهُ – "His sakinah." This isn't ordinary calm. This is a special tranquility that comes directly from Allah ﷻ, unlike any other peace a person might experience. In that cave, with every reason to be terrified, Allah ﷻ wrapped His Messenger ﷺ in divine peace.

وَأَيَّدَهُۥ بِجُنُودٍ لَّمْ تَرَوْهَا – "And supported him with soldiers you did not see."

Angels. An angelic army came to the aid of the Prophet ﷺ when he had no human army. The same Allah ﷻ who sent invisible reinforcements then can send them now. Your 30,000 are not what will determine victory.

وَجَعَلَ كَلِمَةَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱلسُّفْلَىٰ – "And He made the word of those who disbelieved the lowest."

The root س ف ل gives us سُفْلَى, meaning the lowest position. Their word, their power, their authority – all of it was brought down. But notice the grammatical precision in what comes next.

وَكَلِمَةُ ٱللَّهِ هِىَ ٱلْعُلْيَا – "And the word of Allah – it is the highest."

The first part says He made their word low, which implies a change in state – it was high, then He brought it down. But Allah's word? It is العُلْيَا – the highest. Not "He made it high" but "it is high." It was never anything else. There was never a moment when Allah's word wasn't supreme. The grammatical structure itself establishes that Allah's word exists in permanent elevation.

وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ – "And Allah is Mighty and Wise." He has complete authority (عَزِيز) and perfect wisdom (حَكِيم) in how He executes His plans. That's who is running this mission. That's whose word is being carried. So what are you really worried about?

translation: Go forth, whether light or heavy, and strive with your wealth and your lives in the cause of Allah. That is better for you, if you only knew
commentary:
ٱنفِرُوا۟ خِفَافًا وَثِقَالًا وَجَـٰهِدُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِكُمْ وَأَنفُسِكُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ

ٱنفِرُوا۟ خِفَافًا وَثِقَالًا – "March forth, light or heavy."

After establishing divine support and demolishing excuses, Allah ﷻ issues the command directly. خِفَاف and ثِقَال can be understood in multiple ways: young or old, healthy or sick, wealthy or poor, experienced or inexperienced, enthusiastic or reluctant. Whatever state you're in – come forward.

"I'm too old to fight." Come anyway. "I'm too young." Come anyway. "I don't have money." Come anyway. "I have no experience." Come anyway. "I have responsibilities." Come anyway.

This is a universal principle that extends far beyond the Battle of Tabuk. For any struggle in the path of Islam, the call is the same: whatever you have to offer, offer it. Don't disqualify yourself.

There's a beautiful incident that illustrates this. Abdullah ibn Mas'ud (RA) was climbing a tree, and some of the Companions were laughing at his thin legs. The Prophet ﷺ defended him, saying those legs were heavier on the Scale than the mountain of Uhud. One Companion had such little facial hair that others made fun of the single hair on his chin. The Prophet ﷺ said that single hair was worth more than their beards. It's not about what you appear to have – it's about showing up.

وَجَـٰهِدُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِكُمْ وَأَنفُسِكُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ – "And strive with your wealth and your lives in the path of Allah."

Both are required. Your financial contribution and your physical presence. Your money and yourself. Whatever capacity you have in either realm, bring it to the path of Allah ﷻ.

ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ – "That is better for you, if you only knew."

From every visible, practical angle, this expedition looks like disaster. But Allah ﷻ says it is خَيْر – better, good for you. If you only knew – if you had real knowledge, if you understood the reality beyond appearances – you would see that this hardship is in your own best interest.

This is the nature of working for Allah's deen. Organizations measure success with flowcharts and graphs. Last year we had this many sales, this year we increased by this percentage. Humanitarian groups show you exactly how many people they fed, how many wells they built, how much impact they had. When you contribute to any cause, you want to see progress reports: What have you accomplished? What are your financial standings? Show me the data.

But the work of the prophets (AS) doesn't follow that model. If Prophet Nuh (AS) presented his annual progress report, what would it show? "No converts this year. No converts for the last 20 years. After 30 years, no progress. After 40 years, still nothing. After 100 years of work, we have one person. After 200 years, we got two more. We've doubled our numbers!" By worldly metrics, it would be considered an absolute failure. Shut down this operation immediately.

But that's not how prophetic work is measured. You don't gauge the success of carrying Allah's message by visible, tangible progress. All around us are reasons to believe Islam will never flourish. Watch the news, look at social media, see what's happening to Muslim youth, witness the state of the Ummah. From every angle, it looks hopeless. And you know what? We're not the first generation to feel this way. The Companions felt it too. That's exactly what these ayahs are addressing.

translation: Had it been an easy gain and a moderate trip, the hypocrites would have followed you, but distant to them was the journey. And they will swear by Allah, "If we were able, we would have gone forth with you," destroying themselves [through false oaths], and Allah knows that indeed they are liars
commentary:
لَوْ كَانَ عَرَضًا قَرِيبًا وَسَفَرًا قَاصِدًا لَّٱتَّبَعُوكَ وَلَـٰكِنۢ بَعُدَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلشُّقَّةُ ۚ وَسَيَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ لَوِ ٱسْتَطَعْنَا لَخَرَجْنَا مَعَكُمْ يُهْلِكُونَ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ إِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ

لَوْ كَانَ عَرَضًا قَرِيبًا – "If it had been an immediate gain."

The word عَرَض refers to goods or provisions that you acquire, but specifically perishable goods – things that don't last. It's used for temporary supplies, utilities with a short shelf life. So the word itself contains the meaning of something that can be obtained, but won't last long. If there had been immediate, tangible, even if temporary gain...

وَسَفَرًا قَاصِدًا – "And a moderate journey."

The word قَاصِد comes from the root ق ص د, which means to be balanced or moderate. We see this root in قَصْد in the ayah وَاقْصِدْ فِي مَشْيِكَ – "Be moderate in your walking." Don't walk too fast or too slow, don't stomp on the earth, just walk normally. So سَفَرًا قَاصِدًا means an average journey, a typical trip, nothing extreme.

لَّٱتَّبَعُوكَ – "They would have followed you."

If they could see the projections, if the destination was close, if the difficulty was minimal, if the return on investment was clear and immediate – then yes, they would have come. They would have said, "Okay, that makes sense. I'll participate."

وَلَـٰكِنۢ بَعُدَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلشُّقَّةُ – "But the distance was too great for them."

The word شُقَّة carries the meaning of difficulty, exhaustion and hardship combined with distance. It was too far, too hard, too uncertain. 300 miles in the desert heat, financing themselves, facing an unknown enemy, leaving their harvest behind – the difficulty of this journey was beyond what they were willing to accept.

وَسَيَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ لَوِ ٱسْتَطَعْنَا لَخَرَجْنَا مَعَكُمْ – "And they will swear by Allah, 'If we were able, we would have gone out with you.'"

These same people who stayed behind will make elaborate excuses. They'll swear by Allah ﷻ Himself to add weight to their claims: "I swear to God, if I could have come, I would have. You have to believe me. It's just that I couldn't. I'm so sorry. You know how it is. Maybe next time. Next season. When things are better. But not right now. I just can't do it."

يُهْلِكُونَ أَنفُسَهُمْ – "They destroy themselves."

With these false oaths, with these manufactured excuses, with this performance of helplessness – they are destroying their own souls. The word يُهْلِكُون is in the present continuous tense, meaning they are actively, continuously destroying themselves with every oath they swear, with every excuse they manufacture.

وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ إِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ – "And Allah knows that indeed they are liars."

This is the most direct callout of hypocrisy in the entire Quran up to this point. Allah ﷻ doesn't say "they might be lying" or "perhaps they're not truthful." He says إِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ – with the emphatic إِنَّ and the intensifying لام, "they are absolutely, certainly, undoubtedly liars."

When they say "I cannot," Allah ﷻ declares: yes, you can, and you're lying. Allah ﷻ knows their true capacity and their true intentions. This isn't about ability – it's about willingness.

Reading about the munafiqun (hypocrites) should be a terrifying experience for every believer. The Prophet ﷺ taught us that nobody feels safe from hypocrisy except the hypocrite himself. Only a true believer fears that they might have traces of hypocrisy in their heart. If these passages don't make us examine ourselves, don't make us question whether we're making similar excuses in our own commitments to Islam, then we have a serious problem. And if we don't think we have the problem, that's often the ultimate sign that we do.

When Allah ﷻ calls us to His path, in whatever form that takes in our lives, we should ask ourselves: Are we making excuses? Are we waiting for the "moderate journey" and the "immediate gain"? Are we holding back until we can see the tangible results, the guaranteed outcomes, the convenient timing? Or are we answering the call خِفَافًا وَثِقَالًا – regardless of our state, trusting that Allah ﷻ will support His own mission, just as He supported His Messenger ﷺ when he had nothing but one companion in a cave?

translation: May Allah pardon you, [O Muhammad]; why did you give them permission [to remain behind]? [You should not have] until it was evident to you who were truthful and you knew [who were] the liars
commentary:
عَفَا ٱللَّهُ عَنكَ لِمَ أَذِنتَ لَهُمْ حَتَّىٰ يَتَبَيَّنَ لَكَ ٱلَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا۟ وَتَعْلَمَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ

"May Allah ﷻ pardon you! Why did you give them permission until those who were truthful became clear to you and you knew the liars?"

This remarkable ayah opens with Allah ﷻ pardoning the Prophet ﷺ before even mentioning what requires pardon. The structure itself is merciful—عَفَا ٱللَّهُ عَنكَ (Allah has pardoned you) comes first, establishing divine forgiveness immediately. Only then does Allah ﷻ ask: Why did you give them permission?

To understand this ayah, we must recognize the historical context. After years of dealing with the hypocrites in Medina, this expedition represented a golden opportunity to finally expose them. Previously, when battles were not individually mandated, hypocrites could simply avoid participation without explanation. They didn't need to come to the Prophet ﷺ with excuses about being busy or having other commitments. No explanation was necessary.

But now, with this open draft, everything changed. People began approaching the Prophet ﷺ with excuses: "My wife just got sick," "I have family obligations," and various other justifications. Here we see the Prophet's natural disposition—by his very nature, would he accuse someone of lying? Would he say, "No, you're lying"? Of course not. He would accept their word and grant permission.

Yet Allah ﷻ reveals that a different approach was needed: لِمَ أَذِنتَ لَهُمْ (Why did you give them permission?). The Prophet ﷺ should have taken a firm stance. No one should have been let go easily. Everyone should have been told they must go. The policy should have been to not accept excuses from anybody حَتَّىٰ يَتَبَيَّنَ لَكَ ٱلَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا۟ (until those who were truthful became clear to you) وَتَعْلَمَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (and you knew the liars). The Muslims needed to know exactly who was telling the truth and to expose the liars completely.

The hypocrites had figured out that the Prophet ﷺ was approachable, that he listened without assuming evil of people. They viewed this as gullibility. Later in the surah, they even insult him by calling him "all ears"—meaning someone who just listens without judging or questioning. This phrase "he's all ears" carried the insult of being mindless, someone who accepts everything without critique. But what they mistook for weakness was actually the Prophet's noble character of thinking well of people.

translation: Those who believe in Allah and the Last Day would not ask permission of you to be excused from striving with their wealth and their lives. And Allah is Knowing of those who fear Him
commentary:
لَا يَسْتَـْٔذِنُكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ أَن يُجَـٰهِدُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلْمُتَّقِينَ

"Those who believe in Allah ﷻ and the Last Day do not ask your permission to be excused from striving with their wealth and their lives. And Allah ﷻ is Knowing of those who fear Him."

This ayah establishes a powerful principle: لَا يَسْتَـْٔذِنُكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ (Those who believe in Allah and the Last Day do not ask your permission). The true believers wouldn't even come to ask for exemption. Why? Because they understood this was a matter of Islam's very survival, the ultimate state of emergency.

This wasn't a time to bring up allergy problems or personal inconveniences. Whatever personal issues existed, the believers understood they were nothing compared to the emergency at hand. They couldn't bring themselves in good conscience to ask permission when the entire mission and community faced such stakes.

This connects to guidance found elsewhere in the Quran. In Surah An-Nur, we find the opposite scenario—there, seeking permission is presented as a sign of iman. The believers are described as those who, when with the Prophet ﷺ on some collective matter, would never leave without asking permission. How do we reconcile these apparently contradictory teachings?

The answer lies in context. Surah An-Nur addresses normal circumstances—regular community affairs, planned expeditions where the Prophet ﷺ might say, "We should travel to this tribe in a couple of weeks." In such situations, someone could appropriately say, "Ya Rasulallah, I need three weeks instead of two. Is that acceptable?" That's normal protocol, the standard discipline of community organization.

But this situation in Surah At-Tawbah represents life and death, a state of emergency. In a life-and-death situation, those who come asking permission reveal that their personal matters outweigh the survival of the entire mission and community. It becomes proof of a fundamental lack of loyalty.

This distinction is essential for how we apply these principles today. The guidance for normal circumstances is found in Surah An-Nur; the guidance for emergency situations is here in Surah At-Tawbah. We cannot impose emergency protocols on regular organizational activities. A youth group meeting at 8:00 PM, a conference program, a regular community gathering—these are not life-and-death emergencies requiring the Tawbah standard. Emergency situation here specifically means Islam's survival, actual life and death circumstances.

The ayah concludes with وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (And Allah is Knowing of those who fear Him). Those with true taqwa wouldn't bring themselves to ask for exemption because they fear Allah ﷻ more than any worldly inconvenience.

translation: Only those would ask permission of you who do not believe in Allah and the Last Day and whose hearts have doubted, and they, in their doubt, are hesitating
commentary:
إِنَّمَا يَسْتَـْٔذِنُكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَٱرْتَابَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ فَهُمْ فِى رَيْبِهِمْ يَتَرَدَّدُونَ

"Only those ask your permission who do not believe in Allah ﷻ and the Last Day, and whose hearts are in doubt, and they waver in their doubt."

Allah ﷻ now exposes the reality of those seeking permission: إِنَّمَا يَسْتَـْٔذِنُكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ (The only people asking your permission are the ones who don't believe in Allah and the Last Day). The word إِنَّمَا provides emphasis—"only" these people, exclusively these people, are the ones coming with excuses.

But the ayah goes deeper into their psychological state: وَٱرْتَابَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ (and their hearts are filled with doubt). They're riddled with doubt, consumed by uncertainty. فَهُمْ فِى رَيْبِهِمْ يَتَرَدَّدُونَ (And it is in their doubts that they keep going back and forth).

Imagine their internal struggle: "Should we go? If we don't go, the Muslims will know where we really stand. What will happen? What policy will Allah ﷻ reveal about us? Maybe we should just go. But going means we might actually get killed by the Romans!"

They faced pressure from multiple directions. On one hand, there was the military danger—the actual battle with a powerful enemy. On the other hand, there were social consequences—potential boycott, exposure, the community knowing their true allegiance. And the threats from Allah ﷻ weren't light either. Later in this passage, Allah ﷻ instructs the Muslims to tell the hypocrites: "It may be that Allah will give you trouble from Himself, or at our hands." This threat alone—though it was never actually carried out, as the Companions (RA) were never given permission to attack the hypocrites—was enough to shake them up.

So they hesitated, going back and forth in their doubt, paralyzed between competing fears, unable to commit one way or the other. This wavering itself exposes the absence of conviction, the lack of true belief that would make the decision clear and immediate.

translation: And if they had intended to go forth, they would have prepared for it [some] preparation. But Allah disliked their being sent, so He kept them back, and they were told, "Remain [behind] with those who remain
commentary:
۞ وَلَوْ أَرَادُوا۟ ٱلْخُرُوجَ لَأَعَدُّوا۟ لَهُۥ عُدَّةً وَلَـٰكِن كَرِهَ ٱللَّهُ ٱنۢبِعَاثَهُمْ فَثَبَّطَهُمْ وَقِيلَ ٱقْعُدُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلْقَـٰعِدِينَ

"And if they had intended to go forth, they would have prepared for it some preparation. But Allah ﷻ disliked their being sent forth, so He detained them, and it was said, 'Remain with those who remain.'"

This ayah masterfully calls out the bluff of excuse-makers. Picture the scene: someone comes with an elaborate excuse at the very last minute—two hours before the army is about to depart. He doesn't mention anything a week before or a month before. He waits until the final moment. Why? Because at that point, there's no time to investigate his claim. The leadership has no resources to verify anything because more pressing matters demand attention—the actual departure.

He arrives and says: "I was completely ready to go. My bags were packed. I've been preparing for a week. But you won't believe what just happened—something completely unforeseen, completely out of my control." His entire argument rests on this claim: "I really wanted to come. I was so ready. This last-minute thing just happened."

Allah ﷻ exposes the lie with devastating simplicity: وَلَوْ أَرَادُوا۟ ٱلْخُرُوجَ لَأَعَدُّوا۟ لَهُۥ عُدَّةً (If they had really wanted to go forth, they would have prepared for it some preparation). Let's check their houses. Let's see how much packing they actually did. The reality? They had done no packing whatsoever. Not even a backpack ready at home. Nothing. The claim of preparation was completely fabricated.

Then comes the profound statement: وَلَـٰكِن كَرِهَ ٱللَّهُ ٱنۢبِعَاثَهُمْ (But Allah disliked their being sent forth). The word كَرِهَ indicates strong dislike, even disgust. Allah ﷻ was disgusted at the very idea of them rising up for this army, of them being appointed to join this expedition. Allah ﷻ did not want to contaminate His army with this filth. That's why He allowed these lame excuses to work, why He permitted them to stay behind.

فَثَبَّطَهُمْ (So He detained them). The verb ثَبَّطَ is powerful—it means to slow someone down and then stop them completely. There's a distinction in Arabic between concepts: when disease slows you down (أَثْبَتَ), versus when disease stops you in your tracks (ثَبَّطَ). Allah ﷻ didn't just slow them—He stopped them completely, wouldn't let them move forward at all.

Notice the rhetorical shift: Allah ﷻ begins by saying He disliked their going forth, He detained them—active voice with Allah ﷻ as the subject. But then suddenly: وَقِيلَ (and it was said)—passive voice. Why the switch? Because they don't deserve to be directly addressed by Allah ﷻ anymore. The command comes in passive form: ٱقْعُدُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلْقَـٰعِدِينَ (Sit with those who sit).

The word choice here is particularly striking. The term ٱلْقَـٰعِدِينَ (those who sit/remain) was actually used in battle times for women and children—those who would stay behind while the men went to fight. The phrasing itself suggests: "You sit with the women and children. That's where you belong." When Allah ﷻ calls someone out, He really calls them out.

translation: Had they gone forth with you, they would not have increased you except in confusion, and they would have been active among you, seeking [to cause] you fitnah. And among you are avid listeners to them. And Allah is Knowing of the wrongdoers
commentary:
لَوْ خَرَجُوا۟ فِيكُم مَّا زَادُوكُمْ إِلَّا خَبَالًا وَلَأَوْضَعُوا۟ خِلَـٰلَكُمْ يَبْغُونَكُمُ ٱلْفِتْنَةَ وَفِيكُمْ سَمَّـٰعُونَ لَهُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

"Had they gone forth with you, they would have added to you nothing but confusion. And they would have hastened about in your midst, seeking to cause dissent among you. And among you are avid listeners to them. And Allah ﷻ is Knowing of the wrongdoers."

This ayah reveals exactly why Allah ﷻ didn't want them in the army. It's a continuation of the metaphor from the famous hadith: "The believers are like a body." The Muslim army functions like a body, and these hypocrites would have caused nothing but malfunction in various limbs—creating partial problems here, there and everywhere throughout the ranks.

لَوْ خَرَجُوا۟ فِيكُم مَّا زَادُوكُمْ إِلَّا خَبَالًا (Had they gone forth with you, they would have added to you nothing but confusion). The word خَبَالًا is significant. It comes from a root meaning malfunction—when a limb of your body doesn't work properly. Your hand keeps shaking uncontrollably, your arm has trouble moving, you're so sick you can't walk straight—this is khabal. Applied to the army, it means they would go around causing malfunctions in different parts of the Muslim forces, problems everywhere they went.

How would they do this? وَلَأَوْضَعُوا۟ خِلَـٰلَكُمْ (And they would have hastened about in your midst). The verb أَوْضَعُوا is fascinating. Originally it meant to put something down. But in the context of riding, it refers to pushing down the neck of your horse or camel to make it run faster—like leaning forward on a motorcycle to increase speed.

Picture a massive caravan of 30,000 people traveling slowly, as large groups must. The caravan stretches long with clusters of people here and there—small groups on different camels, different sections unable to communicate with those far ahead or behind. These hypocrites would ride their horses quickly between groups, spreading poison as they went.

They'd approach one group: "Man, isn't it hot? I can't believe we're taking on the Romans. We're going to get killed. What do you think?" The Muslims don't see a "hypocrite" label on his forehead—he's just one of the guys. But he starts complaining about the weather, about how they're doomed to die, about how they should have waited just one more day to harvest fruit. He plants these seeds of doubt and despair.

Then he says, "Okay, I'll talk to you later," and speeds his horse to another part of the caravan: "You know what those guys back there are saying? They're saying we're all going to get killed. They're feeling really hot. Are you guys feeling hot too? Anyway, I'll talk to you later." Then he returns to the first group, goes to a third group, constantly moving back and forth—خِلَـٰلَكُمْ (in your midst), weaving through the ranks.

Pretty soon, the entire army feels like everybody thinks this is a bad idea—when actually just one person created that impression. The worst thing you can do to an army is destroy morale, and that's exactly what these individuals would have accomplished.

يَبْغُونَكُمُ ٱلْفِتْنَةَ (Seeking to cause dissent among you). Fitna—creating division, discord, doubt. This was their real agenda, what they desperately wanted to achieve.

Then comes a sobering reality: وَفِيكُمْ سَمَّـٰعُونَ لَهُمْ (And among you are avid listeners to them). This phrase has two main interpretations, both significant:

First interpretation: When these hypocrites come and spread their poison, some people actually listen carefully and repeatedly. The phrase suggests listening over and over, paying close attention. When they talk their trash, some find it makes sense. They don't reject it or recognize it as harmful—instead, they absorb it, entertain it, allow it to affect them. Allah ﷻ identifies this as wrong too. Both are wrongdoers—the one causing the fitna and the one allowing them to talk and spread it without resistance. Why are you letting him speak like this? You should have shut him down immediately, made it clear his words have no place here.

Second interpretation: These hypocrites actually kept certain people from different groups close to them—maintained relationships specifically to gather intelligence on the Muslims. You have spies among you. There are people who listen and report back, who work for the hypocrites, feeding them information about the believers' plans, morale and movements.

The ayah concludes with وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (And Allah is Knowing of the wrongdoers). Allah ﷻ knows exactly who the wrongdoers are—both the hypocrites spreading corruption and those who enable them through passive listening or active collaboration. Nothing is hidden from His knowledge, and His wisdom in keeping them out of the army becomes crystal clear: their presence would have been pure liability, not asset. They would have weakened the Muslims from within, precisely when strength and unity were most desperately needed.

translation: They had already desired dissension before and had upset matters for you until the truth came and the ordinance of Allah appeared, while they were averse
commentary:
لَقَدِ ٱبْتَغَوُا۟ ٱلْفِتْنَةَ مِن قَبْلُ وَقَلَّبُوا۟ لَكَ ٱلْأُمُورَ حَتَّىٰ جَآءَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَظَهَرَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ وَهُمْ كَـٰرِهُونَ

This ayah draws our attention to a pattern of behavior from the hypocrites. Allah ﷻ declares that they had tried to create fitnah from before, making clear that their troublemaking isn't new. There's a powerful connection here to Surah An-Nur, where we see the concrete manifestation of this fitnah—the slander against Aisha (RA).

The phrase وَقَلَّبُوا۟ لَكَ ٱلْأُمُورَ (they turned your matters upside down) captures the devastating impact of scandal. When allegations were raised against the Prophet's ﷺ household, everything became chaotic. The entire community was thrown into turmoil. This teaches us something profound about the nature of slander: it doesn't just affect one person or one issue—it turns everything upside down.

The ayah continues: حَتَّىٰ جَآءَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَظَهَرَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ (until the truth came and the decision of Allah was made manifest). When Aisha's (RA) innocence was finally declared by Allah ﷻ Himself through revelation, her response demonstrates remarkable clarity about where truth comes from. When her mother brought her the news, she said she should thank Allah ﷻ, not the Prophet ﷺ, because he didn't prove her innocence—Allah ﷻ did. Her words during that crisis are golden. With her father and husband both upset with her despite her complete innocence, she stated: "If I say something, you'll call me a liar. If I don't say anything, I'll be considered guilty. So I have to take my matter to Allah ﷻ."

This captures the cruel reality of false accusations. When someone's reputation is tarnished, they're trapped. Silence appears to be an admission of guilt, while defending oneself can seem like desperate justification. The dominant assumption becomes guilt, not innocence. This is why tarnishing someone's reputation is among the worst things you can do to them. Once the damage is done, it's extraordinarily difficult to undo.

The ayah ends with وَهُمْ كَـٰرِهُونَ (and they were disgusted by that). The hypocrites hated seeing their plot fail and the truth prevail.

translation: And among them is he who says, "Permit me [to remain at home] and do not put me to trial." Unquestionably, into trial they have fallen. And indeed, Hell will encompass the disbelievers
commentary:
وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَقُولُ ٱئْذَن لِّى وَلَا تَفْتِنِّىٓ ۚ أَلَا فِى ٱلْفِتْنَةِ سَقَطُوا۟ ۗ وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمُحِيطَةٌۢ بِٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

This ayah presents one of the most pathetic excuses offered to avoid participating in the expedition to Tabuk. Al-Jadd ibn Qays came to the Prophet ﷺ and essentially said, "You know, I just can't help myself around beautiful women, and along the way to Tabuk there are villages with really beautiful women. So don't put me in fitnah—let me stay home." It's perhaps the lamest excuse imaginable.

The Prophet ﷺ gave him permission to stay, which was actually wise, because such a person would only be a liability if he went. But Allah ﷻ responds to this excuse with devastating clarity: أَلَا فِى ٱلْفِتْنَةِ سَقَطُوا۟ (Haven't they already fallen into fitnah?). Before he even opened his mouth to make this excuse, he was already drowning in fitnah. The word سَقَطُوا۟ (they fell) suggests they've already collapsed into it.

The ayah concludes: وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمُحِيطَةٌۢ بِٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (And indeed, Hellfire completely encircles the disbelievers). Notice the shift—the ayah doesn't say "the hypocrites" but الْكَـٰفِرِينَ (the disbelievers). This is significant. Some scholars noted that before this surah, there were three categories: believers, disbelievers, and hypocrites. After this surah exposed them so thoroughly, the hypocrites were essentially reclassified as disbelievers. They were called out and their true nature revealed.

translation: If good befalls you, it distresses them; but if disaster strikes you, they say, "We took our matter [in hand] before," and turn away while they are rejoicing
commentary:
إِن تُصِبْكَ حَسَنَةٌ تَسُؤْهُمْ ۖ وَإِن تُصِبْكَ مُصِيبَةٌ يَقُولُوا۟ قَدْ أَخَذْنَآ أَمْرَنَا مِن قَبْلُ وَيَتَوَلَّوا۟ وَّهُمْ فَرِحُونَ

This ayah captures the twisted mindset of the hypocrites toward the Prophet ﷺ and the believers. The word تُصِبْكَ comes from the root meaning to shoot an arrow that hits its target. When something "hits" you, it was meant for you—it didn't arrive by accident. While this word is commonly used for calamities, it's also used for good things that reach you.

إِن تُصِبْكَ حَسَنَةٌ تَسُؤْهُمْ (If something good targets you, they hate it). When good comes to the Muslims, the hypocrites can't stand it. They were hoping things wouldn't work out. For example, when the Muslims left for Hudaybiyyah to perform pilgrimage, the hypocrites were overjoyed, thinking this was the end. The Muslims were going unarmed to the very people who had previously surrounded them in Medina during the Battle of the Trench. Abdullah ibn Ubayy was so confident the Muslims would perish that he began reconstructing his position of leadership in Medina.

Allah ﷻ describes this attitude in Surah Al-Fath: "You were convinced that the Messenger and the believers would never return to their families, and that idea was beautified in your hearts." When the Muslims returned safely, the hypocrites were devastated.

وَإِن تُصِبْكَ مُصِيبَةٌ (And if a calamity does hit you), يَقُولُوا۟ قَدْ أَخَذْنَآ أَمْرَنَا مِن قَبْلُ (they say, "We took our matter into our own hands beforehand"). The phrase قَدْ أَخَذْنَآ أَمْرَنَا means they took precautions, that they handled their affairs wisely by not going. It's the "I told you so" mentality—trash talking after the fact, saying, "See? That's why we said we shouldn't go."

وَيَتَوَلَّوا۟ وَّهُمْ فَرِحُونَ (And then they would turn away while they are rejoicing). They would actually celebrate at the loss of the Muslims, rejoicing inside at their misfortune.

translation: Say, "Never will we be struck except by what Allah has decreed for us; He is our protector." And upon Allah let the believers rely
commentary:
قُل لَّن يُصِيبَنَآ إِلَّا مَا كَتَبَ ٱللَّهُ لَنَا هُوَ مَوْلَىٰنَا ۚ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ

This ayah provides the Muslim response to such difficult situations. The believers are mentally prepared—victory isn't guaranteed when they go forth. Whatever trials may come are in Allah's ﷻ hands. But whether they go or return, this is what they must tell themselves.

قُل لَّن يُصِيبَنَآ إِلَّا مَا كَتَبَ ٱللَّهُ لَنَا (Say: Nothing will afflict us except what Allah has written for us). Notice the word لَنَا—for us, not علينا—against us. The expected language would be "written against us," but Allah ﷻ uses "for us." This is profound. Even when something difficult happens, the believer is taught to see it as written in their favor. If a problem was going to come, it had to come, but even that difficulty is ultimately for our benefit.

هُوَ مَوْلَىٰنَا (He is our Protector). We have Allah ﷻ as our protective friend. وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (And upon Allah exclusively, let the believers place their trust). Not on strategies, plans, or preparations—though these are important—but the trust itself rests with Allah ﷻ alone.

translation: Say, "Do you await for us except one of the two best things while we await for you that Allah will afflict you with punishment from Himself or at our hands? So wait; indeed we, along with you, are waiting
commentary:
قُلْ هَلْ تَرَبَّصُونَ بِنَآ إِلَّآ إِحْدَى ٱلْحُسْنَيَيْنِ ۖ وَنَحْنُ نَتَرَبَّصُ بِكُمْ أَن يُصِيبَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِعَذَابٍ مِّنْ عِندِهِۦٓ أَوْ بِأَيْدِينَا ۖ فَتَرَبَّصُوٓا۟ إِنَّا مَعَكُم مُّتَرَبِّصُونَ

The word تَرَبَّصُونَ comes from waiting, but specifically waiting for things to get better. It implies holding back until times are more suitable. For example, a shopkeeper waits for merchandise to be sold—he's waiting for things to improve when he has more money. The same word is used for a woman's waiting period after divorce or the death of her husband—waiting for circumstances to improve.

Here it means procrastination in the sense of holding back, hoping things will turn in your favor. Allah ﷻ asks: قُلْ هَلْ تَرَبَّصُونَ بِنَآ إِلَّآ إِحْدَى ٱلْحُسْنَيَيْنِ (Say: Are you holding back from us except for one of the two best things?). الْحُسْنَيَيْنِ is the dual form, meaning "the two best things."

What are these two best outcomes? Victory or martyrdom leading straight to Paradise. Both are absolutely the best possible results. How can we complain? We're heading toward a win-win situation. That was the attitude at Tabuk as well.

The ayah continues: وَنَحْنُ نَتَرَبَّصُ بِكُمْ أَن يُصِيبَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِعَذَابٍ مِّنْ عِندِهِۦٓ أَوْ بِأَيْدِينَا (And we are waiting for Allah to strike you with punishment from Himself, or by our hands). The believers can wait too—waiting for Allah ﷻ to deliver punishment to the hypocrites either directly from Him or through the Muslims themselves.

فَتَرَبَّصُوٓا۟ إِنَّا مَعَكُم مُّتَرَبِّصُونَ (So go ahead and wait. Indeed, we are waiting along with you). This threat never materialized in the way described, but its issuance had an effect—some hypocrites began to worry about what might happen.

translation: Say, "Spend willingly or unwillingly; never will it be accepted from you. Indeed, you have been a defiantly disobedient people
commentary:
قُلْ أَنفِقُوا۟ طَوْعًا أَوْ كَرْهًا لَّن يُتَقَبَّلَ مِنكُمْ ۖ إِنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ قَوْمًا فَـٰسِقِينَ

Some of the wealthy hypocrites, frightened by the previous warnings, came with money as a kind of exemption tax. They couldn't go to war, but here was compensation. The Muslims were in need of funds, yet Allah ﷻ says something remarkable: We don't want your money. Principles come before needs.

قُلْ أَنفِقُوا۟ طَوْعًا أَوْ كَرْهًا لَّن يُتَقَبَّلَ مِنكُمْ (Say: Spend willingly or unwillingly—it will never be accepted from you). Notice the phrase طَوْعًا أَوْ كَرْهًا captures both aspects of their giving. There's an element of compulsion (the alternative is going themselves) and an element of "volunteering" (they're offering money), but it's also كَرْهًا because they hate parting with their wealth. So it's a mixture of both.

لَّن يُتَقَبَّلَ مِنكُمْ (It will never be accepted from you). Why? إِنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ قَوْمًا فَـٰسِقِينَ (Indeed, you have been a corrupt people). The word قَوْمًا (a people) is significant. They're not even considered part of the Muslim community anymore. They're قَوْمًا—a people by themselves, distinguished and separated. قَوْمًا فَـٰسِقِينَ—a corrupt group altogether.

translation: And what prevents their expenditures from being accepted from them but that they have disbelieved in Allah and in His Messenger and that they come not to prayer except while they are lazy and that they do not spend except while they are unwilling
commentary:
وَمَا مَنَعَهُمْ أَن تُقْبَلَ مِنْهُمْ نَفَقَـٰتُهُمْ إِلَّآ أَنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَبِرَسُولِهِۦ وَلَا يَأْتُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ إِلَّا وَهُمْ كُسَالَىٰ وَلَا يُنفِقُونَ إِلَّا وَهُمْ كَـٰرِهُونَ

This ayah explains precisely why their spending is rejected. وَمَا مَنَعَهُمْ أَن تُقْبَلَ مِنْهُمْ نَفَقَـٰتُهُمْ (What prevented their spending from being accepted?) The answer: إِلَّآ أَنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَبِرَسُولِهِۦ (Except that they disbelieved in Allah and in His Messenger).

Notice the dual mention—they had a horrible relationship with Allah ﷻ, and separately, they displayed a terrible relationship with His Messenger ﷺ. Both relationships were corrupt, and this is nothing short of disbelief.

Then comes what could be called the climax of hypocrisy: وَلَا يَأْتُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ إِلَّا وَهُمْ كُسَالَىٰ (And they don't come to prayer except that they are lazy). This puts every believer in an uncomfortable position. Allah ﷻ didn't say "they don't pray at all." No—they do come to prayer. But they never come except in a state of laziness, dragging themselves reluctantly.

This is a mirror for us. When we find ourselves sluggish about prayer, approaching it without energy or enthusiasm, we should recognize that this is a characteristic Allah ﷻ uses to describe the hypocrites.

وَلَا يُنفِقُونَ إِلَّا وَهُمْ كَـٰرِهُونَ (And they don't spend except that they are disgusted). They hate the idea of spending. They only do it to save themselves, with no other motive.

This too should make us reflect. When we're at a fundraiser and the volunteer is about to take our check, notice those few seconds before we let go. What runs through our minds? Bills, tuition, health insurance, car payments, gas—a whole list of expenses floods our thoughts when we're about to spend for Allah ﷻ. 

Yet we don't think this way at Walmart. We throw items into our cart without hesitation, without calculating the electricity bill or thinking about finances. When does the internal accounting start? When we're about to spend for Allah ﷻ. Suddenly everyone becomes a financial analyst, and our entire budget runs through our heads in a split second. But when buying expensive sneakers or eating at restaurants, we tell ourselves, "I got this. I can afford it."

Then we leave the masjid and there's the donation box, and we start flipping through our wallet: "Which bill should I give? The twenty? The ten?" This is the reality of وَهُمْ كَـٰرِهُونَ—spending while disliking it.

This is why spending for Allah's sake is actually a protection of our iman. It's fundamental to our faith. We should encourage our children to give from their toys, from their allowance. The principle we must reinforce: whenever we give, Allah ﷻ gives us multiples back.

One practical approach is to remember that charity never decreases wealth, as the Prophet ﷺ taught us. The best explanation of sadaqah for a believer comes from understanding that we have two accounts—a worldly account and an eternal account. When you transfer funds from one account to another, you don't call it an expense. Giving sadaqah is simply transferring funds. You have short-term accounts and long-term accounts. The long-term account has phenomenal returns—not 1%, but at least 700%. Everything you spend, the return is at least 700 times.

The only way that account gets seized on Judgment Day is through regret. If you regret giving, it's gone. Or if you remind people of your generosity—"You know how much I donated? You know how much I gave?"—that means you did it for someone else's approval, not for Allah ﷻ. You transferred it to the wrong account.

When this is clear to a Muslim, there's no hesitation. He spends, and then Allah ﷻ gives him more. Why? So he can spend more. Then He gives even more so the person can give even more, and his account in the afterlife grows incredibly rich. If you want to get rich in the afterlife, focus on getting rich there, and Allah ﷻ will make you rich in this world too.

Then there are people whom Allah ﷻ gives wealth in this world, but they don't spend it for His sake. We're about to read about them now.

translation: So let not their wealth or their children impress you. Allah only intends to punish them through them in worldly life and that their souls should depart [at death] while they are disbelievers
commentary:
فَلَا تُعْجِبْكَ أَمْوَٰلُهُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُهُمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُم بِهَا فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَتَزْهَقَ أَنفُسُهُمْ وَهُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ

The wealthy hypocrites who tried to save themselves from joining the army didn't bring small amounts—they brought serious cash. Now that they're walking away, it might seem like the army's losing out on significant reserves. Allah ﷻ addresses this: فَلَا تُعْجِبْكَ أَمْوَٰلُهُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُهُمْ (Don't be impressed with their wealth or their children). Don't let it bother you. Leave them be.

إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُم بِهَا فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا (Allah only intends to torture them with it in this worldly life). Their wealth and children—He combines both—will be instruments of their torture. You'll find wealthy people who refused to sacrifice for Allah's sake, and in their old age, their children become the source of their misery. They're genuinely tortured.

Consider wealthy individuals in the Muslim community living in huge mansions whose children refuse to speak to them. Miserable lives. Horrible, painful lives. Their money tortures them—blood pressure rising during tax season, during audits, estate management, all the complications wealth brings. وَتَزْهَقَ أَنفُسُهُمْ وَهُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ (And that their souls depart while they are disbelievers).

The word تَزْهَقَ means to run in defeat, to flee. It comes from the same root as when someone flees a battlefield. Here it describes their souls departing—running away, fleeing in defeat—while they remain in a state of disbelief. Their souls leave them while they're still kāfirūn, having gained nothing from all their wealth and children except misery in this life and loss in the next.

translation: And they swear by Allah that they are from among you while they are not from among you; but they are a people who are afraid
commentary:
وَيَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ إِنَّهُمْ لَمِنكُمْ وَمَا هُم مِّنكُمْ وَلَـٰكِنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ يَفْرَقُونَ

The Arabic language contains numerous words for oaths and swearing. We have قَسَم, يَمِين, and the letters of oaths like وَ. The word حَلِف or يَحْلِف carries a specific meaning - it refers to an oath taken to make amends, to reconcile after a problem has occurred. It's the kind of swearing where someone promises they've changed or that past issues won't resurface.

When Allah ﷻ says وَيَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ إِنَّهُمْ لَمِنكُمْ (they swear by Allah that they are from among you), the very choice of this word reveals something crucial: these hypocrites have already shown treacherous behavior before. Now they're coming back saying, "No, never again. I promise. I swear to Allah ﷻ." The fact that they need to make such oaths exposes their history of betrayal.

Allah ﷻ responds with stark clarity: وَمَا هُم مِّنكُمْ (they are not from among you at all). Then He reveals their true nature: وَلَـٰكِنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ يَفْرَقُونَ (they are a people who split apart). The word يَفْرَقُونَ comes from the same root as when the ocean parted for Musa (AS). These hypocrites are a nation that has split away, and as they split, they're not advancing toward any meaningful cause - they're running away from confronting the truth.

translation: If they could find a refuge or some caves or any place to enter [and hide], they would turn to it while they run heedlessly
commentary:
لَوْ يَجِدُونَ مَلْجَـًٔا أَوْ مَغَـٰرَٰتٍ أَوْ مُدَّخَلًا لَّوَلَّوْا۟ إِلَيْهِ وَهُمْ يَجْمَحُونَ

This ayah exposes the desperate desire of hypocrites to escape. لَوْ يَجِدُونَ مَلْجَـًٔا (if they could find any protective fort) - مَلْجَأ refers to any place of refuge or protection. أَوْ مَغَـٰرَٰتٍ (or any caves they could hide in), أَوْ مُدَّخَلًا (or any hole they could enter) - if any such escape existed, لَّوَلَّوْا۟ إِلَيْهِ (they would turn to it immediately).

The ayah concludes وَهُمْ يَجْمَحُونَ (and they would all be sitting there terrified). This describes people so consumed by fear that they would flee to any refuge available, not out of strategic wisdom but out of pure cowardice and terror.

translation: And among them are some who criticize you concerning the [distribution of] charities. If they are given from them, they approve; but if they are not given from them, at once they become angry
commentary:
وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَلْمِزُكَ فِى ٱلصَّدَقَـٰتِ فَإِنْ أُعْطُوا۟ مِنْهَا رَضُوا۟ وَإِن لَّمْ يُعْطَوْا۟ مِنْهَآ إِذَا هُمْ يَسْخَطُونَ

The word يَلْمِزُكَ carries layers of meaning - to blame falsely, to insult, to speak condescendingly. This is not the Islamic behavior of respectful questioning or holding leadership accountable. We are certainly permitted to ask questions of each other and of our leaders, but there are proper manners. We cannot become people of لَمْز - insulting, condescending speech.

The historical context brings this to life. When the Prophet ﷺ distributed war spoils, he didn't necessarily distribute them equally. He made personal assessments based on need - sometimes giving more to those in greater difficulty, sometimes less to others. One hypocrite stood in line, watching this distribution. When he saw a poor person receive a slightly larger portion, then looked at his own smaller share, he muttered condescendingly, "This is barely enough to cover anything. Is this the justice of Muhammad?"

The Prophet ﷺ became upset and said, "If I don't do justice, who is going to do justice? What's wrong with you?" He put him in his place, and now Allah ﷻ records this incident.

وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَلْمِزُكَ فِى ٱلصَّدَقَـٰتِ (among them is one who blames you regarding charities). The pattern reveals itself: فَإِنْ أُعْطُوا۟ مِنْهَا رَضُوا۟ (if they are given from it, they are pleased) - they wouldn't complain if given extra. They'd simply walk away satisfied. But وَإِن لَّمْ يُعْطَوْا۟ مِنْهَآ إِذَا هُمْ يَسْخَطُونَ (if they are not given from it, immediately they become angry and harsh). The word يَسْخَطُونَ indicates overwhelming rage.

What truly lies in their hearts? They cannot accept him as the Messenger of Allah ﷻ. That's what emerges from their mouths when things don't go their way.

translation: If only they had been satisfied with what Allah and His Messenger gave them and said, "Sufficient for us is Allah; Allah will give us of His bounty, and [so will] His Messenger; indeed, we are desirous toward Allah," [it would have been better for them]
commentary:
وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ رَضُوا۟ مَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ وَقَالُوا۟ حَسْبُنَا ٱللَّهُ سَيُؤْتِينَا ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَرَسُولُهُۥٓ إِنَّآ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ رَٰغِبُونَ

Allah ﷻ presents the ideal response these hypocrites should have had: وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ رَضُوا۟ مَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ (if only they had been pleased with what Allah and His Messenger gave them).

Notice the beautiful shift in the ayah. It begins with مَا ءَاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ (what Allah gave them) using the verb آتى, then transitions to وَقَالُوا۟ حَسْبُنَا ٱللَّهُ (and they should have said, "Allah is sufficient for us"). Then سَيُؤْتِينَا ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَرَسُولُهُۥٓ (Allah will soon give us from His bounty, and His Messenger too) - switching to the verb يُؤْتِي, indicating future provision.

The ayah concludes with the statement that should have been on their tongues: إِنَّآ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ رَٰغِبُونَ (we are only turning toward Allah). In other words, "We're not really concerned about money or material things. What we truly desire is to meet Allah ﷻ." If only that's what they would have said.

translation: Zakah expenditures are only for the poor and for the needy and for those employed to collect [zakah] and for bringing hearts together [for Islam] and for freeing captives [or slaves] and for those in debt and for the cause of Allah and for the [stranded] traveler - an obligation [imposed] by Allah. And Allah is Knowing and Wise
commentary:
۞ إِنَّمَا ٱلصَّدَقَـٰتُ لِلْفُقَرَآءِ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ وَٱلْعَـٰمِلِينَ عَلَيْهَا وَٱلْمُؤَلَّفَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَفِى ٱلرِّقَابِ وَٱلْغَـٰرِمِينَ وَفِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱبْنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ ۖ فَرِيضَةً مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ

This is the famous ayah of zakat distribution. The word الصَّدَقَـٰتُ here, according to the vast majority of scholars and by consensus, refers specifically to zakat - the mandatory charity.
