The word صَدَقَة itself comes from صِدْق (truthfulness), because your charity proves the truthfulness of your claim to Islam.

Allah ﷻ identifies eight categories of recipients:

**1. الْفُقَرَآءِ - The Bankrupt**: Those in absolute poverty, unable to help themselves, completely destitute.

**2. الْمَسَـٰكِينِ - Those in Difficult Situations**: People stuck in tough financial binds, not completely destitute but struggling significantly.

**3. الْعَـٰمِلِينَ عَلَيْهَا - Those Working on It**: The employees of the Islamic treasury. These are the people whose full-time job involves assessing everyone's financial situations, calculating their zakat, collecting it, and managing the distribution. This is sophisticated governance - a portion of the collected zakat funds the very system that collects and distributes it.

**4. الْمُؤَلَّفَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ - Those Whose Hearts Are to Be Joined**: This phrase contains a unique grammatical construction where المؤلفة (the adjective) comes before قلوبهم (what would normally precede it). In modern terms, this refers to funds for diplomatic relations. These are newcomers to Islam or tribes considering Islam. Going to them with gifts creates goodwill and softens hearts toward the faith. This is essentially the foreign policy budget within the zakat system - the Ministry of Foreign Affairs fund.

**5. فِى ٱلرِّقَابِ - In the Necks**: Freeing slaves, liberating captives, releasing people from bondage.

**6. الْغَـٰرِمِينَ - Those Penalized**: People buried under crushing debt, those who have been sued or had penalties imposed on them and are stuck in that situation. Bailing such people out of their debts.

**7. فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - In the Path of Allah**: While we often think all these categories fall under "the path of Allah," Allah ﷻ specifies it as a distinct category. In the time of the Prophet ﷺ, this primarily meant da'wah (calling people to Islam). When Mus'ab ibn Umayr (RA) went to Madinah to represent Islam, he was on a stipend from the Prophet ﷺ. That missionary work was fee sabeelillah. Later, when Muslim armies were constituted and engaged in battle, funding those armies for the sake of Islam also fell under this category. Any work to further and strengthen Islam - whether da'wah, defense, or other initiatives - constitutes fee sabeelillah.

Some modern scholars have interpreted this category to include Islamic schools, masajid, and other Islamic projects, though others have reservations about this expansion.

**8. ابْنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ - The Traveler**: Those caught in the midst of travel. In an Islamic society, there should be a governmental fund dedicated to housing travelers who don't have a place to stay. These would be like guest houses where people traveling through could check in and stay comfortably - meaning the hotel industry wouldn't thrive in the same way because hospitality for travelers would be a public service funded by zakat.

The ayah concludes: فَرِيضَةً مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ (a mandate from Allah) - all of this is obligatory, not optional. وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (And Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise) - He knows the needs of society and has wisely designed this comprehensive system.

translation: And among them are those who abuse the Prophet and say, "He is an ear." Say, "[It is] an ear of goodness for you that believes in Allah and believes the believers and [is] a mercy to those who believe among you." And those who abuse the Messenger of Allah - for them is a painful punishment
commentary:
وَمِنْهُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ ٱلنَّبِىَّ وَيَقُولُونَ هُوَ أُذُنٌ ۚ قُلْ أُذُنُ خَيْرٍ لَّكُمْ يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَيُؤْمِنُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنكُمْ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ رَسُولَ ٱللَّهِ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

Among the hypocrites are those who يُؤْذُونَ ٱلنَّبِىَّ (cause pain to the Prophet). The word أَذَى means to cause harm or pain. These people would come to the Prophet ﷺ and fill his ears with their compelling, deceptive speech. Then behind his back, they would mock him saying هُوَ أُذُنٌ (he's all ears) - meaning he believes everything he hears, he's gullible, he's easily fooled.

Allah's ﷻ response is powerful: قُلْ أُذُنُ خَيْرٍ لَّكُمْ (Say: ears of goodness for you). The fact that he listens to you and assumes the best every time - that's actually in your favor. The fact that he doesn't call you out even though I could reveal to him exactly how to expose you, but I don't - that's a mercy for you.

Then Allah ﷻ describes the true nature of the Prophet ﷺ: يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ (he believes in Allah). Notice the ayah begins with the verb آمَنَ with the preposition بِ, but then immediately shifts: وَيُؤْمِنُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (and he accepts what the believers say). Here the preposition changes to لِ, indicating he gives credence to the believers, he trusts their word. This is not weakness - this is the prophetic character of assuming the best of the faithful.

وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنكُمْ (And he is a mercy for those who believe among you). He's not just someone who listens with good intent - he is an active mercy for the believers.

Then comes the warning: وَٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ رَسُولَ ٱللَّهِ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (And those who cause pain to the Messenger of Allah - for them is painful punishment).

translation: They swear by Allah to you [Muslims] to satisfy you. But Allah and His Messenger are more worthy for them to satisfy, if they should be believers
commentary:
يَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ لَكُمْ لِيُرْضُوكُمْ وَٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥٓ أَحَقُّ أَن يُرْضُوهُ إِن كَانُوا۟ مُؤْمِنِينَ

After the Muslims returned from Tabuk, it became known who had failed to participate. These hypocrites faced social consequences - the Muslim community began boycotting them. The ayahs before this had already shut down their ability to come swear directly to the Messenger ﷺ with their excuses. So now they're thinking of an alternative strategy: convince the regular Muslims around them that they're still okay.

They approach other Muslims saying, "You know us, right? We're good people. I don't know why the Prophet ﷺ is being so harsh. He gets revelations that make him strict, you know how it is." They're trying to avoid complete social boycott, which would be its own form of torture.

Allah ﷻ exposes this: يَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ لَكُمْ لِيُرْضُوكُمْ (They swear by Allah to you to please you). But then comes the correction: وَٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥٓ أَحَقُّ أَن يُرْضُوهُ (but Allah and His Messenger are more worthy that they should seek to please Him). 

The pronoun shifts to singular - يُرْضُوهُ (please Him) - because when you please the Messenger ﷺ, you please Allah ﷻ, and when you please Allah ﷻ, you please the Messenger ﷺ. The two cannot be separated.

إِن كَانُوا۟ مُؤْمِنِينَ (if in fact they are believers) - this conditional challenges their very claim to faith.

translation: Do they not know that whoever opposes Allah and His Messenger - that for him is the fire of Hell, wherein he will abide eternally? That is the great disgrace
commentary:
أَلَمْ يَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّهُۥ مَن يُحَادِدِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ فَأَنَّ لَهُۥ نَارَ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدًا فِيهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْخِزْىُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ

أَلَمْ يَعْلَمُوٓا۟ (Did they not know?) - this rhetorical question suggests they should have known better. What should they have known? أَنَّهُۥ مَن يُحَادِدِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ (that whoever opposes Allah and His Messenger).

The word يُحَادِدِ means to be in opposition, to stand against. In this context, the simple act of not going out to Tabuk when called - that in itself was an act of opposing Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ. It wasn't just a personal decision or a matter of convenience. It was defiance.

فَأَنَّ لَهُۥ نَارَ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدًا فِيهَا (then for him is the Fire of Hell, remaining in it permanently). The consequence is not temporary - it's eternal residence in the Fire.

ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْخِزْىُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (That is the ultimate humiliation). These hypocrites worry about social boycott and public embarrassment in Madinah. Allah ﷻ tells them: you think being socially shunned is humiliating? No, the true humiliation, the ultimate disgrace - that's still coming.

translation: They hypocrites are apprehensive lest a surah be revealed about them, informing them of what is in their hearts. Say, "Mock [as you wish]; indeed, Allah will expose that which you fear
commentary:
يَحْذَرُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ أَن تُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُورَةٌ تُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ ۚ قُلِ ٱسْتَهْزِءُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مُخْرِجٌ مَّا تَحْذَرُونَ

The hypocrites are terrified. يَحْذَرُ means to be extremely cautious and worried. What are they afraid of? أَن تُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُورَةٌ (that a surah might come down about them).

Remember the warning hinted at earlier? The hypocrites had been whispering to each other: "If a surah comes down about us dealing with this issue, we're done for." Now that fear is being acknowledged. They're scared that revelation will تُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ (inform them of what is in their hearts). They already have a pretty good feeling about what would be exposed if that happened.

Allah's ﷻ response is almost dismissive: قُلِ ٱسْتَهْزِءُوٓا۟ (Say: Go ahead and mock). Make fun all you want. Take it as a joke. إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مُخْرِجٌ مَّا تَحْذَرُونَ (Indeed, Allah will bring out the very thing you're so scared about). Eventually, the time will come when you will be exposed.

translation: And if you ask them, they will surely say, "We were only conversing and playing." Say, "Is it Allah and His verses and His Messenger that you were mocking
commentary:
وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُمْ لَيَقُولُنَّ إِنَّمَا كُنَّا نَخُوضُ وَنَلْعَبُ ۚ قُلْ أَبِٱللَّهِ وَءَايَـٰتِهِۦ وَرَسُولِهِۦ كُنتُمْ تَسْتَهْزِءُونَ

This ayah has a powerful story behind it. A young Companion overheard Abdullah ibn Ubayy - the leader of the hypocrites - talking trash about the Messenger ﷺ, openly mocking him in a private gathering.

The context involves an incident where the Prophet ﷺ made a call for spending in Allah's ﷻ path. One Companion, who didn't have much, found work at a farm drawing water from a well. He worked the entire night, and by morning received a bunch of dates as payment. He took half the dates to his family and brought the other half to contribute to the Muslim army.

The Prophet ﷺ was deeply moved. He said, "These dates are blessed. Spread them over the entire field, and they will carry more barakah and will weigh heavier than everything else we've collected." The hypocrites watched this scene and started mocking: "Oh yes, these dates are really going to turn the tide against the Roman army. Let me see what's in these dates."

This was their public mockery. Imagine what they said in private. The young Companion who overheard their private conversation came back and reported to the Prophet ﷺ what they had been saying. 

Now understand the context: this wasn't like the early Makkan period when hypocrites held social power and could meet secretly without consequence. Islam was now established. If hypocrites talked trash and the Prophet ﷺ found out, they would be called to account. They would have to show up and answer for their words.

So when this young man reported them, they were summoned. Abdullah ibn Ubayy gave the youth a threatening look and said dismissively, "You're going to listen to this kid over me? He doesn't know what he's talking about."

Allah ﷻ came to the rescue of that young Companion with this ayah: وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُمْ لَيَقُولُنَّ إِنَّمَا كُنَّا نَخُوضُ وَنَلْعَبُ (And if you were to ask them, they will surely say, "We were only conversing and playing").

The word نَخُوضُ comes from خَوْض - to jump into something without care. Like someone who jumps into water without checking if it's deep or shallow, without any caution. They jump into conversations about anything without concern, running their mouths carelessly. "We were just talking, shooting the breeze. We didn't mean it like that. We were just playing around."

Allah's ﷻ response cuts through their excuse: قُلْ أَبِٱللَّهِ وَءَايَـٰتِهِۦ وَرَسُولِهِۦ كُنتُمْ تَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (Say: "Was it Allah, His verses, and His Messenger that you were mocking?")

This question proves they were in fact doing exactly what they denied. There's no room for "just joking" when it comes to Allah ﷻ, His revelations, and His Messenger ﷺ.

translation: Make no excuse; you have disbelieved after your belief. If We pardon one faction of you - We will punish another faction because they were criminals
commentary:
لَا تَعْتَذِرُوا۟ قَدْ كَفَرْتُم بَعْدَ إِيمَـٰنِكُمْ ۚ إِن نَّعْفُ عَن طَآئِفَةٍ مِّنكُمْ نُعَذِّبْ طَآئِفَةًۢ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ مُجْرِمِينَ

لَا تَعْتَذِرُوا۟ (Don't make excuses). The time for excuses is over. قَدْ كَفَرْتُم بَعْدَ إِيمَـٰنِكُمْ (You have already committed disbelief after your belief).

This is a devastating verdict. Making mockery of Allah ﷻ, His ayat, and His Messenger ﷺ constitutes kufr (disbelief). Even if they claimed it was "just joking," even if they insisted they didn't mean it - the act itself is kufr. You cannot mock the sacred and claim faith.

Then Allah ﷻ makes a distinction: إِن نَّعْفُ عَن طَآئِفَةٍ مِّنكُمْ نُعَذِّبْ طَآئِفَةًۢ (If We pardon one group among you, We will punish another group). Not all of them will receive the same judgment. Some may be forgiven, but others will face consequences.

Why? بِأَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ مُجْرِمِينَ (Because they were criminals). The word مُجْرِمِينَ indicates those who persist in their crimes, who continue in their wrongdoing without repentance.

translation: The hypocrite men and hypocrite women are of one another. They enjoin what is wrong and forbid what is right and close their hands. They have forgotten Allah, so He has forgotten them [accordingly]. Indeed, the hypocrites - it is they who are the defiantly disobedient
commentary:
ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقَـٰتُ بَعْضُهُم مِّنۢ بَعْضٍ ۚ يَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمُنكَرِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَقْبِضُونَ أَيْدِيَهُمْ ۚ نَسُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فَنَسِيَهُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ

ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقَـٰتُ (The hypocrite men and hypocrite women) - both are mentioned, showing this disease affects both genders. بَعْضُهُم مِّنۢ بَعْضٍ (they are from one another). In English we say "birds of a feather flock together." Arabic has the same concept. Hypocrites naturally gravitate toward each other, they form their own circles, they converge and hang out among themselves.

يَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمُنكَرِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمَعْرُوفِ (They command to evil and forbid what is right). This is a powerful statement about how hypocrites invert moral values. The concepts of مَعْرُوف (recognized good) and مُنكَر (recognized evil) have different applications depending on context.

In a society unaware of Islam, معروف and منكر refer to universal moral values - things generally recognized as good or evil. But in Madinah, where the Quran has been revealed and Islamic law established, where Islam has become prominent and victorious, معروف now specifically means the injunctions of Islam, and منكر means whatever opposes Islam. The definitions become more precise.

We must be careful in how we apply these terms today. In societies where Islam hasn't become commonplace, where even Muslims don't fully know their faith, we start with universal معروف and منكر and gradually move people toward the more precise Islamic understanding. You grow with people; you can't expect them to know everything immediately.

In this ayah, the hypocrites command to المنكر - meaning they actively encourage opposition to what the Messenger ﷺ instructs. "Let's go to Tabuk." "No, we're not going. Come on, guys, what are you doing? Don't go." They forbid المعروف - they actively discourage people from following clear Islamic guidance.

This reveals a key psychological pattern of hypocrisy: to stay safe, hypocrites compel others to do what they're doing. Why? So they can say, "I wasn't the only one. Look, these guys did it too." If they can convince everyone else to be cheap, they can label their stinginess as "financial responsibility." If they can convince others not to go to battle, they can frame their cowardice as "concern for safety."

وَيَقْبِضُونَ أَيْدِيَهُمْ (And they close their fists). They grab tight, they don't spend, they don't contribute. They make fists with their hands rather than opening them in generosity.

Then comes a devastating statement: نَسُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فَنَسِيَهُمْ (They forgot Allah, so He forgot them).

The word نَسِيَ has two meanings. One is to forget in the sense of memory: "I forgot today is Sunday." But there's another meaning: to dismiss, to disregard, to not care about. If someone asks, "What do you think of this student?" and I respond, "Forget him" - do I mean I don't remember him? No, I mean I don't care about him anymore. He's nothing to me now. He's dead to me.

نَسُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ (They forgot Allah) means they didn't care about Allah ﷻ. They were indifferent to Him, dismissive of His commands. And the result? فَنَسِيَهُمْ (So He forgot them) - not in the sense that Allah ﷻ lacks knowledge or memory, but that He doesn't care about them anymore. They're a lost cause. They're finished in His sight.

إِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (Indeed, the hypocrites - they are the truly corrupt). The word فَاسِقُونَ indicates those who have departed from the path, who have broken out of the bounds of righteousness. The hypocrites are, in fact, the ultimate manifestation of corruption.

translation: Allah has promised the hypocrite men and hypocrite women and the disbelievers the fire of Hell, wherein they will abide eternally. It is sufficient for them. And Allah has cursed them, and for them is an enduring punishment
commentary:
وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقَـٰتِ وَٱلْكُفَّارَ نَارَ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ هِىَ حَسْبُهُمْ ۚ وَلَعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّقِيمٌ

Allah ﷻ has promised the hypocrite men and women, along with the disbelievers, the fire of Hell, in which they will remain eternally. This ayah establishes three levels of punishment for those who opposed the truth: the promise of Hellfire itself, Allah's curse upon them, and an enduring punishment that will never cease.

The word حَسْبُهُمْ carries a profound meaning—it is enough for them. The fire itself is sufficient as their recompense. They need no other punishment beyond this, though Allah ﷻ adds that He has cursed them and they will have a punishment that is مُّقِيمٌ—staying, stationary, never leaving them. This demonstrates the permanence and severity of their condition in the Hereafter.

translation: [You disbelievers are] like those before you; they were stronger than you in power and more abundant in wealth and children. They enjoyed their portion [of worldly enjoyment], and you have enjoyed your portion as those before you enjoyed their portion, and you have engaged [in vanities] like that in which they engaged. [It is] those whose deeds have become worthless in this world and in the Hereafter, and it is they who are the losers
commentary:
كَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ كَانُوٓا۟ أَشَدَّ مِنكُمْ قُوَّةً وَأَكْثَرَ أَمْوَٰلًا وَأَوْلَـٰدًا فَٱسْتَمْتَعُوا۟ بِخَلَـٰقِهِمْ فَٱسْتَمْتَعْتُم بِخَلَـٰقِكُمْ كَمَا ٱسْتَمْتَعَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُم بِخَلَـٰقِهِمْ وَخُضْتُمْ كَٱلَّذِى خَاضُوٓا۟ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ

The hypocrites of Medina are compared to those who came before them—nations that were far more powerful, wealthier, and had more children. Yet they enjoyed their خَلَاق—their portion or share from creation. This word refers to the temporary pleasures and benefits that people are allotted in this worldly life. Those previous nations enjoyed their portion, and the hypocrites being addressed here enjoyed theirs in the same manner.

The phrase خُضْتُمْ كَٱلَّذِى خَاضُوٓا means "you talked empty talk just like they talked empty talk." You ran your mouths in the same way they ran theirs. This refers to the meaningless debates, the mockery of truth, and the spreading of rumors and lies that characterized both the hypocrites of Medina and the disbelieving nations before them.

The result is devastating: حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ—their deeds have been seized, nullified in both this world and the next. They are the ultimate losers, having wasted their worldly portion on temporary pleasures and lost everything in the Hereafter.

translation: Has there not reached them the news of those before them - the people of Noah and [the tribes of] 'Aad and Thamud and the people of Abraham and the companions of Madyan and the towns overturned? Their messengers came to them with clear proofs. And Allah would never have wronged them, but they were wronging themselves
commentary:
أَلَمْ يَأْتِهِمْ نَبَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَعَادٍ وَثَمُودَ وَقَوْمِ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَأَصْحَـٰبِ مَدْيَنَ وَٱلْمُؤْتَفِكَـٰتِ ۚ أَتَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ ۖ فَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيَظْلِمَهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ

This ayah shifts the tone dramatically, taking on the flavor of Makkan revelation. The rhetorical question begins: Didn't the news reach them of those who came before them? The people of Nuh (AS), 'Ad, Thamud, the people of Ibrahim (AS), the companions of Madyan, and al-mu'tafikat—the overturned towns?

This is the only place in the Quran where it is alluded to that Ibrahim (AS) was sent to a particular nation that was subsequently destroyed. We don't have a detailed historical account of exactly who this nation was, but the mention here indicates that Ibrahim (AS) faced opposition from a people who suffered consequences for their rejection.

The word الْمُؤْتَفِكَـٰتِ comes from ifk, meaning to turn something upside down and smash it. These were towns that were turned over and destroyed—a reference to the cities of the people of Lut (AS). Messengers came to all these nations with the clearest proofs, yet they rejected them.

Allah ﷻ declares: فَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيَظْلِمَهُمْ —it wasn't becoming of Allah to wrong them. Allah would never be one to wrong them. Rather, كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ —they were the ones wronging themselves. Their destruction was not injustice from Allah ﷻ but the natural consequence of their own choices and rejection of truth.

translation: The believing men and believing women are allies of one another. They enjoin what is right and forbid what is wrong and establish prayer and give zakah and obey Allah and His Messenger. Those - Allah will have mercy upon them. Indeed, Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise
commentary:
وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتُ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ ۚ يَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَيُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَيُطِيعُونَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥٓ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ سَيَرْحَمُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ

After describing the hypocrites and their characteristics, Allah ﷻ now presents the contrast: the believing men and women. بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ —some of them are protective friends, close allies, and reliable supporters to one another. This stands in stark contrast to the hypocrites, who were described earlier as supporting one another in evil.

The believers command what is known to be good (ma'ruf) and forbid what is evil and unrecognizable as good (munkar). They establish the salah—and this phrase carries particular weight when understood in contrast to the hypocrites. Earlier, Allah ﷻ described the hypocrites as being lazy in their prayer. So when we read here that the believers "establish the salah," it means they are not lazy in it. They perform it with full presence, dedication, and consistency.

They give zakah, which also contrasts with the hypocrites who were described as only spending when they disliked it, and who gave charity hoping for compensation or exemption from joining the Messenger ﷺ in his campaigns. The believers, by contrast, give zakah sincerely and obey Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ.

The promise for such people is beautiful: أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ سَيَرَحَمُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ —those, Allah will give them mercy. Notice the word "سَ" (soon)—Allah ﷻ is already showing them mercy now, but soon a special mercy will come from Him. The ayah concludes by reminding us that إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ —certainly Allah is the ultimate authority, All-Wise.

translation: Allah has promised the believing men and believing women gardens beneath which rivers flow, wherein they abide eternally, and pleasant dwellings in gardens of perpetual residence; but approval from Allah is greater. It is that which is the great attainment
commentary:
وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا وَمَسَـٰكِنَ طَيِّبَةً فِى جَنَّـٰتِ عَدْنٍ ۚ وَرِضْوَٰنٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ أَكْبَرُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ

Allah ﷻ has promised believing men and women gardens—multiple gardens, not just one—beneath which rivers flow. They will remain in them eternally. And they will have مَسَـٰكِنَ طَيِّبَةً—beautiful, pure homes.

The word طَيِّبَةً carries multiple dimensions. Tayyib means something you like, something that is good in itself, and something that is pure. So these homes have three qualities: they are pleasing to the residents, they are inherently good, and they are completely pure and free from any defect or impurity. These homes will be in جَنَّـٰتِ عَدْنٍ —the Gardens of Eden, of permanent residence.

Then comes the most remarkable part of the promise: وَرِضْوَٰنٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ أَكْبَرُ —and contentment from Allah is greater. This phrase has multiple layers of meaning. First, it means Allah ﷻ is pleased with them. Second, the contentment they experience can only come from Allah ﷻ. Third, their contentment comes from what Allah ﷻ has given them, and that contentment is greater than all the physical pleasures of Paradise combined.

ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ—that is the ultimate, the great success.

translation: O Prophet, fight against the disbelievers and the hypocrites and be harsh upon them. And their refuge is Hell, and wretched is the destination
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ جَـٰهِدِ ٱلْكُفَّارَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ وَٱغْلُظْ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَمَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ

This ayah contains a critical command to the Prophet ﷺ: جَـٰهِدِ ٱلْكُفَّارَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ —struggle against the disbelievers and the hypocrites.

This ayah is essential for understanding the proper meaning of jihad. The word jihad appears in this surah, which is more associated with fighting and military campaigns than perhaps any other surah. Yet here, the word jihad is used to command the Prophet ﷺ to struggle against the hypocrites—and the Prophet ﷺ never fought the hypocrites in battle. He never engaged them in physical combat. So what does this jihad mean?

It refers to the struggle of exposing their plots, dismantling their groups, undoing the rumors they tried to spread, and standing firm against their attempts to weaken the Muslim community from within. This was jihad—a different form of struggle, but jihad nonetheless.

This demonstrates that it is an oversimplification to equate jihad only with fighting. The term is broader and encompasses various forms of struggle against opposition to Islam. The most powerful evidence for this is in this very surah, which is so closely connected with military campaigns, yet uses jihad to refer to something other than fighting.

The command continues: وَٱغْلُظْ عَلَيْهِمْ—and be tough against them, be stern with them. The word اغْلُظْ means to be firm, even harsh or insensitive to their complaints and manipulations. It's the same word used to describe angels who are stern and unyielding in carrying out Allah's commands. In another place in the Quran, the Prophet ﷺ is told that if he had been rough and hard-hearted, people would have run away from him—but that was in dealing with the believers. Here, with those who are working against Islam, he is commanded to be firm and uncompromising.

Their final destination is جَهَنَّمُ—Hellfire. وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ—and what a horrible final destination that is.

translation: They swear by Allah that they did not say [anything against the Prophet] while they had said the word of disbelief and disbelieved after their [pretense of] Islam and planned that which they were not to attain. And they were not resentful except [for the fact] that Allah and His Messenger had enriched them of His bounty. So if they repent, it is better for them; but if they turn away, Allah will punish them with a painful punishment in this world and the Hereafter. And there will not be for them on earth any protector or helper
commentary:
يَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ مَا قَالُوا۟ وَلَقَدْ قَالُوا۟ كَلِمَةَ ٱلْكُفْرِ وَكَفَرُوا۟ بَعْدَ إِسْلَـٰمِهِمْ وَهَمُّوا۟ بِمَا لَمْ يَنَالُوا۟ ۚ وَمَا نَقَمُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ أَنْ أَغْنَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ ۚ فَإِن يَتُوبُوا۟ يَكُ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ ۖ وَإِن يَتَوَلَّوْا۟ يُعَذِّبْهُمُ ٱللَّهُ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ وَمَا لَهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ

The hypocrites swear by Allah ﷻ that they never said anything wrong—مَا قَالُوا—we didn't say it, we swear to God we didn't say anything bad. But Allah ﷻ exposes them: وَلَقَدْ قَالُوا۟ كَلِمَةَ ٱلْكُفْرِ—they absolutely did say words of disbelief.

Not only did they utter disbelief, but وَكَفَرُوا۟ بَعْدَ إِسْلَـٰمِهِمْ—they disbelieved after their Islam. They had entered Islam, but then they turned back into disbelief.

Then comes a phrase that seems intentionally ambiguous: وَهَمُّوا۟ بِمَا لَمْ يَنَالُوا—and they desired something they never got. What did they want that they didn't get?

This refers to a specific incident that occurred on the return journey from Tabuk. During the night, the Prophet ﷺ had to pass through a narrow valley where only one rider could pass at a time. His defensive guard was reduced to just two Companions. Some of the hypocrites saw this as a golden opportunity. They covered their faces with black cloaks in the darkness and attempted to assassinate the Prophet ﷺ.

The attempt failed. The two Companions with him were Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman (RA) and 'Ammar ibn Yasir (RA). Allah ﷻ revealed to the Messenger ﷺ the names of those who had tried to kill him. The Prophet ﷺ called Hudhayfah (RA) and told him the names but commanded him to keep this information secret. This is why Hudhayfah (RA) became known as the "keeper of the secret" regarding the identities of the hypocrites.

When 'Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA) later became khalifah, he would ask Hudhayfah (RA), "Is my name on that list?" Hudhayfah (RA) would tell him, "I cannot reveal the list, but I can tell you that you are not on it." This shows the deep concern even the greatest Companions had about the disease of hypocrisy.

The ayah then reveals something shocking: وَمَا نَقَمُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ أَنْ أَغْنَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ—and what were they avenging? What was their grudge? Only that Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ made them rich from His favor.

They had nothing. Islam gave them dignity, purpose, community, and even material blessings. Yet instead of gratitude, they harbored resentment. This is the psychology of certain hypocrites—they cannot stand to be in debt to the truth, so they try to destroy it.

Then comes an invitation to redemption: فَإِن يَتُوبُوا۟ يَكُ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ—if they were to repent, it would be better for them. Notice the grammar here: يَكُ instead of يَكُونَ—the noon is dropped, which is permissible in Arabic and adds a sense of immediacy and directness.

But if they turn away—وَإِن يَتَوَلَّوْا۟ يُعَذِّبْهُمُ ٱللَّهُ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ—Allah will torture them with a painful punishment in this world and the next.

Previously, hypocrites had escape routes. They could flee to Quraysh, seek support from other tribes, find refuge elsewhere. But now: وَمَا لَهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ—they won't find in the land any protector or any helper whatsoever. All their options have been cut off.

translation: And among them are those who made a covenant with Allah, [saying], "If He should give us from His bounty, we will surely spend in charity, and we will surely be among the righteous
commentary:
۞ وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ عَـٰهَدَ ٱللَّهَ لَئِنْ ءَاتَىٰنَا مِن فَضْلِهِۦ لَنَصَّدَّقَنَّ وَلَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ

This ayah begins a profound commentary on the hypocrites of Medina, but it carries universal lessons for all of us across time. وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ عَـٰهَدَ ٱللَّهَ—among them is the one who made a promise to Allah ﷻ.

What was the promise? لَئِنْ ءَاتَىٰنَا مِن فَضْلِهِۦ لَنَصَّدَّقَنَّ وَلَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ—if He were to give us from His blessings, we will certainly give in charity and we will certainly be from among the righteous people.

This is the promise people make when they're in difficulty: "Allah, just get me through this and I'll be different. I'll change. I'll be charitable. I'll be righteous. Just let me pass this test, let me get this job, let me recover from this illness, let me escape this hardship."

translation: But when he gave them from His bounty, they were stingy with it and turned away while they refused
commentary:
فَلَمَّآ ءَاتَىٰهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦ بَخِلُوا۟ بِهِۦ وَتَوَلَّوا۟ وَّهُم مُّعْرِضُونَ

But then what happened? فَلَمَّآ ءَاتَىٰهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦ—when He gave them from His blessings, when He granted them what they had asked for, what did they do?

بَخِلُوا۟ بِهِۦ—they became miserly with it. They were cheap. They held back. وَتَوَلَّوا۟ وَّهُم مُّعْرِضُونَ—and they turned away while they were ignoring, as though they had never made that promise to Allah ﷻ at all.

This is a fresh reminder not only of the sirah but of contemporary Islamic history. Consider the last four or five centuries of Muslim experience. Much of the Muslim world was colonized by European powers. In those dark times, Muslims struggled against oppressive colonial forces across Africa, Asia, and the Arab world. People were crying out to Allah ﷻ: "Free us from these rulers who prevent us from raising our children properly, from living according to Your guidance, from declaring Your greatness openly."

They struggled and they struggled, and eventually, independence came. But what did the following generations do with that freedom? What did they establish? How did they live? What happened to those promises they made to Allah ﷻ?

Consider Algeria. The struggle against French colonization was devastating. The average age in Algeria is relatively young because so many of the older generation died fighting the French. They gave their lives begging Allah ﷻ for a time when their lands would be free and their descendants would be able to say "La ilaha illallah" without oppression.

Yet today, the greatest aspiration of many young Algerians is to move to France. Arabic, the language of the Quran, has been relegated to "the language of the street people," while French is considered sophisticated. This internalized inferiority complex exists throughout much of the Muslim world.

Allah ﷻ gave them what they asked for—victory, freedom, independence. They became cheap with it. بَخِلُوا۟ بِهِۦ—they were miserly with what Allah ﷻ had given them. And they turned away from their promises.

translation: So He penalized them with hypocrisy in their hearts until the Day they will meet Him - because they failed Allah in what they promised Him and because they [habitually] used to lie
commentary:
فَأَعْقَبَهُمْ نِفَاقًا فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يَلْقَوْنَهُۥ بِمَآ أَخْلَفُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا وَعَدُوهُ وَبِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْذِبُونَ

What was the consequence? فَأَعْقَبَهُمْ نِفَاقًا فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ—He made hypocrisy follow them, chase them. The word أَعْقَبَ comes from عَقِب, which means the heels right behind you. It's as if hypocrisy was pursuing them like a hunter chasing prey, and it caught up with them and entered deep inside their hearts.

إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يَلْقَوْنَهُۥ—until the day they will meet Him. This hypocrisy became embedded, permanent, lasting until they meet Allah ﷻ.

Why? بِمَآ أَخْلَفُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا وَعَدُوهُ—because they broke the promise they made to Allah ﷻ. Whatever they had promised Him, they went back on it.

وَبِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْذِبُونَ—and because of the lies they made. The lying became so deeply rooted that hypocrisy became the common currency in their land.

This is a warning to all believers across time. When people beg Allah ﷻ for blessings and promise to change, to be charitable, to be righteous, and then when those blessings come they turn away and break their promises, Allah ﷻ may make hypocrisy chase them until it catches them and settles in their hearts.

In some of the most corrupt nations of the world today, many of which are Muslim lands, we see the characteristics the Prophet ﷺ described: lying has become normal, promises are routinely broken, when people get angry they explode in rage, and when they're trusted with something they violate that trust. May Allah ﷻ protect us from this disease of nifaq, both the conscious kind and the unconscious kind that we don't even realize we're suffering from.

The path forward is clear: keep our promises to Allah ﷻ, especially the ones we make in times of difficulty. Be grateful when He grants us His blessings. And constantly examine our hearts to ensure that hypocrisy hasn't taken root there.

translation: Did they not know that Allah knows their secrets and their private conversations and that Allah is the Knower of the unseen
commentary:
أَلَمْ يَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ سِرَّهُمْ وَنَجْوَىٰهُمْ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَّـٰمُ ٱلْغُيُوبِ

Don't they know that Allah ﷻ knows their secret? He knows their secret councils, the gatherings they held to undermine the work of the Prophet ﷺ. And don't they know that Allah ﷻ is the ultimate Knower of all the unseen?

This ayah addresses those hypocrites who thought their private conversations and hidden schemes went unnoticed. They would meet in secret to plot against the believers and mock the Prophet's ﷺ mission. The phrase نَجْوَىٰهُمْ refers specifically to their secret councils and conspiracies. Allah ﷻ reminds them that nothing is hidden from Him. Every whisper, every plot, every thought they believed was concealed—He knows it all. The title عَلَّـٰمُ ٱلْغُيُوبِ, the ultimate Knower of all unseen matters, emphasizes that Allah's ﷻ knowledge extends beyond what any human could perceive.

translation: Those who criticize the contributors among the believers concerning [their] charities and [criticize] the ones who find nothing [to spend] except their effort, so they ridicule them - Allah will ridicule them, and they will have a painful punishment
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ يَلْمِزُونَ ٱلْمُطَّوِّعِينَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ فِى ٱلصَّدَقَـٰتِ وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَجِدُونَ إِلَّا جُهْدَهُمْ فَيَسْخَرُونَ مِنْهُمْ ۙ سَخِرَ ٱللَّهُ مِنْهُمْ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

This ayah exposes a particularly ugly characteristic of the hypocrites: they would mock and belittle those who contributed to the cause of Islam, regardless of whether they gave much or little.

The word يَلْمِزُونَ means to put blame on someone, to criticize them unfairly, to belittle their efforts. The ٱلْمُطَّوِّعِينَ are those who volunteer, who go out of their way to contribute something extra. These are believers who sacrificed their time, wealth and energy purely for the sake of Allah ﷻ.

There's a beautiful historical context to this ayah. One companion worked all night pulling water from a well just to earn a small amount of dates that he could contribute to the military campaign. When he brought his modest contribution, the hypocrites mocked him: "He thinks he has something to contribute? Look at him. Does he really think the army is going to be strengthened because of those few dates he earned?" They belittled his sincere effort.

But their mockery didn't stop at those who gave little. They also criticized the wealthy believers who contributed generously. No matter what the believers did, the hypocrites found something to mock. This reveals the emptiness of their criticism—it was never about the amount given, but about their hatred for anything that strengthened the Muslim community.

The phrase لَا يَجِدُونَ إِلَّا جُهْدَهُمْ is particularly beautiful: "they don't find anything to contribute except their own effort." This describes those believers who had nothing material to give but their labor, their time, their physical presence. Yet even this sincere offering became a target for the hypocrites' ridicule.

Allah's ﷻ response is powerful: سَخِرَ ٱللَّهُ مِنْهُمْ—"Allah mocked them." What does it mean for Allah ﷻ to mock them? On the Day of Judgment, when these hypocrites are burning in punishment, all they will receive from Allah ﷻ and the believers is mockery in return. And beyond that immediate recompense, وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ—they will have an exclusively painful punishment.

translation: Ask forgiveness for them, [O Muhammad], or do not ask forgiveness for them. If you should ask forgiveness for them seventy times - never will Allah forgive them. That is because they disbelieved in Allah and His Messenger, and Allah does not guide the defiantly disobedient people
commentary:
ٱسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ أَوْ لَا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ إِن تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ سَبْعِينَ مَرَّةً فَلَن يَغْفِرَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ

Allah ﷻ tells His Messenger ﷺ: ask forgiveness for them or don't ask forgiveness for them—it makes no difference. These particular hypocrites have reached a terminal stage. They've crossed a line by mocking the sincere believers who were making sacrifices in the path of Allah ﷻ. They're beyond hope.

Then Allah ﷻ adds a striking statement: إِن تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ سَبْعِينَ مَرَّةً فَلَن يَغْفِرَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ—"Even if you ask forgiveness for them seventy times, Allah will not forgive them."

But we must understand the emotional weight of this ayah. As we read these passages, we might feel anger toward these hypocrites. But imagine the Prophet's ﷺ reaction. His concern for his Ummah was so deep that when he heard "seventy times," his immediate thought was: "Then I'll ask more than seventy times." That was his sentiment toward this Ummah, even toward the worst of them.

The reason for Allah's ﷻ firm rejection is clear: ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ—"That is because they disbelieved in Allah and His Messenger." And the concluding statement is decisive: وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ—"Allah does not guide the corrupt nation."

translation: Those who remained behind rejoiced in their staying [at home] after [the departure of] the Messenger of Allah and disliked to strive with their wealth and their lives in the cause of Allah and said, "Do not go forth in the heat." Say, "The fire of Hell is more intensive in heat" - if they would but understand
commentary:
فَرِحَ ٱلْمُخَلَّفُونَ بِمَقْعَدِهِمْ خِلَـٰفَ رَسُولِ ٱللَّهِ وَكَرِهُوٓا۟ أَن يُجَـٰهِدُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَقَالُوا۟ لَا تَنفِرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْحَرِّ ۗ قُلْ نَارُ جَهَنَّمَ أَشَدُّ حَرًّا ۚ لَّوْ كَانُوا۟ يَفْقَهُونَ

Those who were left behind were extremely happy with staying where they stayed, going against the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. Notice the grammatical shift here: instead of using the active participle (those who stayed behind), Allah ﷻ uses the passive participle ٱلْمُخَلَّفُونَ—those who were made to stay behind.

This choice of words carries two meanings. First, it reinforces what Allah ﷻ already revealed—that He made them stay behind as a consequence of their hypocrisy. Second, it indicates that they were made to stay behind because they followed the authority of Iblis. He succeeded with them, and so they remained behind.

These people were happy to sit at home while the Prophet ﷺ and the believers went out to struggle. The phrase خِلَـٰفَ رَسُولِ ٱللَّهِ means they positioned themselves in opposition to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. They weren't just neutral—they actively opposed the mission.

وَكَرِهُوٓا۟ أَن يُجَـٰهِدُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ—they detested, they were disgusted by the very idea of having to struggle with their wealth and their lives in the path of Allah ﷻ.

Then they went further. Not only did they refuse to participate, but they also tried to discourage others: وَقَالُوا۟ لَا تَنفِرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْحَرِّ—"They said: Don't go out to fight in this intense heat." The context here is significant. The expedition to Tabuk required traveling through the desert during extreme heat. The hypocrites used this as an excuse and spread discouragement among others.

There's even a historical account of one man who came out intending to join the expedition, but when he felt the intense desert heat, he retreated back into the shade and ultimately stayed behind. The physical discomfort became an insurmountable obstacle for those whose hearts were weak.

Allah's ﷻ response cuts through their excuse with devastating clarity: قُلْ نَارُ جَهَنَّمَ أَشَدُّ حَرًّا—"Tell them: The fire of Hell is far hotter." The heat of the desert that they're trying to avoid is nothing—absolutely nothing—compared to the heat of Hellfire that awaits those who abandon their duty.

لَّوْ كَانُوا۟ يَفْقَهُونَ—"If only they had deep understanding." The word فِقْه here doesn't just mean surface-level knowledge. It means deep, grounded understanding that penetrates the heart and changes behavior. If they truly understood the reality of what awaits them, they would never have made such a foolish choice.

translation: So let them laugh a little and [then] weep much as recompense for what they used to earn
commentary:
فَلْيَضْحَكُوا۟ قَلِيلًا وَلْيَبْكُوا۟ كَثِيرًا جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ

Let them laugh a little, and let them cry much—as a compensation for what they used to earn.

These hypocrites were so pleased with themselves for staying behind. They thought they had cleverly avoided hardship. Allah ﷻ tells them: enjoy your laughter now, because it won't last. The crying that's coming will far exceed any momentary pleasure they're experiencing.

فَلْيَضْحَكُوا۟ قَلِيلًا—let them laugh a little. وَلْيَبْكُوا۟ كَثِيرًا—and let them cry much. The contrast between قَلِيلًا (little) and كَثِيرًا (much) is striking. Their laughter is temporary and limited. Their crying will be extensive and prolonged.

جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ—as recompense for what they earned for themselves. They earned this outcome through their own choices and actions.

This ayah is sometimes misused by people who don't understand its context. Someone might see another Muslim looking happy and quote this ayah as if Islam disapproves of happiness or laughter. But look at who Allah ﷻ is addressing here: hypocrites who were happy about abandoning the struggle in Allah's ﷻ path, who mocked the sincere believers, who actively discouraged others from participating in jihad.

The Prophet ﷺ himself smiled regularly. He had a sense of humor. He would joke with the companions (RA). There are times to be serious and times to be at ease. It's not anti-Islamic to have a personality or to experience joy. The idea that the more religious you become, the more socially withdrawn you must be—this has no basis in our religion.

The ayah isn't condemning happiness in general. It's condemning the specific joy that the hypocrites felt in abandoning their duty and undermining the Muslim community.

translation: If Allah should return you to a faction of them [after the expedition] and then they ask your permission to go out [to battle], say, "You will not go out with me, ever, and you will never fight with me an enemy. Indeed, you were satisfied with sitting [at home] the first time, so sit [now] with those who stay behind
commentary:
فَإِن رَّجَعَكَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَىٰ طَآئِفَةٍ مِّنْهُمْ فَٱسْتَـْٔذَنُوكَ لِلْخُرُوجِ فَقُل لَّن تَخْرُجُوا۟ مَعِىَ أَبَدًا وَلَن تُقَـٰتِلُوا۟ مَعِىَ عَدُوًّا ۖ إِنَّكُمْ رَضِيتُم بِٱلْقُعُودِ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ فَٱقْعُدُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلْخَـٰلِفِينَ

If Allah ﷻ brings you back to a group of them, and they ask your permission to go out with you, then tell them: "You will never come out with me again, ever. And you will never fight any enemy by my side."

This ayah establishes a permanent policy for those who refused to participate in the expedition to Tabuk. When the Prophet ﷺ returns and these hypocrites come asking for permission to join the next campaign—perhaps thinking they can make up for their absence—the divine instruction is clear and final: No. Your service in the Muslim military is over.

لَّن تَخْرُجُوا۟ مَعِىَ أَبَدًا—"You will never come out with me, ever." The word أَبَدًا means "never, for all time." This isn't a temporary suspension; it's a permanent dismissal.

وَلَن تُقَـٰتِلُوا۟ مَعِىَ عَدُوًّا—"And you will never fight any enemy alongside me." They've lost the honor of standing beside the Prophet ﷺ in battle.

The reason is stated clearly: إِنَّكُمْ رَضِيتُم بِٱلْقُعُودِ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ—"You were pleased with sitting back the first time around." When it mattered, when the test came, you chose comfort over duty. You were happy to stay behind.

فَٱقْعُدُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلْخَـٰلِفِينَ—"So sit with those who remain behind." Since you chose their company before, that's where you belong permanently.

translation: And do not pray [the funeral prayer, O Muhammad], over any of them who has died - ever - or stand at his grave. Indeed, they disbelieved in Allah and His Messenger and died while they were defiantly disobedient
commentary:
وَلَا تُصَلِّ عَلَىٰٓ أَحَدٍ مِّنْهُم مَّاتَ أَبَدًا وَلَا تَقُمْ عَلَىٰ قَبْرِهِۦٓ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَمَاتُوا۟ وَهُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ

And do not pray over any one of them who dies, ever. And do not stand at his grave.

This ayah takes the separation between believers and these particular hypocrites to its ultimate conclusion. Even in death, there can be no connection. The funeral prayer—the final act of communal respect and supplication for a Muslim—is forbidden for these individuals.

وَلَا تُصَلِّ عَلَىٰٓ أَحَدٍ مِّنْهُم مَّاتَ أَبَدًا—"Don't you pray over any one of them who dies, ever." This is directed specifically at the Prophet ﷺ, but it sets a principle. Even the Prophet's ﷺ prayer, with all its weight and blessing, is withheld from these people.

وَلَا تَقُمْ عَلَىٰ قَبْرِهِۦٓ—"And don't even stand at his grave." Don't acknowledge the grave. Don't make any supplication there. The separation is complete.

There's important historical context here. When Abdullah ibn Ubayy, the chief of the hypocrites, died, the Prophet ﷺ offered the funeral prayer for him. This ayah had not yet been revealed. After this incident, Allah ﷻ sent down this instruction: no more prayers for such people.

This represents the ultimate separation. The hypocrites were already separated from the believers in life—they couldn't join the military campaigns. Now they're separated even in death.

إِنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَمَاتُوا۟ وَهُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ—"They disbelieved in Allah and His Messenger, and they died while they were corrupt."

It's crucial to understand that this scenario—where someone is definitively labeled as a hypocrite by revelation—can never happen again. This was possible only during the time of the Prophet ﷺ when revelation was active. Allah ﷻ created a specific situation where refusing to participate in jihad became an official, legal label of hypocrisy, not just a spiritual one.

For us, for all time after the Prophet ﷺ, hypocrisy remains a spiritual matter, not a legal one. We cannot call someone a hypocrite. We don't have access to people's hearts. Only Allah ﷻ knows what's in the heart. We can observe behaviors and address those behaviors, but we cannot judge the person's inner state.

This distinction is critical. Just because someone exhibits symptoms doesn't mean they have the disease. Someone coughing doesn't necessarily mean they're terminally ill. Similarly, someone displaying problematic behavior doesn't automatically make them a hypocrite. In Islam, we have the responsibility to address wrong behavior, not to condemn people.

Unfortunately, these ayahs are sometimes misused by people who learned their religion prematurely or superficially. They throw these verses at each other during arguments. This is deeply wrong and obscene—to use the words of Allah ﷻ as ammunition in petty disputes.

Someone might get angry during a basketball game and another person immediately quotes the hadith about anger being a sign of hypocrisy, declaring them a quarter of the way to being a complete hypocrite. This kind of immaturity needs to stop. We cannot allow the misuse of sacred texts in casual conversation. A foul in a basketball game doesn't indicate a terminal spiritual disease that would lead someone to eternal Hellfire.

We must address behaviors—anger, lying, breaking promises—without condemning the entire person or declaring them to have a diseased heart.

translation: And let not their wealth and their children impress you. Allah only intends to punish them through them in this world and that their souls should depart [at death] while they are disbelievers
commentary:
وَلَا تُعْجِبْكَ أَمْوَٰلُهُمْ وَأَوْلَـٰدُهُمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ أَن يُعَذِّبَهُم بِهَا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَتَزْهَقَ أَنفُسُهُمْ وَهُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ

Don't be impressed by their wealth or their children.

This instruction appears for the second time in this Surah, and the context is important. When the hypocrites return from staying behind, they're still the wealthy ones. There may be more expeditions necessary, more expenses required for the community. The temptation might arise to overlook their hypocrisy because of their financial resources—to think that the community needs their wealth.

Allah ﷻ tells the Prophet ﷺ and the believers: don't be impressed by what they have. Don't let their material resources cloud your judgment about who they really are.

إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ أَن يُعَذِّبَهُم بِهَا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا—"Allah only intends to punish them by means of it in this world." Their wealth and children, the very things they're so attached to and that others might envy, become a source of torment for them in this life.

How? Through constant anxiety about losing it, through the responsibilities and burdens that come with it, through the way it distracts them from what truly matters, through the conflicts it creates. What appears to be a blessing becomes a punishment.

وَتَزْهَقَ أَنفُسُهُمْ وَهُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ—"And their souls depart while they are in a state of disbelief." The word تَزْهَقَ suggests a soul that leaves forcefully, with difficulty. They've lost themselves—their souls have departed from any connection to truth—and they remain in this state of kufr.

translation: And when a surah was revealed [enjoining them] to believe in Allah and to fight with His Messenger, those of wealth among them asked your permission [to stay back] and said, "Leave us to be with them who sit [at home]
commentary:
وَإِذَآ أُنزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ أَنْ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَجَـٰهِدُوا۟ مَعَ رَسُولِهِ ٱسْتَـْٔذَنَكَ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلطَّوْلِ مِنْهُمْ وَقَالُوا۟ ذَرْنَا نَكُن مَّعَ ٱلْقَـٰعِدِينَ

Whenever a Surah comes down instructing "Believe in Allah and struggle alongside His Messenger," those among them who have great means ask your permission to be excused.

The word ٱلطَّوْلِ refers to financial means, resources, capability. We've seen this word before in discussions about marriage—when someone doesn't have the financial means to marry. Here it describes those hypocrites who had abundant resources but used them as an excuse to avoid participation.

These weren't poor people who genuinely couldn't afford to participate. These were the wealthy ones who had every material capability to join the struggle but chose not to. They would come asking for permission to stay behind: ذَرْنَا نَكُن مَّعَ ٱلْقَـٰعِدِينَ—"Leave us, let us be with those who are staying behind."

Notice the phrase "with those who are staying behind." This is a derogatory reference to the women and children who remained in Madinah during military expeditions. These men wanted to stay with the non-combatants—the women and children—rather than join the fighting men. For men to actively choose this position is shameful in that cultural context.

translation: They were satisfied to be with those who stay behind, and their hearts were sealed over, so they do not understand
commentary:
رَضُوا۟ بِأَن يَكُونُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلْخَوَالِفِ وَطُبِعَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ

They were content to be with those who stayed behind, and a seal was placed upon their hearts, so they do not understand.

رَضُوا۟ بِأَن يَكُونُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلْخَوَالِفِ—"They were really happy to stay among the women and children." The word ٱلْخَوَالِفِ is a particularly derogatory term when applied to men—it emphasizes that they chose the position of those who remain behind while others go out to defend the community.

This wasn't just a neutral choice. They were pleased with this position. They actively wanted it.

The consequence: وَطُبِعَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ—"A seal has been placed upon their hearts." This is the spiritual result of their choices. When someone persistently chooses comfort over duty, appearance over reality, material security over faith, their heart eventually becomes sealed. They lose the ability to perceive truth.

فَهُمْ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ—"So they're not going to have understanding." They won't achieve that deep, grounded understanding that transforms a person. The word فِقْه here isn't just about knowledge—it's about comprehension that reaches the heart and changes how you live.

translation: But the Messenger and those who believed with him fought with their wealth and their lives. Those will have [all that is] good, and it is those who are the successful
commentary:
لَـٰكِنِ ٱلرَّسُولُ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ جَـٰهَدُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمُ ٱلْخَيْرَٰتُ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ

However, on the contrary, the Messenger and those who believed alongside him—they made struggles with their wealth and their lives.

After describing the hypocrites, Allah ﷻ shifts to honor the true believers. لَـٰكِنِ—"However, on the contrary"—marks this transition sharply.

ٱلرَّسُولُ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ—"The Messenger and those who believed alongside him." The word مَعَهُۥ, "with him," is critical here. Anyone can claim to have Iman. But now there's an additional condition, an additional proof: you have to be with him when he goes on the mission. You have to join him in the struggle.

This مَعَهُۥ isn't just a casual phrase. It's essential in this Surah. Being "with" the Prophet ﷺ in his mission is proof of genuine Iman.

جَـٰهَدُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ—"They struggled with their wealth and their lives." Both dimensions of sacrifice are mentioned: material sacrifice (wealth) and personal sacrifice (themselves—their time, energy, safety, comfort).

The reward is described in two parts:

وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمُ ٱلْخَيْرَٰتُ—"Those are the ones who will have multiple forms of good coming their way." The word ٱلْخَيْرَٰتُ is plural, indicating abundant, varied forms of goodness—not just one type of blessing, but many.

وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ—"And those are the ones who are ultimately successful." The word ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ indicates complete, ultimate success—not temporary worldly achievement, but true, lasting success in both worlds.

translation: Allah has prepared for them gardens beneath which rivers flow, wherein they will abide eternally. That is the great attainment
commentary:
أَعَدَّ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ

Allah has prepared for them gardens beneath which rivers flow, in which they will remain forever. That is the supreme achievement.

The language here is beautiful and deliberate. Instead of simply saying "they will have gardens" (لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ), Allah ﷻ says أَعَدَّ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ—"Allah has prepared for them."

This phrasing emphasizes several things: Allah ﷻ Himself has made something special for them. It's already prepared, waiting for them. It's custom-made, crafted by Allah ﷻ specifically for these believers who sacrificed alongside His Messenger ﷺ.

جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ—"Gardens beneath which rivers flow." The image of flowing water in a desert context represents the ultimate in comfort, beauty and abundance. For people who lived in the Arabian Peninsula, where water was precious and vegetation scarce, this description evokes the highest form of paradise.

خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا—"Remaining in them forever." The word خَـٰلِدِينَ emphasizes permanence, eternity. This isn't temporary. This isn't conditional. They will remain in these gardens forever.

ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ—"That is the supreme achievement." The word ٱلْفَوْزُ means success, victory, winning. ٱلْعَظِيمُ means tremendous, supreme, magnificent. This is the ultimate victory—greater than any worldly achievement, greater than any temporary comfort they might have gained by staying behind.

translation: And those with excuses among the bedouins came to be permitted [to remain], and they who had lied to Allah and His Messenger sat [at home]. There will strike those who disbelieved among them a painful punishment
commentary:
وَجَآءَ ٱلْمُعَذِّرُونَ مِنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ لِيُؤْذَنَ لَهُمْ وَقَعَدَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ ۚ سَيُصِيبُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

And those who make excuses from among the Bedouins came so that permission might be given to them.

The word ٱلْمُعَذِّرُونَ refers to those who make extensive excuses, who come with elaborate justifications. Many Bedouins came to Islam during this period, but they hadn't gone through the tarbiyah—the spiritual training and education—of Islam. For many of them, Islam was more of a social or political affiliation than a deeply internalized faith. They didn't really understand what it meant to be Muslim.

When the call came for the expedition to Tabuk, they heard through the grapevine that they might be able to get permission to stay behind. So groups of them organized, came up with their excuses, and presented themselves to the Prophet ﷺ seeking permission to be excused from military service.

There's an image here of a whole line of Bedouins coming, each with their prepared excuses, asking for permission to stay behind.

وَقَعَدَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ—"And those who lied against Allah and His Messenger, they sat." In other words, they didn't even bother coming with excuses. They simply stayed home. They lied in their commitment to Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ when they claimed to be Muslim, and then they demonstrated that lie by refusing to participate.

The ayah makes a distinction: some came with excuses (which may or may not have been legitimate), and others didn't even bother with that—they just stayed behind.

سَيُصِيبُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ—"Those who disbelieved among them will soon be struck with a painful punishment."

The phrase مِنْهُمْ, "among them," is important. Not all of them were disbelievers. Some of them had legitimate excuses—genuine reasons they couldn't participate. Some were sincere in their Islam but hadn't yet developed deep understanding. But among them were those who had no real faith, who were only affiliated with Islam to ride the wave of its success. Those specific individuals—the disbelievers among them—will face a painful punishment.

Allah ﷻ distinguishes between those with genuine limitations and those with hypocritical hearts. The punishment isn't for everyone who stayed behind, but specifically for ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ—those among them who disbelieved.

translation: There is not upon the weak or upon the ill or upon those who do not find anything to spend any discomfort when they are sincere to Allah and His Messenger. There is not upon the doers of good any cause [for blame]. And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
لَّيْسَ عَلَى ٱلضُّعَفَآءِ وَلَا عَلَى ٱلْمَرْضَىٰ وَلَا عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَجِدُونَ مَا يُنفِقُونَ حَرَجٌ إِذَا نَصَحُوا۟ لِلَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ۚ مَا عَلَى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ مِن سَبِيلٍ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

This ayah establishes the legitimate exemptions from participating in military campaigns. Allah ﷻ clarifies that there is no blame upon the weak (الضُّعَفَآءِ), the sick (الْمَرْضَىٰ), and those who lack the financial means to equip themselves. The structure of the ayah emphasizes exclusivity through the particle إِنَّمَا, indicating it is *only* upon these categories that there is no harm.

Consider the practical reality: someone ready to march 300 miles needs provisions, a weapon, and transport. If they cannot afford even basic necessities for the journey, they simply cannot go. The exemption recognizes this physical impossibility.

However, Allah ﷻ adds a crucial condition: إِذَا نَصَحُوا۟ لِلَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ - "if they are sincere to Allah and His Messenger." The word نَصَحُوا indicates genuine well-wishing and sincerity. This is not merely about telling the Messenger ﷺ "I wish I could go." Rather, it's about their heart's true state before Allah ﷻ. Even those who remain behind due to legitimate circumstances must maintain sincerity in their intention. In their hearts, they genuinely wish they were healthy or had the means to participate.

The ayah concludes with مَا عَلَى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ مِن سَبِيلٍ - "there is no case against those who excel." This is a classical Arabic expression meaning no complaint or accusation can be leveled against them. The term المُحْسِنِينَ (those who excel) indicates that even while staying behind for valid reasons, their sincerity and proper intention constitute a form of excellence. Allah ﷻ closes with His attributes: غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ - Forgiving and Merciful, acknowledging human limitation while recognizing sincere hearts.

translation: Nor [is there blame] upon those who, when they came to you that you might give them mounts, you said, "I can find nothing for you to ride upon." They turned back while their eyes overflowed with tears out of grief that they could not find something to spend [for the cause of Allah]
commentary:
وَلَا عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا مَآ أَتَوْكَ لِتَحْمِلَهُمْ قُلْتَ لَآ أَجِدُ مَآ أَحْمِلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ تَوَلَّوا۟ وَّأَعْيُنُهُمْ تَفِيضُ مِنَ ٱلدَّمْعِ حَزَنًا أَلَّا يَجِدُوا۟ مَا يُنفِقُونَ

This ayah presents a striking contrast to what came before. In earlier passages, wealthy individuals approached the Prophet ﷺ asking permission to stay behind, and when granted, they left happy and content. Now we see the poorest members of the community coming with the opposite request: they desperately want to go.

These believers came to the Prophet ﷺ asking لِتَحْمِلَهُمْ - for him to provide them transportation, typically a camel or horse to ride. The Messenger ﷺ had to tell them: لَآ أَجِدُ مَآ أَحْمِلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ - "I don't have anything to mount you upon." The resources had been exhausted; the last animal had been allocated.

Allah ﷻ captures their emotional state with profound imagery: تَوَلَّوا۟ وَّأَعْيُنُهُمْ تَفِيضُ مِنَ ٱلدَّمْعِ - "they turned away while their eyes were overflowing with tears." The verb تَفِيضُ suggests a continuous flowing, not just occasional tears but eyes pouring over with grief. The reason: حَزَنًا أَلَّا يَجِدُوا۟ مَا يُنفِقُونَ - sorrow that they could not find the means to spend for Allah's cause.

This scene captures a fundamental truth about sincerity. The rich came seeking exemption and left pleased. The poor came seeking participation and left weeping. Their financial poverty stood in stark contrast to their spiritual richness. Allah ﷻ immortalizes their tears in His Book as testimony to the sincerity He values above all material contribution.

translation: The cause [for blame] is only upon those who ask permission of you while they are rich. They are satisfied to be with those who stay behind, and Allah has sealed over their hearts, so they do not know
commentary:
إِنَّمَا ٱلسَّبِيلُ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْتَـْٔذِنُونَكَ وَهُمْ أَغْنِيَآءُ ۚ رَضُوا۟ بِأَن يَكُونُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلْخَوَالِفِ وَطَبَعَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

After establishing who bears no blame, Allah ﷻ now identifies who does: إِنَّمَا ٱلسَّبِيلُ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ - "The case is only against those who..." The word سَبِيل here means the grounds for blame or accusation.

These are people who sought permission to stay behind وَهُمْ أَغْنِيَآءُ - while they were wealthy. They had every capability to participate: health, strength, financial means, and physical ability. Unlike those in the previous ayahs who had legitimate barriers, these individuals faced no obstacle except their own unwillingness.

Their internal state is revealed: رَضُوا۟ بِأَن يَكُونُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلْخَوَالِفِ - "they were content to be among those who stayed behind." The term الْخَوَالِفِ refers to those who remain behind, typically women, children, and the elderly. These able-bodied men were satisfied staying with those who had legitimate reasons not to fight. They felt no shame in this position, no internal discomfort at being grouped with the exempt.

The ayah concludes with a severe pronouncement: وَطَبَعَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ - "Allah has sealed upon their hearts." This sealing is not arbitrary but consequential - it results from their own choices and contentment with remaining behind. The natural consequence: فَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ - "they do not know." They lack awareness of the gravity of their decision, the opportunity they've squandered, and the spiritual reality of their condition.

translation: They will make excuses to you when you have returned to them. Say, "Make no excuse - never will we believe you. Allah has already informed us of your news. And Allah will observe your deeds, and [so will] His Messenger; then you will be taken back to the Knower of the unseen and the witnessed, and He will inform you of what you used to do
commentary:
يَعْتَذِرُونَ إِلَيْكُمْ إِذَا رَجَعْتُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ ۚ قُل لَّا تَعْتَذِرُوا۟ لَن نُّؤْمِنَ لَكُمْ قَدْ نَبَّأَنَا ٱللَّهُ مِنْ أَخْبَارِكُمْ ۚ وَسَيَرَى ٱللَّهُ عَمَلَكُمْ وَرَسُولُهُۥ ثُمَّ تُرَدُّونَ إِلَىٰ عَـٰلِمِ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ

This ayah was revealed during the expedition itself, preparing the believers for what awaited them upon return. Allah ﷻ informs them: يَعْتَذِرُونَ إِلَيْكُمْ إِذَا رَجَعْتُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ - "They will present their excuses to you when you return to them."

The hypocrites were already preparing elaborate justifications for their absence. Allah ﷻ instructs the Prophet ﷺ with a response that seems uncharacteristic of his usual patient listening: قُل لَّا تَعْتَذِرُوا۟ - "Say: Do not make excuses." This represents a significant shift. Previously, the Prophet ﷺ would listen to everyone's explanations, which led to the criticism mentioned earlier in the Surah. Now Allah ﷻ instructs him to cut through the pretense entirely.

The reason is stated clearly: لَن نُّؤْمِنَ لَكُمْ - "We will never believe you." This isn't about being harsh; it's about acknowledging revealed truth. قَدْ نَبَّأَنَا ٱللَّهُ مِنْ أَخْبَارِكُمْ - "Allah has already informed us of your news." Divine revelation has exposed the reality, making their carefully crafted excuses irrelevant.

The ayah then shifts to a deeper accountability: وَسَيَرَى ٱللَّهُ عَمَلَكُمْ وَرَسُولُهُۥ - "Allah will observe your deeds, and His Messenger." This phrase has sparked scholarly discussion about the Prophet ﷺ witnessing the deeds of the Ummah even after his earthly life, with some texts suggesting that the actions of this community are presented to him.

The final warning brings ultimate perspective: ثُمَّ تُرَدُّونَ إِلَىٰ عَـٰلِمِ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ - "Then you will be returned to the Knower of the unseen and the witnessed." No excuse will work before the One who knows both what is hidden and what is manifest. فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَن - "He will inform you of what you used to do." Complete, thorough accountability awaits.

translation: They will swear by Allah to you when you return to them that you would leave them alone. So leave them alone; indeed they are evil; and their refuge is Hell as recompense for what they had been earning
commentary:
سَيَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ لَكُمْ إِذَا ٱنقَلَبْتُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ لِتُعْرِضُوا۟ عَنْهُمْ ۖ فَأَعْرِضُوا۟ عَنْهُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ رِجْسٌ ۖ وَمَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ

Allah ﷻ continues describing the hypocrites' behavior upon the believers' return: سَيَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ لَكُمْ - "They will swear by Allah to you." Not content with mere excuses, they will invoke oaths, using Allah's name to convince the returning soldiers of their sincerity.

Their motivation: لِتُعْرِضُوا۟ عَنْهُمْ - "so that you would overlook them," hoping their dramatic oaths would make the community forget their transgression and accept them back without question.

Allah's response is direct: فَأَعْرِضُوا۟ عَنْهُمْ - "Then turn away from them." Grant them exactly what they claim to want - being overlooked - but not in the way they intended. This turning away is not reconciliation but social rejection.

The reason is stated with stark severity: إِنَّهُمْ رِجْسٌ - "They are filth," or more accurately, a spiritual contamination. The word رِجْس denotes something deeply impure, a dangerous disease that can spread. Their presence poses a threat to the community's spiritual health.

Their ultimate destination: وَمَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ - "Their refuge is Hellfire." The word مَأْوَىٰ (refuge or dwelling place) carries bitter irony - the place they sought refuge from jihad leads to the place from which there is no refuge. جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ - "A recompense for what they used to earn." Their actions have been accumulating, and this is the natural harvest of what they planted.

translation: They swear to you so that you might be satisfied with them. But if you should be satisfied with them - indeed, Allah is not satisfied with a defiantly disobedient people
commentary:
يَحْلِفُونَ لَكُمْ لِتَرْضَوْا۟ عَنْهُمْ ۖ فَإِن تَرْضَوْا۟ عَنْهُمْ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَرْضَىٰ عَنِ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ

This ayah reinforces the previous message with an important clarification. يَحْلِفُونَ لَكُمْ لِتَرْضَوْا۟ عَنْهُمْ - "They swear to you so that you might be pleased with them." The hypocrites don't just want to be overlooked; they want active approval and acceptance from the Muslim community.

Allah ﷻ then presents a conditional statement that carries a profound warning: فَإِن تَرْضَوْا۟ عَنْهُمْ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَرْضَىٰ عَنِ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ - "Even if you are pleased with them, certainly Allah is not pleased with the corrupt people."

This reveals a critical principle: human approval means nothing when it contradicts divine judgment. The believers might feel social pressure to accept the hypocrites' oaths and reintegrate them to maintain community harmony. Allah ﷻ makes clear that such human considerations are irrelevant. His displeasure with الْقَوْمِ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (the corrupt people, those who deviate from His commands) stands regardless of human opinion.

The message is clear: seek Allah's pleasure, not the approval of people. If the entire community were to accept these hypocrites, it would not change Allah's judgment one bit. This ayah protects believers from the trap of prioritizing social cohesion over spiritual integrity.

translation: The bedouins are stronger in disbelief and hypocrisy and more likely not to know the limits of what [laws] Allah has revealed to His Messenger. And Allah is Knowing and Wise
commentary:
ٱلْأَعْرَابُ أَشَدُّ كُفْرًا وَنِفَاقًا وَأَجْدَرُ أَلَّا يَعْلَمُوا۟ حُدُودَ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ

Allah ﷻ now addresses the Bedouins (الْأَعْرَابُ), the desert-dwelling Arabs living on the outskirts of established settlements. This ayah requires careful understanding to avoid misapplication.

ٱلْأَعْرَابُ أَشَدُّ كُفْرًا وَنِفَاقًا - "The Bedouins are more intense in disbelief and hypocrisy." This is not a blanket condemnation of all Bedouins, as the following ayahs will clarify. Rather, it acknowledges a sociological reality: those living far from Medina lacked exposure to the Prophet's teachings, the refinement of character that occurred in his presence, and the transformative environment of the Muslim community.

They may have ceremonially embraced Islam, but they remained in the outskirts, never experiencing the tarbiyah (spiritual development) that Medinan Muslims received daily. Without that constant exposure to revelation, prophetic example, and community reinforcement, deeper problems with faith and sincerity are understandable.

وَأَجْدَرُ أَلَّا يَعْلَمُوا۟ حُدُودَ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ - "And more likely not to know the limits of what Allah has revealed to His Messenger." The word أَجْدَرُ means "more fitting" or "more likely." Distance from the source of revelation naturally results in less familiarity with its boundaries and requirements. This isn't entirely their fault - it's a consequence of geographical and social circumstances.

Allah ﷻ concludes: وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ - "And Allah is Knowing and Wise." He knows their circumstances, their challenges, their hearts. His judgment accounts for all factors with perfect wisdom.

translation: And among the bedouins are some who consider what they spend as a loss and await for you turns of misfortune. Upon them will be a misfortune of evil. And Allah is Hearing and Knowing
commentary:
وَمِنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ مَن يَتَّخِذُ مَا يُنفِقُ مَغْرَمًا وَيَتَرَبَّصُ بِكُمُ ٱلدَّوَآئِرَ ۚ عَلَيْهِمْ دَآئِرَةُ ٱلسَّوْءِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ

This ayah examines a specific problematic attitude among some Bedouins. وَمِنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ مَن يَتَّخِذُ مَا يُنفِقُ مَغْرَمًا - "Among the Bedouins are those who take what they spend as a penalty."

The context is crucial: Zakat had been implemented as an obligatory pillar of Islam. For the first time, these independent Bedouin tribes - who had never known central authority or taxation - now had to contribute 2.5% of their wealth to a central government in Medina. To them, this felt like an unwelcome tax imposed by distant authorities.

Their mindset resembles those who resist government oversight: "We don't want big government interfering in our lives." They viewed Zakat not as purification of wealth or means of earning Allah's pleasure, but as مَغْرَمًا - a financial penalty or loss.

وَيَتَرَبَّصُ بِكُمُ ٱلدَّوَآئِرَ - "And they wait for misfortune to befall you." The word يَتَرَبَّصُ means to wait expectantly. They hoped circumstances would turn against the Muslims, perhaps the Prophet's government would collapse, and they could return to their previous autonomous existence without religious obligations. This attitude was vindicated when, immediately after the Prophet's death ﷺ, many Bedouin tribes apostatized, refusing to pay Zakat.

Their relationship with Islam was so superficial that the only aspect they noticed was the financial obligation. That 2.5% occupied their entire consciousness regarding religion.
