Allah ﷻ responds: عَلَيْهِمْ دَآئِرَةُ ٱلسَّوْءِ - "Upon them will be the circle of misfortune." The very turning of events they hoped would afflict the Muslims will instead circle back upon them. وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ - "Allah is Hearing and Knowing." Though they may be on the distant outskirts, imagining their complaints unheard, Allah ﷻ hears everything and knows their true intentions.

translation: But among the bedouins are some who believe in Allah and the Last Day and consider what they spend as means of nearness to Allah and of [obtaining] invocations of the Messenger. Unquestionably, it is a means of nearness for them. Allah will admit them to His mercy. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
وَمِنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ مَن يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَيَتَّخِذُ مَا يُنفِقُ قُرُبَـٰتٍ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَصَلَوَٰتِ ٱلرَّسُولِ ۚ أَلَآ إِنَّهَا قُرْبَةٌ لَّهُمْ ۚ سَيُدْخِلُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ فِى رَحْمَتِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

After describing problematic Bedouins, Allah ﷻ immediately clarifies that generalization is forbidden. وَمِنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ مَن يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ - "And among the Bedouins are those who truly believe in Allah and the Last Day."

These believers have a completely different relationship with their spending: وَيَتَّخِذُ مَا يُنفِقُ قُرُبَـٰتٍ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ - "And they take what they spend as a means of getting closer to Allah." The word قُرُبَـٰتٍ comes from قُرْب, meaning closeness or proximity. Each dirham they spend, they view as bringing them nearer to their Lord.

This reveals a profound spiritual understanding: Sadaqah is not merely about reward points in the hereafter. It's about proximity to Allah ﷻ in this life. Every sincere contribution creates spiritual closeness, bridging the distance between servant and Lord.

وَصَلَوَٰتِ ٱلرَّسُولِ - "And the prayers of the Messenger." They also hope that their spending will earn them the Prophet's supplications ﷺ. When they give, he prays for them, and they understand the value of being in the dua of Allah's Messenger ﷺ.

Allah ﷻ confirms their understanding: أَلَآ إِنَّهَا قُرْبَةٌ لَّهُمْ - "Know well that it is indeed a means of closeness for them." Their perception is absolutely correct. Their spending truly does bring them closer to Allah ﷻ.

The promise: سَيُدْخِلُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ فِى رَحْمَتِهِۦٓ - "Allah will soon admit them into His mercy." The same geographical distance from Medina, the same Bedouin circumstances, but an entirely different heart produces an entirely different outcome. إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ - "Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful."

translation: And the first forerunners [in the faith] among the Muhajireen and the Ansar and those who followed them with good conduct - Allah is pleased with them and they are pleased with Him, and He has prepared for them gardens beneath which rivers flow, wherein they will abide forever. That is the great attainment
commentary:
وَٱلسَّـٰبِقُونَ ٱلْأَوَّلُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُهَـٰجِرِينَ وَٱلْأَنصَارِ وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوهُم بِإِحْسَـٰنٍ رَّضِىَ ٱللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا۟ عَنْهُ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى تَحْتَهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ

After discussing the hypocrites and the mixed character of the Bedouins, Allah ﷻ now honors the true pioneers of Islam. وَٱلسَّـٰبِقُونَ ٱلْأَوَّلُونَ - "The foremost, the first ones." This term السَّـٰبِقُونَ carries deep psychological and spiritual significance beyond mere chronological priority.

Consider the courage required to be first. When the Messenger ﷺ called people to Islam, he wasn't asking for a small commitment - he demanded complete submission of life priorities and acceptance of him as the final divine authority. Responding to such a call requires looking past all social consequences.

Most people hesitate, waiting to see what others will do. We're deeply concerned with social opinion - it influences how we dress, decorate our homes, and make major life decisions. The Sabiqun didn't wait to see what others thought. They didn't look around the room before raising their hands. They simply stood up and said, "I'm ready. I don't care what anyone else says. This is truth, and I'll face whatever consequences come."

By the time this ayah was revealed, Muslims numbered in the thousands. Becoming Muslim had become relatively easy because everyone around you was Muslim. But those first believers? They were the only ones in their community doing something everyone else considered insanity.

The term Sabiqun applies to مِنَ ٱلْمُهَـٰجِرِينَ وَٱلْأَنصَارِ - "from the Muhajirun and the Ansar." Each group had to be first in their context: the Muhajirun were first in Mecca, and the Ansar were first in Medina. Being first in your community requires exceptional courage.

Then Allah ﷻ extends the honor: وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوهُم بِإِحْسَـٰنٍ - "And those who followed them with excellence." This includes all subsequent generations, including us. We too must be Sabiqun in a sense - willing to take steps in our lives that defy what our entire family or society is doing for the sake of Allah's commands.

Many of you come from Muslim backgrounds, yet your families and extended communities aren't very Islamic. When you decide to live according to Islam, you become the target of significant criticism. You must first decide: will I live based on what people say, or based on what Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ say? Then you must mentally prepare for the consequences of that choice.

Consider the example of a young woman from rural Alabama - the daughter of a priest who saw the Kaaba in dreams without knowing what it was. She Googled "worshiping cube," found Islam, and converted despite being kicked out of her home. When she tried to visit a mosque, she was initially turned away because sisters weren't allowed in that section. She nearly gave up, thinking Islam wasn't for her. But she persisted, found proper guidance, earned a medical school scholarship, and continued despite complete family ostracization. That is what being Sabiqun means in her context.

The divine response to such courage: رَّضِىَ ٱللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا۟ عَنْهُ - "Allah is pleased with them, and they are pleased with Him." This mutual pleasure is the ultimate achievement. They didn't seek human approval; they sought and found divine approval. In turn, they are pleased with Him - with His decree, His commands, His plan for their lives.

The reward: وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى تَحْتَهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ - "And He has prepared for them gardens beneath which rivers flow." خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا - "They will remain therein forever." The temporary sacrifices of this world yield permanent joy in the next.

ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ - "That is the great success." After mentioning various groups - hypocrites who thought they succeeded by avoiding hardship, Bedouins who thought they succeeded by avoiding taxes - Allah ﷻ defines true success. It's not comfort or wealth or social approval. True success is earning Allah's pleasure and eternal paradise.

translation: And among those around you of the bedouins are hypocrites, and [also] from the people of Madinah. They have become accustomed to hypocrisy. You, [O Muhammad], do not know them, [but] We know them. We will punish them twice [in this world]; then they will be returned to a great punishment
commentary:
وَمِمَّنْ حَوْلَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ مُنَـٰفِقُونَ ۖ وَمِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْمَدِينَةِ ۖ مَرَدُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلنِّفَاقِ لَا تَعْلَمُهُمْ ۖ نَحْنُ نَعْلَمُهُمْ ۚ سَنُعَذِّبُهُم مَّرَّتَيْنِ ثُمَّ يُرَدُّونَ إِلَىٰ عَذَابٍ عَظِيمٍ

Allah ﷻ concludes this section with a sobering reality check about hypocrisy existing within the community itself. وَمِمَّنْ حَوْلَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ مُنَـٰفِقُونَ - "And among those around you from the Bedouins are hypocrites."

But the hypocrisy isn't limited to the outskirts: وَمِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْمَدِينَةِ - "And from the people of Medina." Even within the Prophet's city, among those who pray behind him and hear his teachings daily, there exist hypocrites.

مَرَدُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلنِّفَاقِ - "They have become bent upon hypocrisy." The verb مَرَدُوا۟ suggests they've become hardened in it, stuck in hypocrisy as a fixed state. They're no longer wavering or experimenting with it - they're established hypocrites.

Then comes a statement that must have been deeply unsettling: لَا تَعْلَمُهُمْ - "You do not know them." The Prophet ﷺ himself, with all his insight and wisdom, doesn't know who they are. They're that skilled at concealing their true nature. If even the Messenger ﷺ cannot identify them, how can any of us claim to definitively know who the hypocrites are in our communities?

نَحْنُ نَعْلَمُهُمْ - "We know them." While they hide from human perception, nothing is hidden from Allah ﷻ. He knows exactly who they are, their internal states, their true motivations.

سَنُعَذِّبُهُم مَّرَّتَيْنِ - "We will punish them twice." The scholars discuss what these two punishments entail. The first likely refers to the internal psychological torment they'll experience - the anxiety of maintaining deception, the social consequences when some of their hypocrisy is exposed, and specific earthly trials Allah ﷻ will send. Allah ﷻ will use their own wealth and children as means of punishing them in this life.

The second punishment relates to the social boycott that will be revealed shortly - a complete communal abandonment. When believers returned from the expedition, hypocrites had to present their excuses. One sincere believer who had mistakenly stayed behind came forward and simply admitted his fault without elaborate justification. His honesty revealed he wasn't a hypocrite, just someone with weak faith at that moment.

The distinction is critical: weak iman is not the same as hypocrisy. How do you know which one you have? If you can admit your mistakes and shortcomings, you haven't crossed into hypocrisy yet. You're still struggling with weak faith, which can be strengthened. But when you lose the ability to admit you're wrong, when you constantly justify and make excuses, you've begun the dangerous progression toward hypocrisy.

ثُمَّ يُرَدُّونَ إِلَىٰ عَذَابٍ عَظِيمٍ - "Then they will be returned to a tremendous punishment." After the two worldly punishments comes the ultimate accountability. The temporary discomfort they sought to avoid by staying behind will be nothing compared to the eternal punishment awaiting them.

This ayah serves as both warning and comfort: warning that hypocrisy can exist even in the most sacred spaces, and comfort that Allah ﷻ knows the truth even when we cannot perceive it. Our responsibility is not to hunt for hypocrites but to guard our own hearts against hypocrisy's subtle beginnings.

translation: And [there are] others who have acknowledged their sins. They had mixed a righteous deed with another that was bad. Perhaps Allah will turn to them in forgiveness. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
وَءَاخَرُونَ ٱعْتَرَفُوا۟ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ خَلَطُوا۟ عَمَلًا صَـٰلِحًا وَءَاخَرَ سَيِّئًا عَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

This ayah introduces a distinct category of people, separate from the hypocrites discussed previously. These are believers who openly acknowledged their wrongdoing. The phrase اعْتَرَفُوا۟ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ (they admitted to their sins) marks them as fundamentally different from the munafiqun who concealed their true intentions.

The description خَلَطُوا۟ عَمَلًا صَـٰلِحًا وَءَاخَرَ سَيِّئًا (they have mixed good deeds with bad deeds) captures their spiritual state perfectly. They are neither hypocrites nor exemplary believers. Rather, they represent the reality of many Muslims—people who have faith and perform righteous actions, yet also fall into sin. Their lives are characterized by this mixture, this back-and-forth between obedience and disobedience.

The word عَسَى (perhaps) followed by أَن يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ (Allah will accept their repentance) offers tremendous hope. When Allah ﷻ uses عَسَى in the Quran, it carries the weight of divine promise. The ayah doesn't guarantee their forgiveness immediately, but it opens the door wide, signaling that Allah's mercy is available to those who acknowledge their mistakes.

The closing إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (Certainly Allah is extremely forgiving, always merciful) reinforces this message of hope. The combination of غَفُورٌ (intensively forgiving) and رَّحِيمٌ (constantly merciful) emphasizes that Allah's nature is to forgive and show mercy to those who turn to Him in sincerity.

translation: Take, [O, Muhammad], from their wealth a charity by which you purify them and cause them increase, and invoke [Allah 's blessings] upon them. Indeed, your invocations are reassurance for them. And Allah is Hearing and Knowing
commentary:
خُذْ مِنْ أَمْوَٰلِهِمْ صَدَقَةً تُطَهِّرُهُمْ وَتُزَكِّيهِم بِهَا وَصَلِّ عَلَيْهِمْ ۖ إِنَّ صَلَوٰتَكَ سَكَنٌ لَّهُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ

This ayah presents a stark contrast to how the Prophet ﷺ was commanded to deal with the hypocrites. Regarding the hypocrites, he was told not to accept their charity and not to pray for them. But here, Allah ﷻ instructs the Prophet ﷺ: خُذْ مِنْ أَمْوَٰلِهِمْ صَدَقَةً (Take charity from their wealth).

The acceptance of their sadaqah itself is a sign of Allah's acceptance of them. The Companions who fell into this category had brought charity as compensation for their mistakes, eager for it to be accepted. The instruction to take it validates their sincerity and their standing before Allah ﷻ.

The dual function of charity is beautifully expressed: تُطَهِّرُهُمْ وَتُزَكِّيهِم بِهَا (it will purify them and cleanse them). The word تُطَهِّرُ refers to external, physical purification, while تُزَكِّي encompasses spiritual cleansing and growth. Charity doesn't just remove the stain of sin; it elevates the person, helping them grow in righteousness.

The command وَصَلِّ عَلَيْهِمْ (and pray upon them) carries immense significance. The Prophet's supplication for someone was a tremendous blessing, a means of divine mercy reaching that person. The ayah explains why: إِنَّ صَلَوٰتَكَ سَكَنٌ لَّهُمْ (Your prayer is a means of tranquility for them). The word سَكَنٌ indicates the absence of disturbance, a deep calm. These believers were disturbed by their mistakes, troubled by their sins. The prayer of the Prophet ﷺ would bring them peace and reassurance.

Compare this to the explicit prohibition regarding hypocrites: don't accept their charity, don't pray for them, and even if you seek forgiveness for them seventy times, it will be pointless. The contrast highlights the fundamental difference between those who sincerely acknowledge their faults and those who persist in deception.

The closing وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (And Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing) reminds us that Allah ﷻ hears the prayers offered for these believers and knows the sincerity of their repentance.

translation: Do they not know that it is Allah who accepts repentance from His servants and receives charities and that it is Allah who is the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful
commentary:
أَلَمْ يَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ يَقْبَلُ ٱلتَّوْبَةَ عَنْ عِبَادِهِۦ وَيَأْخُذُ ٱلصَّدَقَـٰتِ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

This ayah contains a powerful reminder that shifts the focus directly to Allah ﷻ as the source of acceptance. The rhetorical question أَلَمْ يَعْلَمُوٓا۟ (Don't they know?) prompts reflection: shouldn't this be obvious already?

The emphasis in أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ يَقْبَلُ ٱلتَّوْبَةَ عَنْ عِبَادِهِۦ (that Allah, He Himself accepts repentance from His servants) is striking. The pronoun هُوَ adds weight—Allah Himself, directly, without intermediary, accepts the repentance of His servants. While the prayer of the Prophet ﷺ is beautiful and brings comfort, it is not a substitute for one's own sincere turning to Allah ﷻ.

Similarly, وَيَأْخُذُ ٱلصَّدَقَـٰتِ (and He takes the charities) emphasizes that Allah ﷻ is the ultimate accepter of sadaqah. The Messenger ﷺ physically collects it, but Allah ﷻ is the One who truly accepts and rewards it.

The message here balances the importance of seeking the Prophet's supplication with the essential truth that one must develop their own direct relationship with Allah ﷻ. We should long for the Messenger's prayers and seek the intercession of the righteous, but this cannot replace our own tawbah, our own turning back to Allah ﷻ with sincerity.

The closing وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمٌ (and that Allah, He is the One who continually accepts repentance, the Always Merciful) uses two intensive forms. التَّوَّابُ (the continual accepter of repentance) and الرَّحِيمُ (the perpetually merciful) convey that Allah's door is always open, His mercy always available to those who seek it sincerely.

translation: And say, "Do [as you will], for Allah will see your deeds, and [so, will] His Messenger and the believers. And you will be returned to the Knower of the unseen and the witnessed, and He will inform you of what you used to do
commentary:
وَقُلِ ٱعْمَلُوا۟ فَسَيَرَى ٱللَّهُ عَمَلَكُمْ وَرَسُولُهُۥ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ۖ وَسَتُرَدُّونَ إِلَىٰ عَـٰلِمِ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ

After acknowledging their past mistakes and receiving the promise of Allah's mercy, these believers needed direction for moving forward. The command ٱعْمَلُوا۟ (Work! Put yourselves to work!) is direct and energizing. Having made tawbah, now is the time to prove that repentance through action.

The phrase فَسَيَرَى ٱللَّهُ عَمَلَكُمْ (then soon Allah will see your deeds) carries both encouragement and accountability. Allah ﷻ will witness how they compensate for their previous mistakes through their renewed commitment to righteous action.

Adding وَرَسُولُهُۥ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (and His Messenger and the believers) extends this witnessing to the community. The believers around them will also see their transformation, their efforts to make amends. This public dimension matters—the community observes whether someone's repentance is genuine or merely words.

The reminder وَسَتُرَدُّونَ إِلَىٰ عَـٰلِمِ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ (Then you will be returned to the Knower of the unseen and the seen) shifts the perspective to the ultimate accountability. Allah ﷻ knows both what is hidden (الْغَيْبِ) and what is apparent (الشَّهَـٰدَةِ). No deed, intention or secret escapes His knowledge.

The conclusion فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (then He will thoroughly inform you of what you used to do) indicates the Day of Judgment, when every action will be brought forth and accounted for. The comprehensive knowledge of Allah ﷻ means that the accountability will be complete and perfect.

translation: And [there are] others deferred until the command of Allah - whether He will punish them or whether He will forgive them. And Allah is Knowing and Wise
commentary:
وَءَاخَرُونَ مُرْجَوْنَ لِأَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ إِمَّا يُعَذِّبُهُمْ وَإِمَّا يَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ

This ayah addresses yet another category of people, distinct from those mentioned in ayah 102. The word مُرْجَوْنَ (suspended, left hanging) comes from the root meaning to delay or postpone. These are people whose verdict has been intentionally suspended by Allah ﷻ.

This includes those famous Companions who physically tied themselves to the pillars of the masjid as a demonstration of their remorse, refusing to free themselves until the Prophet ﷺ freed them. It also includes Ka'b ibn Malik (RA), whose story is particularly powerful—he was boycotted by the entire Muslim community for fifty days, to the point where even his own wife would not speak to him.

These believers were left in a state of suspense, anxiously waiting for لِأَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ (the command of Allah). They didn't know whether they would be forgiven or punished. The phrase إِمَّا يُعَذِّبُهُمْ وَإِمَّا يَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ (either He will punish them or He will accept their repentance) captures this uncertainty.

This suspension served a purpose. It tested their sincerity, allowed them to demonstrate genuine remorse, and gave them time to reflect deeply on their mistakes. The waiting itself was part of their purification.

The closing وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (And Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise) assures us that this suspension wasn't arbitrary. Allah ﷻ, in His perfect knowledge and wisdom, knew exactly how long each person needed to remain in this state of uncertainty before His verdict would be revealed.

translation: And [there are] those [hypocrites] who took for themselves a mosque for causing harm and disbelief and division among the believers and as a station for whoever had warred against Allah and His Messenger before. And they will surely swear, "We intended only the best." And Allah testifies that indeed they are liars
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مَسْجِدًا ضِرَارًا وَكُفْرًا وَتَفْرِيقًۢا بَيْنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَإِرْصَادًا لِّمَنْ حَارَبَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ مِن قَبْلُ ۚ وَلَيَحْلِفُنَّ إِنْ أَرَدْنَآ إِلَّا ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ

This ayah introduces one of the most insidious plots against the Muslim community—the construction of Masjid Dirar (the Mosque of Harm). To understand this fully, we must know the background of Abu Amir al-Rahib, a Christian scholar originally from Khazraj in Madinah. Unlike Waraqah ibn Nawfal (RA), who recognized the Prophet ﷺ and affirmed his message, Abu Amir recognized the truth yet chose to oppose it out of hatred.

His animosity was so intense that when the Prophet ﷺ established Islam in Madinah, Abu Amir moved to Makkah. He even participated in the Battle of Uhud alongside the mushrikun, attempting before the battle to convince the Ansar—many of whom had been Christians before Islam and respected him—to abandon the Prophet ﷺ. When his efforts failed and Islam continued to grow, eventually conquering Makkah itself, he fled to Syria.

But Abu Amir wasn't finished. He maintained contact with his old companions in Madinah, conscious hypocrites who pretended to be Muslims while serving as spies. Together, they conceived a plan: build a mosque on the outskirts of Madinah, not far from Masjid Quba. This building would serve as a front for covert activities—intelligence gathering, secret meetings, and plotting against the Muslims.

The ayah lists four devastating purposes for this structure: ضِرَارًا (with intent of harm), وَكُفْرًا (and to spread disbelief), وَتَفْرِيقًۢا بَيْنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (and to cause division among the Muslims), وَإِرْصَادًا لِّمَنْ حَارَبَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ مِن قَبْلُ (and as a base of ambush for those who had waged war against Allah and His Messenger before).

This last purpose is particularly strategic. If an army or group of assassins wanted to attack Madinah, they would now have a safe house, a place to stay and prepare their assault. No one would question their presence because they would appear to be Muslims praying in a mosque. With the rapid growth of Islam, it had become difficult to authenticate who was genuinely Muslim, making this disguise especially effective.

Despite these evil intentions, the builders maintained their deception: وَلَيَحْلِفُنَّ إِنْ أَرَدْنَآ إِلَّا ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ (And they swear: We intended nothing but the best). They presented themselves as pious Muslims building a place of worship with pure motives.

But Allah ﷻ exposes the truth: وَٱللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ (And Allah Himself testifies that they are indeed liars). No matter how convincing their oaths, no matter how religious their appearance, Allah ﷻ sees through their deception and declares their falsehood.

translation: Do not stand [for prayer] within it - ever. A mosque founded on righteousness from the first day is more worthy for you to stand in. Within it are men who love to purify themselves; and Allah loves those who purify themselves
commentary:
لَا تَقُمْ فِيهِ أَبَدًا ۚ لَّمَسْجِدٌ أُسِّسَ عَلَى ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ مِنْ أَوَّلِ يَوْمٍ أَحَقُّ أَن تَقُومَ فِيهِ ۚ فِيهِ رِجَالٌ يُحِبُّونَ أَن يَتَطَهَّرُوا۟ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُطَّهِّرِينَ

The command to the Prophet ﷺ is unequivocal: لَا تَقُمْ فِيهِ أَبَدًا (Never stand in it. Ever.). The word أَبَدًا (ever, eternally) leaves no room for compromise. This wasn't a temporary prohibition—the Prophet ﷺ was forbidden from ever praying in Masjid Dirar.

This prohibition carried crucial wisdom. If the Prophet ﷺ had prayed there even once, it would have legitimized the structure in the eyes of the Muslims. Whatever the Prophet ﷺ does becomes sunnah, something the community follows. The Muslims would have thought, "If the Prophet prays there, surely it's obligatory for us to pray there too." The entire purpose of exposing this mosque as a front for hostile activities would have been undermined.

The masjid was subsequently destroyed, torn down to prevent it from serving its malicious purposes.

In contrast, Allah ﷻ directs attention to لَّمَسْجِدٌ أُسِّسَ عَلَى ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ مِنْ أَوَّلِ يَوْمٍ (a masjid established on the foundation of taqwa from the very first day)—a reference to Masjid Quba. This masjid was built on pure intentions, on consciousness of Allah ﷻ, from its very inception.

The phrase أَحَقُّ أَن تَقُومَ فِيهِ (it is more deserving that you stand in it) established the sunnah of visiting and praying in Masjid Quba. To this day, visitors to Madinah find this ayah displayed on the walls of Masjid Quba, and the practice of praying there continues as an established sunnah.

Allah ﷻ praises the people of this masjid: فِيهِ رِجَالٌ يُحِبُّونَ أَن يَتَطَهَّرُوا۟ (In it are men who love to purify themselves). When these men were asked why Allah ﷻ praised them specifically, they explained their practices of maintaining wudu consistently and using both water and earth (stones or dirt) for purification after relieving themselves—a practice that demonstrates thoroughness in physical and spiritual cleanliness.

The ayah concludes: وَٱللَّهُ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُطَّهِّرِينَ (And Allah loves those who make efforts to purify themselves). The word ٱلْمُطَّهِّرِينَ indicates those who actively, continuously work to purify themselves—not just physically, but in all dimensions of their being.

translation: Then is one who laid the foundation of his building on righteousness [with fear] from Allah and [seeking] His approval better or one who laid the foundation of his building on the edge of a bank about to collapse, so it collapsed with him into the fire of Hell? And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people
commentary:
أَفَمَنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيَـٰنَهُۥ عَلَىٰ تَقْوَىٰ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرِضْوَٰنٍ خَيْرٌ أَم مَّنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيَـٰنَهُۥ عَلَىٰ شَفَا جُرُفٍ هَارٍ فَٱنْهَارَ بِهِۦ فِى نَارِ جَهَنَّمَ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

This ayah presents a powerful comparison between two foundations, but with a brilliant linguistic shift. While the discussion began with the foundations of actual buildings (the two masjids), it now transitions to the foundations of character and faith themselves.

The rhetorical question begins: أَفَمَنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيَـٰنَهُۥ عَلَىٰ تَقْوَىٰ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرِضْوَٰنٍ (Is the one who established his foundation upon taqwa that comes from Allah and seeking His pleasure better...?). Notice the phrase تَقْوَىٰ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ—taqwa that stems from consciousness of Allah ﷻ. The foundation isn't just taqwa in abstract; it's taqwa rooted in awareness of Allah ﷻ and the desire for His pleasure (رِضْوَٰنٍ).

This describes someone whose entire character, whose very foundation as a person, is built on God-consciousness and the pursuit of Allah's approval. Every decision, every action flows from this bedrock.

The contrast: أَم مَّنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيَـٰنَهُۥ عَلَىٰ شَفَا جُرُفٍ هَارٍ (or the one who established his foundation on the brink of a cliff about to collapse?). The imagery is vivid and terrifying. The word شَفَا means edge or brink. جُرُفٍ refers to a rock formation that water constantly erodes, weakening it over time. هَارٍ means something on the verge of collapse, about to fall.

Imagine building a house on the edge of a crumbling cliff. It's not a question of if it will fall, but when. The structure might stand for a moment, but its foundation is inherently unstable, doomed to fail.

Then comes the inevitable result: فَٱنْهَارَ بِهِۦ فِى نَارِ جَهَنَّمَ (so it collapses with him into the fire of Hell). The verb انْهَارَ is specifically used for buildings collapsing, and particularly for collapses that crush what's beneath them. The person built on this false foundation doesn't just fall—the entire structure collapses on top of him, and he plunges with it into the fire of Jahannam.

Where you might expect water at the bottom of a cliff, Allah ﷻ places fire—the fire of Hell itself. This is the ultimate destination of those who build their lives on foundations other than taqwa.

The ayah closes with وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people). Those who choose to establish their lives on false foundations, who persist in wrongdoing, will not receive divine guidance to save them from their inevitable collapse.

translation: Their building which they built will not cease to be a [cause of] skepticism in their hearts until their hearts are stopped. And Allah is Knowing and Wise
commentary:
لَا يَزَالُ بُنْيَـٰنُهُمُ ٱلَّذِى بَنَوْا۟ رِيبَةً فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ إِلَّآ أَن تَقَطَّعَ قُلُوبُهُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ

This final ayah reveals the internal consequence of building on a false foundation. The phrase لَا يَزَالُ بُنْيَـٰنُهُمُ ٱلَّذِى بَنَوْا۟ رِيبَةً فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ (That foundation they built continues to be a source of doubt in their hearts) masterfully unifies the two foundations discussed: the physical structure of Masjid Dirar and the spiritual structure of their character built without taqwa.

Both foundations have merged into one poisonous reality. The building they constructed and the false character they developed have become an ongoing source of رِيبَةً (doubt, suspicion, unease) in their hearts. This doubt doesn't fade with time—لَا يَزَالُ (it continues, it persists). Every day, that foundation breeds more uncertainty, more spiritual disease in their hearts.

The exception clause is chilling: إِلَّآ أَن تَقَطَّعَ قُلُوبُهُمْ (unless their hearts are cut to pieces). The verb تَقَطَّعَ indicates being cut into many pieces, torn apart completely. This imagery recalls a medical analogy: when cancer spreads to a single limb, amputation might save the patient. But when cancer has metastasized throughout the entire body, reaching every organ and cell, there's nothing left to cut away. The disease has become terminal.

Similarly, the spiritual disease of nifaq (hypocrisy) has spread so thoroughly through every chamber of their hearts that removal is impossible. You would have to literally shred their hearts to pieces to extract all the doubt and deception embedded within. The corruption has become inseparable from their hearts themselves.

This isn't merely about the physical building anymore. It's about the psychological and spiritual reality of those who live double lives, who build facades of righteousness while harboring harmful intentions. That deception becomes part of them, a constant source of internal turmoil and doubt that never leaves them.

The ayah concludes as so many in this passage do: وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (And Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise). Allah ﷻ knows the depths of their deception, the extent of the disease in their hearts, and in His perfect wisdom has revealed their true nature and warned the believers about their plots.

translation: Indeed, Allah has purchased from the believers their lives and their properties [in exchange] for that they will have Paradise. They fight in the cause of Allah, so they kill and are killed. [It is] a true promise [binding] upon Him in the Torah and the Gospel and the Qur'an. And who is truer to his covenant than Allah? So rejoice in your transaction which you have contracted. And it is that which is the great attainment
commentary:
۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱشْتَرَىٰ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَأَمْوَٰلَهُم بِأَنَّ لَهُمُ ٱلْجَنَّةَ ۚ يُقَـٰتِلُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَيَقْتُلُونَ وَيُقْتَلُونَ ۖ وَعْدًا عَلَيْهِ حَقًّا فِى ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ وَٱلْإِنجِيلِ وَٱلْقُرْءَانِ ۚ وَمَنْ أَوْفَىٰ بِعَهْدِهِۦ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَٱسْتَبْشِرُوا۟ بِبَيْعِكُمُ ٱلَّذِى بَايَعْتُم بِهِۦ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ

This stands as one of the most powerful ayahs in the Quran, presenting a profound lesson on the motivation of the believer. Allah ﷻ frames the relationship between Himself and the believers as a transaction, a sale. But this isn't just any transaction—it's unlike anything the world has ever known.

When the ayah opens with إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱشْتَرَىٰ (Indeed, Allah has purchased), we immediately recognize that buying involves two parties: one who purchases and one who sells. Here, Allah ﷻ is the purchaser. By the end of the ayah, we discover that the believers are celebrating their sale to Him. But what exactly is being bought and sold?

Allah ﷻ has purchased from the believers أَنفُسَهُمْ وَأَمْوَٰلَهُم—their very selves and their wealth. Notice the order here: selves come before wealth. This sequence carries deep significance. While in other passages discussing jihad, we often see wealth mentioned first because building an army requires funds before manpower, this transaction operates on a different plane entirely. This is a spiritual exchange. When someone is ready to surrender their very self, their entire being, what is money in comparison? It becomes merely an extension of that primary sacrifice. First comes the willingness to give up everything you are, and naturally flowing from that comes the willingness to give up everything you have.

The remarkable aspect of this divine transaction becomes clear when we examine what believers receive in return: بِأَنَّ لَهُمُ ٱلْجَنَّةَ—for them is Jannah. Consider the nature of this deal for a moment. If someone offered to buy your laptop for $2,000 but wouldn't pay you until next April, you'd hesitate. Another person offers you only $550, but pays cash immediately—which would you choose? Human nature gravitates toward immediate payment. We want the transaction completed now. Cash, not credit. Present reality, not future promise.

Yet here, Allah ﷻ asks believers to make their payment immediately and in full. Surrender your priorities now. Reorient your entire life now. Sacrifice your youth, your wealth, your very existence now. And in exchange? You'll receive Jannah—but not now. You'll receive it when you're buried, when this life ends, when you transition to the next realm.

If any other entity in existence proposed such terms, you'd reject it instantly. The proposition seems absurd from a worldly perspective. You're being asked to transform all your life priorities, to spend beyond any conceivable limit, to give up everything—and for what? For a place you cannot see, cannot visit, cannot inspect. You're being asked to purchase real estate you cannot tour, based solely on descriptions in revelation, with full payment due immediately and possession granted only after death.

Imagine a disbelieving relative approaches someone who has made this transaction: "You spent everything? What did you get?" "Jannah." "Have you seen it?" "No." "How do you know it exists?" "Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ said so." "That's enough for you?" "Yes, completely." "But this Messenger you trust—he hasn't seen it either, has he?" "Actually, he was given a glimpse of it." "So you trust this man entirely?" "Of course. He is the Messenger of Allah ﷻ." "You must have tremendous trust to give up everything based on that."

And that's precisely the point. This transaction requires an extraordinary level of trust and conviction. Consider what believers sacrifice: youth, when others their age indulge in worldly pleasures; wealth, when they could pursue limitless material gain; career paths that would make them millionaires on Wall Street or investment banking, positions with no ethics but enormous financial rewards. Instead, they choose the halal path, they choose to serve this deen, they choose more modest means. And everyone around them who lacks their vision tells them they've wasted their life, thrown away their potential, limited themselves unnecessarily.

Yet the believer remains firm because they alone can see what others cannot: they're actually the only ones thinking clearly about their future. The real future. The eternal future.

The ayah continues: يُقَـٰتِلُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَيَقْتُلُونَ وَيُقْتَلُونَ—they fight in the path of Allah, and they kill and are killed. Notice the progression. The believers' willingness to sacrifice began with surrendering their very selves. Once that foundational sacrifice is made, fighting becomes a natural extension. The Companions (RA) were always ready to answer the call because fighting means risking your life—but it's not your life anymore. You already sold it. You already completed the transaction. You already received Jannah in exchange. So this self you're putting at risk? It's no longer yours anyway. The ultimate expression of spending your wealth, your youth, your efforts is found in being mentally prepared for death itself.

Then comes the divine guarantee: وَعْدًا عَلَيْهِ حَقًّا فِى ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ وَٱلْإِنجِيلِ وَٱلْقُرْءَانِ—a promise binding upon Him, true in the Torah, the Gospel, and the Quran. This isn't a new arrangement unique to Muslims. This promise of paradise, this transaction where Allah ﷻ purchases the lives and wealth of believers, existed for all previous believing communities. It was there for the followers of Musa (AS), for the followers of Isa (AS), and now for the followers of Muhammad ﷺ.

وَمَنْ أَوْفَىٰ بِعَهْدِهِۦ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ—and who is more faithful to his covenant than Allah? This rhetorical question answers itself. No one. Absolutely no one fulfills promises like Allah ﷻ does. The ayah begins in third person, discussing "whoever fulfills his promise with Allah," maintaining a certain distance. Then suddenly, dramatically, Allah ﷻ shifts to direct address: فَٱسْتَبْشِرُوا۟ بِبَيْعِكُمُ ٱلَّذِى بَايَعْتُم بِهِۦ—so rejoice in your transaction which you have concluded!

This shift from third person to second person is called iltifat in Arabic rhetoric, a drawing near. Allah ﷻ is so pleased with those who make this transaction that He immediately moves closer to them, addressing them directly. "Congratulations! Celebrate the sale you've just completed!" Notice the word بَايَعْتُم—you have mutually transacted. This is a mutual sale, a بَيْع that involves both parties. You sold something to Allah ﷻ, and Allah ﷻ sold something to you. The transaction is complete, binding, and mutual.

وَذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ—and that is the supreme triumph, the ultimate success. Not worldly success, not temporary victory, but success in its truest, most complete, most eternal form.

translation: [Such believers are] the repentant, the worshippers, the praisers [of Allah], the travelers [for His cause], those who bow and prostrate [in prayer], those who enjoin what is right and forbid what is wrong, and those who observe the limits [set by] Allah. And give good tidings to the believers
commentary:
ٱلتَّـٰٓئِبُونَ ٱلْعَـٰبِدُونَ ٱلْحَـٰمِدُونَ ٱلسَّـٰٓئِحُونَ ٱلرَّٰكِعُونَ ٱلسَّـٰجِدُونَ ٱلْـَٔامِرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَٱلنَّاهُونَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَٱلْحَـٰفِظُونَ لِحُدُودِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

After describing this magnificent transaction, the natural question arises: what do the people who've made this sale actually look like? How will they know they're fulfilling their part of the bargain? This ayah provides the answer through a series of profound characteristics.

ٱلتَّـٰٓئِبُونَ—those who constantly repent. Notice this isn't ٱلَّذِينَ تَابُوا (those who repented), a simple past tense. The form ٱلتَّـٰٓئِبُونَ indicates continuous, ongoing action. These are people perpetually engaged in tawbah, constantly returning to Allah ﷻ. This is the foundation: recognizing your shortcomings and continuously turning back.

ٱلْعَـٰبِدُونَ—those who constantly worship. Now that you've recognized your sins and made tawbah, you immerse yourself in acts of worship. You occupy yourself with devotion to Allah ﷻ.

ٱلْحَـٰمِدُونَ—those who constantly praise. Having made tawbah and having been blessed with the ability to worship, you become overwhelmingly grateful. You recognize these as gifts from Allah ﷻ, so you're perpetually in a state of hamd, of praising Him.

ٱلسَّـٰٓئِحُونَ—those who travel, or more specifically, those who fast. The word سَائِحُونَ literally means travelers or wanderers, but it carries the meaning of fasting because travel is itself a form of fasting. When you travel, you're deprived of the comforts and luxuries you normally have access to. Similarly, fasting is voluntary deprivation for the sake of Allah ﷻ. These believers go beyond obligatory worship to embrace additional forms of devotion and self-discipline.

ٱلرَّٰكِعُونَ ٱلسَّـٰجِدُونَ—those who bow and those who prostrate. They engage fully in salah, the physical manifestation of submission to Allah ﷻ.

ٱلْـَٔامِرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَٱلنَّاهُونَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ—those who enjoin what is right and forbid what is wrong. Their devotion isn't self-contained. They actively work to spread good and prevent evil in their communities.

Then comes a shift: وَٱلْحَـٰفِظُونَ لِحُدُودِ ٱللَّهِ—and those who guard the limits of Allah. This is the only quality preceded by the conjunction و (and), separating it from all the previous characteristics. Why this separation? Because all the other qualities should exist under any circumstance throughout the ages. Whether a Muslim lives in our time or in the 1500s or during the Prophet's ﷺ era, these qualities should always be present.

But guarding the hudud of Allah ﷻ—establishing and maintaining the legal boundaries prescribed by Allah ﷻ—this isn't always possible. It requires Islamic authority, governance, the ability to implement divine law. When believers have that capacity, they will guard these limits. When they don't, they cannot. So Allah ﷻ didn't make this a constant requirement but rather a conditional one, applying when circumstances permit.

The ayah concludes: وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ—and give good news to the believers. These qualities aren't burdens but blessings. This lifestyle isn't restrictive but liberating. So give glad tidings to those who embrace it.

translation: It is not for the Prophet and those who have believed to ask forgiveness for the polytheists, even if they were relatives, after it has become clear to them that they are companions of Hellfire
commentary:
مَا كَانَ لِلنَّبِىِّ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَن يَسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ وَلَوْ كَانُوٓا۟ أُو۟لِى قُرْبَىٰ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ أَنَّهُمْ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ

مَا كَانَ لِلنَّبِىِّ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَن يَسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ—it is not fitting for the Prophet ﷺ and those who believe to seek forgiveness for the polytheists. This represents one of the most emotionally difficult teachings in the Quran. The ayah addresses an excruciating reality: what happens when someone you love, someone close to you, dies as a disbeliever?

وَلَوْ كَانُوٓا۟ أُو۟لِى قُرْبَىٰ—even if they were close relatives. Even if these mushrikeen are your parents, your siblings, your children. Blood relation doesn't change the ruling.

مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ أَنَّهُمْ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ—after it has become clear to them that they are companions of the Hellfire. Once someone dies in a state of shirk, once it's absolutely clear they rejected Islam and passed away as disbelievers, you can no longer seek forgiveness for them. This is the boundary, the limit.

The emotional weight of this command is staggering. All your efforts to reach them, all your prayers for their guidance, all your attempts to bring them to Islam—these must happen while they're still alive. Once they die as disbelievers, that door closes. As emotionally traumatic as this reality is, this is where the matter ends.

translation: And the request of forgiveness of Abraham for his father was only because of a promise he had made to him. But when it became apparent to Abraham that his father was an enemy to Allah, he disassociated himself from him. Indeed was Abraham compassionate and patient
commentary:
وَمَا كَانَ ٱسْتِغْفَارُ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ إِلَّا عَن مَّوْعِدَةٍ وَعَدَهَآ إِيَّاهُ فَلَمَّا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُۥٓ أَنَّهُۥ عَدُوٌّ لِّلَّهِ تَبَرَّأَ مِنْهُ ۚ إِنَّ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ لَأَوَّٰهٌ حَلِيمٌ

Allah ﷻ doesn't leave us in this emotionally difficult situation without providing an example, a model to follow. He directs our attention to one of the greatest prophets who faced precisely this trial: Ibrahim (AS).

وَمَا كَانَ ٱسْتِغْفَارُ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ إِلَّا عَن مَّوْعِدَةٍ وَعَدَهَآ إِيَّاهُ—and the request of forgiveness by Ibrahim for his father was only because of a promise he had made to him. When Ibrahim's (AS) father expelled him from their home, kicked him out for his faith, Ibrahim (AS) said: سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكَ رَبِّي—"I will ask my Lord to forgive you" (19:47). This was a promise, and Ibrahim (AS) was a man of his word. So he continued making this dua for his father because he had said he would.

فَلَمَّا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُۥٓ أَنَّهُۥ عَدُوٌّ لِّلَّهِ تَبَرَّأَ مِنْهُ—but when it became clear to him that he was an enemy to Allah, he dissociated himself from him. When the reality became absolutely certain, when there was no longer any doubt that his father had died as an enemy of Allah ﷻ, Ibrahim (AS) practiced barāʾah—complete dissociation. He separated himself even from his own father.

This concept of barāʾah, this willingness to dissociate from those who oppose Allah ﷻ even when they're family, traces back to our father Ibrahim (AS). When the mushrikeen of Makkah told the believers, "You're from our tribe, you owe us loyalty based on tribal bonds," the believers could respond: "We are the children of Ibrahim (AS), and the first one who practiced dissociation from those who rejected Allah ﷻ was our forefather Ibrahim (AS). So even in separating ourselves from you, we're reviving and following the legacy of Ibrahim (AS)."

But this separation wasn't easy for him, and Allah ﷻ makes this clear: إِنَّ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ لَأَوَّٰهٌ حَلِيمٌ—indeed, Ibrahim was one who kept turning back to Allah, and extremely forbearing. The word أَوَّٰهٌ describes someone who constantly returns to Allah ﷻ, who turns back again and again. And حَلِيمٌ means forbearing, compassionate, particularly in the context of family relationships. It describes someone with a tender, sensitive heart.

Ibrahim (AS) wasn't cold or emotionless. He was a family man with a compassionate heart. This wasn't easy for him. He didn't casually say, "Fine, my father's going to Hell." It pained him deeply. But despite his emotional sensitivity, despite his natural compassion, he submitted to Allah's ﷻ command because he was awwāh—he kept turning back to Allah ﷻ, prioritizing divine guidance over his own feelings.

translation: And Allah would not let a people stray after He has guided them until He makes clear to them what they should avoid. Indeed, Allah is Knowing of all things
commentary:
وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيُضِلَّ قَوْمًۢا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدَىٰهُمْ حَتَّىٰ يُبَيِّنَ لَهُم مَّا يَتَّقُونَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ

وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيُضِلَّ قَوْمًۢا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدَىٰهُمْ—and Allah would never lead a people astray after He has guided them. The Companions (RA) were concerned: "We've been making dua for our mushrik relatives all this time. Were we doing something wrong? Were we misguided?" This ayah addresses their concern directly.

حَتَّىٰ يُبَيِّنَ لَهُم مَّا يَتَّقُونَ—until He makes clear to them what they should avoid. Allah ﷻ doesn't misguide people after guiding them. He first clarifies what they should be cautious of, what they should protect themselves from. The Companions (RA) had been making dua for their relatives before this ruling came down. Now that the guidance has arrived, now that Allah ﷻ has made it clear, this practice is forbidden. But before this clarification? They weren't misguided. Allah ﷻ doesn't hold people accountable for what hasn't been made clear to them.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ—indeed, Allah is Knowing of all things. He knows your intentions, your confusion, your emotional struggles. He knows everything about your situation and has perfect wisdom in the timing and manner of His guidance.

translation: Indeed, to Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth; He gives life and causes death. And you have not besides Allah any protector or any helper
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ ۚ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ

This ayah appears strategically placed after the emotionally difficult command about not seeking forgiveness for deceased mushrikeen. إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ—indeed, to Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth. Everything is ultimately His. Every soul, every destiny, every outcome rests in His hands alone.

يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ—He gives life and causes death. He alone controls who lives and who dies, who is guided and who goes astray. This isn't in your hands or anyone else's.

وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ—and you have not besides Allah any protector or helper. When people face difficulties, they naturally turn to family. Family becomes the safety net, the support system. But this ayah reminds believers: your ultimate reliance cannot be on family ties. Yes, you can seek help from family in worldly matters, but understand that your real protector, your true helper, especially in matters of faith and eternity, is Allah ﷻ alone.

This realization is crucial. You cannot save your relatives from Hellfire through your own power. Only Allah ﷻ can guide them, and only He can save them. Your job is to make the effort while they're alive, but the outcome is entirely in His hands. Place your trust where it truly belongs.

translation: Allah has already forgiven the Prophet and the Muhajireen and the Ansar who followed him in the hour of difficulty after the hearts of a party of them had almost inclined [to doubt], and then He forgave them. Indeed, He was to them Kind and Merciful
commentary:
لَّقَد تَّابَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَى ٱلنَّبِىِّ وَٱلْمُهَـٰجِرِينَ وَٱلْأَنصَارِ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوهُ فِى سَاعَةِ ٱلْعُسْرَةِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا كَادَ يَزِيغُ قُلُوبُ فَرِيقٍ مِّنْهُمْ ثُمَّ تَابَ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ بِهِمْ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ

لَّقَد تَّابَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَى ٱلنَّبِىِّ وَٱلْمُهَـٰجِرِينَ وَٱلْأَنصَارِ—Allah has already turned in mercy to the Prophet ﷺ, the Muhajireen, and the Ansar. This refers to the expedition of Tabuk, one of the most challenging military campaigns in early Islamic history.

ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوهُ فِى سَاعَةِ ٱلْعُسْرَةِ—those who followed him in the hour of difficulty. The word سَاعَةِ means "hour," but it refers to an extended period of extreme hardship. Tabuk was undertaken during scorching heat, with scarce resources, at a time when the dates were ripening and people preferred to stay in the comfort of their homes. Yet they answered the call.

مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا كَادَ يَزِيغُ قُلُوبُ فَرِيقٍ مِّنْهُمْ—after the hearts of a party of them had almost inclined to doubt. Even among these righteous Companions (RA), some felt their hearts wavering. The hardship was so intense that doubt began creeping in. The journey was so difficult that some were on the verge of turning back.

ثُمَّ تَابَ عَلَيْهِمْ—then He turned to them in forgiveness. Despite their momentary weakness, despite their hearts nearly wavering, Allah ﷻ accepted them and forgave them because they ultimately persevered and completed the journey.

إِنَّهُۥ بِهِمْ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ—indeed, He is to them Kind and Merciful. Allah ﷻ specifically uses رَءُوفٌ (extremely compassionate) and رَّحِيمٌ (constantly merciful) to describe His relationship with these believers. He understands their human weakness. He knows the limits of their endurance. And He is gentle with them in their struggles.

translation: And [He also forgave] the three who were left behind [and regretted their error] to the point that the earth closed in on them in spite of its vastness and their souls confined them and they were certain that there is no refuge from Allah except in Him. Then He turned to them so they could repent. Indeed, Allah is the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful
commentary:
وَعَلَى ٱلثَّلَـٰثَةِ ٱلَّذِينَ خُلِّفُوا۟ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا ضَاقَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْأَرْضُ بِمَا رَحُبَتْ وَضَاقَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ أَنفُسُهُمْ وَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَن لَّا مَلْجَأَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَّآ إِلَيْهِ ثُمَّ تَابَ عَلَيْهِمْ لِيَتُوبُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

وَعَلَى ٱلثَّلَـٰثَةِ ٱلَّذِينَ خُلِّفُوا۟—and also to the three who were left behind. This refers to three Companions (RA) who didn't join the expedition to Tabuk without valid excuse. They didn't lie about their reasons; they simply admitted they had no legitimate excuse for staying back. As a consequence, the Prophet ﷺ ordered a complete social boycott of these three men. The entire Muslim community stopped speaking to them.

حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا ضَاقَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْأَرْضُ بِمَا رَحُبَتْ—until when the earth closed in on them despite its vastness. Imagine the psychological state described here: you're living in a vast land, but it feels completely constricting. Everywhere you go, people avoid you. No one speaks to you. You're isolated despite being surrounded by your community. The physical vastness of the earth means nothing when you're emotionally and spiritually suffocated.

وَضَاقَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ أَنفُسُهُمْ—and their own souls closed in on them. External isolation is one thing, but internal torment is another. They couldn't live with themselves. Their own conscience was constricting around them. They couldn't escape their guilt even in their own minds.

وَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَن لَّا مَلْجَأَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَّآ إِلَيْهِ—and they were certain that there is no refuge from Allah except in Him. This phrase captures the essence of true tawbah: لَا مَلْجَأَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَّآ إِلَيْهِ—no escape from Allah except to Him. When you've messed up, when you've sinned, when Allah ﷻ is displeased with you, where can you run? 

The only refuge from Allah ﷻ is Allah ﷻ Himself. It's like a child who touches something forbidden and gets in trouble with his mother. He's scared, he's in trouble, Mom is upset—but where does he run? Back to Mom. She's the only safety he knows. Even when she's upset with him, even when he's in trouble, he has nowhere else to go but to her. Similarly, we have a far deeper relationship with Allah ﷻ. When we've messed up, we still have nowhere to go except back to Him.

ثُمَّ تَابَ عَلَيْهِمْ لِيَتُوبُوٓا۟—then He turned to them so that they could repent. Notice the beautiful structure here: Allah ﷻ turned to them in mercy first, which then enabled them to make sincere tawbah. Their repentance was only possible because He first turned to them with His mercy.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ—indeed, it is Allah who is the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful. The word ٱلتَّوَّابُ indicates someone who repeatedly accepts repentance, who keeps coming back no matter how many times you return. And ٱلرَّحِيمُ emphasizes His constant, unfailing mercy.

translation: O you who have believed, fear Allah and be with those who are true
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَكُونُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ—O you who have believed, fear Allah, be conscious of Allah. After narrating the story of those three Companions (RA) who were boycotted and then forgiven, Allah ﷻ immediately addresses all believers with this command.

وَكُونُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ—and be with those who are truthful. Who are these truthful ones? They include those very three Companions (RA) whose tawbah was just accepted in the previous ayah. Their trial is over. Their repentance has been accepted. Allah ﷻ has declared His mercy upon them.

Now Allah ﷻ turns to the entire Muslim community and says: be with them. Be in the company of الصَّـٰدِقِينَ (the truthful ones). Don't think they're evil or disgraced. Don't continue to isolate them. Their tawbah has been accepted, and I consider them among the righteous. I have issued My verdict on them: they are truthful, they are sincere, they are righteous. So be with them.

This ayah serves multiple purposes. It rehabilitates the three Companions (RA) back into society. It teaches the Ummah about the nature of sincere repentance. It emphasizes that being with righteous company—مَعَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ—is itself a commandment. And it demonstrates that Allah's ﷻ mercy and forgiveness, when granted, completely restore a person's status. The past is wiped away. They are now counted among the truthful, the sincere, those with whom every believer should want to be.

translation: It was not [proper] for the people of Madinah and those surrounding them of the bedouins that they remain behind after [the departure of] the Messenger of Allah or that they prefer themselves over his self. That is because they are not afflicted by thirst or fatigue or hunger in the cause of Allah, nor do they tread on any ground that enrages the disbelievers, nor do they inflict upon an enemy any infliction but that is registered for them as a righteous deed. Indeed, Allah does not allow to be lost the reward of the doers of good
commentary:
مَا كَانَ لِأَهْلِ ٱلْمَدِينَةِ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ أَن يَتَخَلَّفُوا۟ عَن رَّسُولِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا يَرْغَبُوا۟ بِأَنفُسِهِمْ عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ لَا يُصِيبُهُمْ ظَمَأٌ وَلَا نَصَبٌ وَلَا مَخْمَصَةٌ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا يَطَـُٔونَ مَوْطِئًا يَغِيظُ ٱلْكُفَّارَ وَلَا يَنَالُونَ مِنْ عَدُوٍّ نَّيْلًا إِلَّا كُتِبَ لَهُم بِهِۦ عَمَلٌ صَـٰلِحٌ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

It was not becoming for the people of Medina nor those surrounding them from the Bedouins that they should stay behind the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. The phrase وَلَا يَرْغَبُوا۟ بِأَنفُسِهِمْ عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ tells us they should not give priority to their own selves over him. When they chose to remain behind during the expedition to Tabuk, they were motivated by one reason alone: to avoid the hardships of the journey.

Allah ﷻ then explains the profound reward system He has established for those who do accompany His Messenger ﷺ in such trials. The ayah states ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ لَا يُصِيبُهُمْ ظَمَأٌ وَلَا نَصَبٌ وَلَا مَخْمَصَةٌ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ (that neither thirst, nor exhaustion, nor starvation afflicts them in the path of Allah). Every moment of discomfort they experience is recorded as a good deed.

The level of detail in Allah's recording is remarkable. The ayah continues: وَلَا يَطَـُٔونَ مَوْطِئًا يَغِيظُ ٱلْكُفَّارَ (they do not take a single step that angers the disbelievers). Not just the major battles, but every single step that enrages the enemy is written down for them. وَلَا يَنَالُونَ مِنْ عَدُوٍّ نَّيْلًا (nor do they approach any enemy) - every contact with the enemy, every moment of that engagement, all of it is documented as righteous deeds.

This recording system should transform how we view our efforts in Allah's path. When you travel to attend a class, a conference, or to engage in humanitarian work, understand that angels are recording your journey. Every tree you pass, every road sign, every moment of that travel is being written down. When you stop for gas and that meter ticks up with each dollar spent, know that a parallel recording is happening in the heavens. Every small expense, every effort is being documented.

The ayah concludes with the powerful statement: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (Indeed, Allah does not waste the reward of those who excel). Allah ﷻ appreciates and honors the efforts of the believers. He doesn't just count them - He elevates them.

translation: Nor do they spend an expenditure, small or large, or cross a valley but that it is registered for them that Allah may reward them for the best of what they were doing
commentary:
وَلَا يُنفِقُونَ نَفَقَةً صَغِيرَةً وَلَا كَبِيرَةً وَلَا يَقْطَعُونَ وَادِيًا إِلَّا كُتِبَ لَهُمْ لِيَجْزِيَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ أَحْسَنَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

This ayah continues describing the meticulous recording of the believers' efforts. وَلَا يُنفِقُونَ نَفَقَةً صَغِيرَةً وَلَا كَبِيرَةً (They do not spend any expenditure, small or large). Whether it's a small charity or a substantial one, both are recorded with equal care. وَلَا يَقْطَعُونَ وَادِيًا (nor do they cross a valley) - literally, they haven't crossed a single valley إِلَّا كُتِبَ لَهُمْ (except that it is written for them).

Then comes one of the most beautiful divine promises: لِيَجْزِيَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ أَحْسَنَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (so that Allah may reward them with the best of what they used to do). This phrase reveals something extraordinary about Allah's generosity. We perform many deeds - some we execute really well, others less so. If we were to grade our own good deeds, some might rate a 30, some a 40, some a 50, perhaps a few 90s, 95s, or even 100s. Most would be at the lower end, with only a few peaks of excellence.

Allah ﷻ takes this entire record and elevates all of our deeds to the level of our best performance. He doesn't average them out. He raises everything to match our highest achievements. This should make us reflect seriously: what's the best deed on my record? What's truly excellent in my transcript of actions? If we can't identify strong peaks in our record, then we need to urgently add them, because those peaks will determine how everything else is elevated.

translation: And it is not for the believers to go forth [to battle] all at once. For there should separate from every division of them a group [remaining] to obtain understanding in the religion and warn their people when they return to them that they might be cautious
commentary:
۞ وَمَا كَانَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ لِيَنفِرُوا۟ كَآفَّةً ۚ فَلَوْلَا نَفَرَ مِن كُلِّ فِرْقَةٍ مِّنْهُمْ طَآئِفَةٌ لِّيَتَفَقَّهُوا۟ فِى ٱلدِّينِ وَلِيُنذِرُوا۟ قَوْمَهُمْ إِذَا رَجَعُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَحْذَرُونَ

After discussing military mobilization, Allah ﷻ establishes a crucial principle for the preservation and spread of religious knowledge. The ayah begins: وَمَا كَانَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ لِيَنفِرُوا۟ كَآفَّةً (It is not appropriate for the believers to all march out together). Not everyone can leave for military campaigns. Communities need people to remain, particularly for the purpose of learning and teaching.

The solution is presented: فَلَوْلَا نَفَرَ مِن كُلِّ فِرْقَةٍ مِّنْهُمْ طَآئِفَةٌ (Then why should not a group from every faction go forth). From every tribe, every region, every community, at least one representative group should come out - not for war, but لِّيَتَفَقَّهُوا۟ فِى ٱلدِّينِ (to gain deep understanding in the religion).

This word تَفَقُّه is significant. It doesn't just mean learning; it means gaining profound, penetrating understanding of the religion. Every city, every community should send people to gain this depth of knowledge. But notice what comes next: وَلِيُنذِرُوا۟ قَوْمَهُمْ إِذَا رَجَعُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِمْ (so they can return and warn their people).

This is remarkable. Allah ﷻ says they should get deep understanding, but He doesn't say they should return to "teach" their people - He says they should return to "warn" them. This reveals a profound educational strategy. The people back home don't need depth yet. They need the basics first. They need to be awakened, reminded, and warned. You start with warning, with the fundamentals, with making people conscious of their responsibilities.

If this was the strategy for the first generation of Muslims, our approach should be no different. Communities across the world face a shortage of knowledgeable people and an even greater shortage of those who can effectively warn and remind. The solution remains the same: small groups from every community should go forth to study, gain deep understanding, and return as assets to revive their communities.

The ayah concludes: لَعَلَّهُمْ يَحْذَرُونَ (so that they may be cautious). Regular reminder and warning stabilize the hearts of believers. Without this constant spiritual vigilance, communities drift. This is why the Bedouins struggled - they weren't receiving regular reminders. Consistent warning and teaching help people live careful, conscious lives as Muslims.

translation: O you who have believed, fight those adjacent to you of the disbelievers and let them find in you harshness. And know that Allah is with the righteous
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ قَـٰتِلُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ يَلُونَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْكُفَّارِ وَلْيَجِدُوا۟ فِيكُمْ غِلْظَةً ۚ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ

This ayah marks a shift in military policy. يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ قَـٰتِلُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ يَلُونَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْكُفَّارِ (O you who believe, fight those who are near you from among the disbelievers).

The word يَلُونَكُم is particularly instructive. يَلِي means to come into contact with something softly - like a gentle bump or an encounter. The instruction is to fight those who even casually encounter you from the disbelievers. If you're traveling in a caravan and you happen to run into them, there's no more diplomatic courtesy. The period of treaties and tolerance has ended.

This was a dramatic policy change. Previously, there was restraint and strategic calculation in military engagement. But now, after the conquest of Makkah and the spread of Islam's message, the command becomes immediate: if you encounter the mushrikeen of Arabia, engage them then and there. No negotiations, no delay.

The reason is stated: وَلْيَجِدُوا۟ فِيكُمْ غِلْظَةً (and let them find harshness in you). The word غِلْظَة means toughness, firmness, sternness. When the disbelievers see the Muslims, they should find an unyielding strength. This is the opposite of the earlier policy of restraint and measured response. The previous approach of gradual engagement is over. Now it's time to cleanse Arabia of shirk.

The four-month grace period announced at the beginning of this surah has expired. Those who continued to resist after that deadline would face immediate consequences. This new policy applies once the sacred months have passed and the treaties have been fulfilled.

The ayah concludes with a crucial reminder: وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (And know that Allah is with those who maintain taqwa). Even in executing this tough military stance, consciousness of Allah ﷻ must be maintained. The strength comes not from brutality or vengeance, but from righteousness and adherence to divine command.

translation: And whenever a surah is revealed, there are among the hypocrites those who say, "Which of you has this increased faith?" As for those who believed, it has increased them in faith, while they are rejoicing
commentary:
وَإِذَا مَآ أُنزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ فَمِنْهُم مَّن يَقُولُ أَيُّكُمْ زَادَتْهُ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ إِيمَـٰنًا ۚ فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ فَزَادَتْهُمْ إِيمَـٰنًا وَهُمْ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ

The surah now shifts to expose the reactions of different groups to divine revelation. وَإِذَا مَآ أُنزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ (And whenever a surah is revealed), فَمِنْهُم مَّن يَقُولُ أَيُّكُمْ زَادَتْهُ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ إِيمَـٰنًا (some of them say, "Which of you has this increased in faith?").

This is dripping with sarcasm. These hypocrites would mock the revelation, saying things like, "Wow, that was amazing, wasn't it?" or "Did that really increase your iman?" Their tone reveals their contempt for the Quran. They're making fun of something the believers hold sacred.

Allah ﷻ then contrasts the two reactions. فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ فَزَادَتْهُمْ إِيمَـٰنًا (As for those who believe, it increases them in faith). For the believers, revelation has a powerful, tangible effect. Each new surah genuinely strengthens their iman. وَهُمْ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ (and they rejoice). They don't just accept it passively - they're overjoyed. They congratulate each other. They celebrate the arrival of new guidance.

This ayah establishes an important principle: iman increases and decreases. The believers experience growth in faith through the Quran, while the hypocrites experience the opposite, as we'll see in the next ayah.

translation: But as for those in whose hearts is disease, it has [only] increased them in evil [in addition] to their evil. And they will have died while they are disbelievers
commentary:
وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ فَزَادَتْهُمْ رِجْسًا إِلَىٰ رِجْسِهِمْ وَمَاتُوا۟ وَهُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ

In stark contrast to the believers: وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ (And as for those in whose hearts is disease), فَزَادَتْهُمْ رِجْسًا إِلَىٰ رِجْسِهِمْ (it increases them in filth upon their filth).

The word رِجْس means filth, impurity, corruption. Their hearts were already contaminated with hypocrisy, doubt, and disbelief. When new revelation comes down and they mock it, deny it, or react with sarcasm, that mockery doesn't leave them neutral - it adds more corruption to what's already there. Their spiritual disease compounds.

The progression is devastating: the Quran comes down, they reject it, and layer upon layer of spiritual filth accumulates in their hearts. Each rejection makes the next one easier. Each act of mockery hardens their hearts further.

The ayah concludes with their ultimate fate: وَمَاتُوا۟ وَهُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ (and they die while they are disbelievers). Their trajectory leads to dying in a state of kufr. This is the natural endpoint of rejecting guidance - persistent rejection until death seals them in that state.

translation: Do they not see that they are tried every year once or twice but then they do not repent nor do they remember
commentary:
أَوَلَا يَرَوْنَ أَنَّهُمْ يُفْتَنُونَ فِى كُلِّ عَامٍ مَّرَّةً أَوْ مَرَّتَيْنِ ثُمَّ لَا يَتُوبُونَ وَلَا هُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ

Allah ﷻ poses a rhetorical question to these hypocrites: أَوَلَا يَرَوْنَ أَنَّهُمْ يُفْتَنُونَ فِى كُلِّ عَامٍ مَّرَّةً أَوْ مَرَّتَيْنِ (Do they not see that they are tried once or twice every year?).

During the final years of the Prophet's life ﷺ, major events were occurring with remarkable frequency - at least once or twice annually. From military expeditions to the revelation of harsh ayahs exposing hypocrisy, these were tests. Each event was an opportunity for the hypocrites to recognize their condition, feel shame, and repent.

But the pattern repeats: ثُمَّ لَا يَتُوبُونَ (yet they do not repent). Despite being tested repeatedly, despite seeing their duplicity exposed again and again, they don't make tawbah. وَلَا هُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ (nor do they remember). They don't even make an effort to remember, to reflect, to take heed.

The tragedy here is the missed opportunity. Allah ﷻ gives them test after test, chance after chance, but they persist in their heedlessness. They neither turn back to Him nor do they engage in any form of remembrance or reflection that might awaken their hearts.

translation: And whenever a surah is revealed, they look at each other, [saying], "Does anyone see you?" and then they dismiss themselves. Allah has dismissed their hearts because they are a people who do not understand
commentary:
وَإِذَا مَآ أُنزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ نَّظَرَ بَعْضُهُمْ إِلَىٰ بَعْضٍ هَلْ يَرَىٰكُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ ثُمَّ ٱنصَرَفُوا۟ ۚ صَرَفَ ٱللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُم بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَفْقَهُونَ

This ayah captures a moment of guilty behavior: وَإِذَا مَآ أُنزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ (And whenever a surah is revealed), نَّظَرَ بَعْضُهُمْ إِلَىٰ بَعْضٍ (they look at one another). Picture the scene: a new revelation comes down, particularly one that's harsh against the hypocrites, and they're sitting in the gathering. They start exchanging furtive glances.

هَلْ يَرَىٰكُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ (Does anyone see you?) - they check if anyone is watching them. Then ثُمَّ ٱنصَرَفُوا۟ (then they slip away). The word انصرف suggests turning away, departing, slipping out. They want to leave without being noticed.

The imagery is powerful: they're thinking, "This surah is probably about us. This is going to be embarrassing. We better leave before it gets worse." So they give each other that knowing look - the look criminals give each other when they need to coordinate an escape. One signals, "Can you see anyone watching?" The other subtly gestures, "You go first, then I'll slip out."

They think they're being clever, sneaking away unnoticed. But Allah ﷻ responds: صَرَفَ ٱللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُم (Allah has turned their hearts away). They want to turn their hearts away from the guidance, so Allah ﷻ facilitates that turning away as a form of punishment. بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَفْقَهُونَ (because they are a people who do not understand).

The phrase لَّا يَفْقَهُونَ means they don't understand, but more accurately, they don't want to understand. They've made a choice not to comprehend.

This carries a universal lesson that extends beyond those original hypocrites. There are people in every Muslim community who suffer from a twisted logic: "I want to learn the religion, but the more I learn, the more responsible I become. If I attend this program, I'll probably learn things I'm not currently doing. Then Allah ﷻ will hold me accountable for knowing and not acting. So I'm probably safer not knowing. That way I'm protected from that responsibility."

This reasoning is fundamentally flawed and represents a greater crime. When you deliberately avoid learning because you fear responsibility, you're inviting Allah ﷻ to turn your heart away from the deen altogether. That's far worse than learning and struggling to implement. The attitude of "I'd rather not know" leads to Allah ﷻ sealing your heart from wanting to understand at all.

translation: There has certainly come to you a Messenger from among yourselves. Grievous to him is what you suffer; [he is] concerned over you and to the believers is kind and merciful
commentary:
لَقَدْ جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ مِّنْ أَنفُسِكُمْ عَزِيزٌ عَلَيْهِ مَا عَنِتُّمْ حَرِيصٌ عَلَيْكُم بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ

The surah reaches its conclusion with one of the most beautiful descriptions of the Prophet ﷺ in the entire Quran. Consider the journey of Surah At-Tawbah: it began without بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم, without invoking Allah's mercy. It opened with harsh ultimatums and severe warnings. But now, as it closes, mercy floods back in - not through mentioning Allah's mercy directly, but through describing the merciful nature of His Messenger ﷺ.

لَقَدْ جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ مِّنْ أَنفُسِكُمْ (There has certainly come to you a Messenger from among yourselves). He's not foreign to you. He's one of you - he speaks your language, understands your culture, shares your lineage. The phrase مِّنْ أَنفُسِكُمْ emphasizes this intimate connection.

عَزِيزٌ عَلَيْهِ مَا عَنِتُّمْ (Grievous to him is what you suffer). The word عَزِيز means difficult, hard, unbearable. Everything that causes you hardship is deeply distressing to him ﷺ. Your pain is his pain. Your difficulty weighs heavily on his heart.

A powerful example of this is found in the well-known hadith about the number of daily prayers. When fifty prayers were initially prescribed, the Prophet ﷺ negotiated with Allah ﷻ, going back repeatedly to request a reduction. Who told him to do this? Musa (AS), who understood that people struggle with excessive obligations. But even after the reduction to five, the Prophet ﷺ knew his ummah would find difficulty. He understood their capacity and their challenges. When something is too hard for us, it's genuinely hard on him ﷺ.

This sensitivity is particularly remarkable in the context of Surah At-Tawbah. When Allah ﷻ told him, "Even if you seek forgiveness for them seventy times, I will not forgive them," that wasn't easy for the Prophet ﷺ to hear. His natural inclination was to want to ask even more than seventy times. When the lamest excuse-makers would come to him seeking permission to stay behind from battle, what did he do? He gave them permission - to the point where Allah ﷻ had to correct him: عَفَا اللَّهُ عَنكَ (May Allah pardon you) - why did you give them permission?

Notice that Allah ﷻ begins with forgiveness before even mentioning the mistake. Normally, you point out the error first, then offer forgiveness. But with His beloved Messenger ﷺ, Allah ﷻ leads with عَفَا اللَّهُ عَنكَ (Allah has pardoned you), essentially saying, "Don't worry, you're not in trouble, you're already forgiven" - and only then asks, "Why did you give them permission?" This is the special love Allah ﷻ has for His Messenger ﷺ.

The ayah continues: حَرِيصٌ عَلَيْكُم (Concerned over you). The word حَرِيص denotes someone who is zealous, intensely eager, even greedy for something. The Quran uses this word elsewhere to describe how the Jews were greedy to preserve their own lives: وَلَتَجِدَنَّهُمْ أَحْرَصَ النَّاسِ عَلَىٰ حَيَاةٍ (And you will surely find them the most greedy of people for life) (2:96). Here, that same intensity of desire is directed by the Prophet ﷺ toward our welfare. He's zealous for any good he can secure for you. Any opportunity to benefit you, he seizes it. This is someone who cries at night saying "أُمَّتِي أُمَّتِي" (My ummah, my ummah), weeping out of concern for his community.

Then comes a grammatical observation that reveals something beautiful: بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (to the believers, he is kind and merciful). Notice that رَءُوفٌ and رَّحِيمٌ are mentioned without the definite article "al" when describing the Prophet ﷺ, while earlier in the surah, Allah ﷻ withheld His mercy. But the Prophet's mercy toward the believers is unrestricted, open-ended - indicated by the indefinite form.

رَءُوفٌ is particularly significant. This word describes someone who is deeply compassionate, someone who can see things from your perspective. This is one of the most challenging qualities to develop in life - the ability to truly see from another person's point of view. A husband struggles to see from his wife's perspective. A father can't quite grasp his son's viewpoint. A mother doesn't fully understand her daughter's experience. Even when we try, even when we intellectually recognize someone else's situation, we often can't feel their pain.

This is why people in trouble often say, "I can't talk to my parents - they don't understand what I'm going through." People seek out others who've experienced similar struggles because those people can truly relate. But here's what makes the Prophet ﷺ unique: what trouble has he not experienced? What pain can he not identify with?

There's a powerful incident where a woman who had lost her child erupted at the Prophet ﷺ when he counseled her to have patience. She cried out, "What do you know about this?" In her grief, she forgot that he ﷺ had lost children too. He had lost babies - children who had started walking and talking, who had captured his heart completely. We all know how it feels to look at a two-year-old picture of our now-older children and feel that tender ache of remembrance. He ﷺ lived with those memories. He had lost more than one child, and they held a permanent place in his heart. He would hear their names and weep.
