When Allah ﷻ restored his son to him in the famous hadith, السَّلَامُ عَلَيْكُمْ دَارَ قَوْمٍ مُؤْمِنِينَ (Peace be upon you, O inhabitants of the believers' abode), the Prophet's voice would break.

So when he ﷺ tells someone to have patience, it's not theoretical advice from someone reading ayahs about sabr. It comes from someone who has lived through that pain. He's offering counsel from a very real, very personal place. This is what رَءُوفٌ means - deeply understanding and compassionate toward the believers. And he is رَّحِيمٌ - always merciful to them.

The surah that began with severity, with the threat of divine punishment, with ultimatums to the hypocrites and mushrikeen, concludes by reminding us that we have been given a messenger who embodies mercy. The harsh message of Surah At-Tawbah was itself an act of mercy - delivered through a messenger ﷺ whose entire being is characterized by compassion for his ummah.

translation: But if they turn away, [O Muhammad], say, "Sufficient for me is Allah; there is no deity except Him. On Him I have relied, and He is the Lord of the Great Throne
commentary:
فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَقُلْ حَسْبِىَ ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ ۖ وَهُوَ رَبُّ ٱلْعَرْشِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ

This powerful ayah brings Surah At-Tawbah full circle, ending where it began. The surah opened by addressing those who turned away from Allah's ﷻ message and His Messenger ﷺ, and it concludes with the same theme. This final instruction to the Prophet ﷺ carries profound weight—these are not his own words, but the words of Allah ﷻ that he is commanded to declare.

The previous ayah described the Messenger's ﷺ deep care and compassion for the believers, how close he is to them and how concerned he is for their well-being. Yet immediately after emphasizing this closeness, Allah ﷻ commands: *فإن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَقُلْ* (but if they turn away, then say). Even you, O Messenger, who love them so much—if they turn away from this message, you must tell them: *حَسْبِىَ ٱللَّهُ* (Allah ﷻ is sufficient for me). 

The Messenger ﷺ couldn't bring himself to say these words on his own. Allah ﷻ had to command him to say them. If the people turn away, he must declare: Allah ﷻ is enough for me, and I have nothing to do with those who reject this message.

## The Declaration of Tawheed and Trust

*حَسْبِىَ ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ* (Allah ﷻ is sufficient for me—there is no deity worthy of worship and obedience except Him). This statement encapsulates complete reliance on Allah ﷻ alone. The phrase *لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ* isn't merely about worship in ritual—it encompasses obedience in every aspect of life. No one has the right to be worshiped and obeyed in any manner except Him.

The declaration continues: *'عَلَیۡهِ تَوَكَّلۡتُ* (upon Him I have placed my trust). The Messenger ﷺ is essentially declaring independence from his own people if they choose to turn away. Notice the progression in the surah—it began with a declaration of innocence from the *مشركين* (those who commit shirk), and now concludes with a declaration concerning those who abandon the Messenger's mission. These are the ones who stayed behind, who turned away from supporting the Prophet ﷺ in his struggle.

## The Ultimate Authority

*وَهُوَ رَبُّ ٱلۡعَرۡشِ ٱلۡعَظِیمِ* (And He is the Master of the Tremendous Throne). In Arabia, the throne represented the ultimate seat of power and authority. For the Arabs, the Ka'bah was that throne—the prize, the sacred center of their world. The announcement of *براءة* (disavowal) was made at that very throne, at the Ka'bah. But Allah ﷻ declares that while this may be the center on earth, He is the Master of the ultimate throne, the Throne that transcends all earthly authority.

After the Ka'bah had been cleansed and purified, after this sacred house of Allah ﷻ was restored to its proper purpose, the only destination remaining for the Messenger ﷺ was the highest place—*الرفيق الأعلى* (the Highest Companion). In his final moments, as he lay among those who had stood by him through every trial, the Prophet ﷺ looked at his companions and spoke of the highest companionship, referring to the Master of that tremendous throne.

## The Enduring Mission

This surah can be summarized with one central theme: loyalty to the mission of Allah's Messenger ﷺ. The Muslim Ummah must feel a profound sense of loyalty to the mission he carried. That mission didn't end with his passing—it continues, and we bear the responsibility of carrying it forward.

Consider the example of the Sahaba (RA). When the Prophet ﷺ passed away, he had already prepared a battalion to confront the Persians. Did the companions cancel that mission after his death? No. They understood that while the Messenger may no longer be physically present, his mission remains alive. They maintained the chain of command he had established because their loyalty was to that mission.

On the hardest day for the Ummah—the day of the Prophet's passing—Abu Bakr (RA) stood before a community in shock and delivered words that captured this reality: "Whoever used to worship Muhammad, then Muhammad is dead. Whoever worships Allah ﷻ, then Allah ﷻ is living and does not die." Allah's Word doesn't die, and because His Word is eternal, the mission of the Prophet ﷺ never dies. This Word—the Quran—keeps the mission alive, the mission that was entrusted to the Messenger ﷺ *لِّتَكُونُوا۟ شُهَدَاۤءَ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ* (so that you may be a witness over all people).

## The Foundation for Reform

The Prophet's ﷺ greatest worldly accomplishment was the transformation of society. Understanding how he brought about this reform requires deep study, but we can identify its essential framework. The guidance revealed to him addressed two levels: reforming the individual and reforming society.

At the individual level, Islamic law applies immediately upon accepting Islam. The moment someone becomes Muslim, the obligations of Salah, Zakah, halal and haram—all of these apply right away. This is quantifiable law. Alongside this, Islam cultivates something that cannot be quantified in the same way: character and morality. Qualities like humility, kindness, honesty, maintaining family ties—these are enhanced and beautified over time. The bare minimum for an individual is adherence to Islamic law, while morality represents continuous development.

When it comes to societal change, the approach differs. There are two aspects of *أمر بالمعروف و نهي عن المنكر* (commanding good and forbidding evil): there is Makkan *أمر بالمعروف*, and there is Madinan *أمر بالمعروف*. The Madinan form includes legal frameworks—laws of inheritance, divorce, governance, and so forth. In Makkah, these laws weren't present. The focus was purely on faith and morality.

## The Sequence of Transformation

This reveals a critical principle: while law applies to individuals immediately upon embracing Islam, the message to society must first be a moral message. It begins with faith, then establishes morality at the societal level—values that don't require someone to be Muslim to recognize as good. Concepts like honesty, not burying daughters alive, fairness in business dealings—these are *معروف*, universally recognized as good.

Once faith-driven morality takes hold in society, then that society becomes ready to implement Islamic law. Law is the culmination, not the starting point. It works when built upon a foundation of faith and morality that already exists at the social level.

## When the Foundation is Missing

What happens when Islamic law is imposed without this foundation? Only two outcomes are possible. 

First, there will be a revolt against those laws. Society will reject them, and once this happens, it becomes nearly impossible to reintroduce them. The people will have such an aversion that the door closes indefinitely. Consider Prohibition in America—alcohol was banned, but because the moral foundation opposing it wasn't there, people brewed more alcohol during those years than ever before. It backfired completely.

Second, the law becomes hollow and can be manipulated. Without morality, law itself doesn't guarantee justice. Imagine four witnesses who are considered reliable under Islamic law falsely testifying against someone. Was the law executed? Yes. Was justice served? No. What would embolden someone to give false testimony? The absence of morality.

Law is like justice depicted in classical imagery—blindfolded, requiring the guidance of morality and faith to serve its true purpose. Without this foundation, law can create injustice rather than prevent it. This was the fundamental problem with Bani Israel—they often abided by the letter of the law while missing its spirit, lacking the foundational morality the Quran demanded from them.

## Individual versus Society

Some raise the objection: Are you suggesting that because revelation came in stages, we should also implement things gradually, perhaps allowing what's prohibited until society is ready? This confuses the individual with society. For Muslims, Islamic law applies immediately at the individual level. Alcohol is haram now—this isn't something that develops over time for the individual believer.

But societally, you cannot impose it where it has no meaning. The reality is that Islamic law doesn't apply to non-Muslims. You cannot force a society to accept prohibitions they don't believe in. The Prophet's ﷺ first contribution was creating a moral revolution among people, and for those people, implementing Islamic law became the easy part. The difficult work was building faith and morality—that was the true struggle.

Morals don't require force; you call people to them without fighting. Laws, however, require authority, and authority cannot be established without struggle. This is why Musa (AS) was commanded to fight, and why the Prophet ﷺ was eventually commanded to fight—to gain the authority necessary to implement law. But that's a much later stage, not the starting point.

## The Contemporary Challenge

If we examine Muslims anywhere in the world today, what appears to be the fundamental problem? Some places implement aspects of Islamic law—they collect Zakah, prohibit alcohol publicly, and claim to champion Islamic lifestyle. Yet these same places often exhibit the worst forms of racism, financial corruption, and rigid class hierarchies. The law may be present, but justice is absent because the foundation of morality is missing.

Our call to humanity must be first and foremost a call to morality. While some portray Islam as merely concerned with punishments and restrictions, two-thirds of the Quran is dedicated to building faith and morality. That's not a small portion—it's the major focus. This represents the foundation of the prophetic strategy for reform. It cannot be overlooked or bypassed.

The instructions to fight in Allah's ﷻ path came only after this foundation was established in Madinah. Before this foundation existed, the command was to restrain your hands. The intense instructions regarding fighting in Surah At-Tawbah represent the completion of divine retribution against those who refused to believe when the Prophet ﷺ was in their midst—similar to the flood of Nuh (AS), a divine judgment against obstinate rejection.

The loyalty Allah ﷻ demands from us is loyalty to this complete mission: building faith, establishing morality, and when the conditions are right, implementing law with justice. This is the legacy of the Messenger ﷺ, and this ayah reminds us that our sufficiency lies with Allah ﷻ alone, the Master of the Tremendous Throne, as we strive to carry this mission forward.
